Author full names: Qiqi Lu, Yuen Hung Wong
Title: A BIM-based approach to automate the design and coordination process of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems
Abstract: The design and spatial coordination of mechanical, electrical, plumbing, and fire protection systems (MEP) have become a major challenge for successful project delivery. Traditionally, MEP coordination has been conducted based on overlaying 2D drawings from different trades to detect functional and spatial interferences between MEP systems. Such coordination is time consuming and of low efficiency. Recently, the advent and development of Building Information Modelling (BIM) have significantly changed this process by affording a visual and collaborative manner. However, it still needs much manual work and many rounds of coordination as the conventional 2D design was still used. Therefore, this paper proposes a well-defined approach based on BIM to improve the workflow and automate the process by developing an MEP rule-based automated engine using Revit Application Programming Interface (API) tool. The engine is developed primarily to help analyse available ceiling zone for MEP elements in the first phase and then extended to facilitate rule-based routing in the second phase. The proposed approach is verified and validated by an illustrative example, followed by a real-life MEP design case. The result shows that with the help of the proposed approach, clashes and the number of coordination iteration are reduced substantially.
Author Keywords: Automation; BIM; MEP coordination; MEP deign; Rule-based routing
Funding Details: Hip Hing Construction Co Ltd
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination broadly, with electrical systems treated as one of several trades (alongside air conditioning, fire service, plumbing, drainage) rather than as the central focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops a BIM-based automated rule-based engine using Revit API to automate MEP design and coordination.
relevance_notes: Electrical is included as part of MEP coordination but not the primary focus; the rule-based BIM tool development is directly relevant to digital technology in construction.
research_problem: Conventional MEP coordination relies on overlaying 2D drawings to detect spatial and functional interferences, which is time-consuming, error-prone, and requires many iterations. Even with BIM adoption, coordination still depends on 2D-based design and lacks common routing rules agreed before each trade develops its own drawings, leading to excessive clashes and rework. Manual ceiling-zone analyses room-by-room are tedious and inefficient, and there is no automated, rule-based mechanism to streamline the MEP design and coordination workflow.
research_objective: To propose a BIM-based approach that automates MEP design and coordination by developing a rule-based engine using the Revit API for ceiling space analysis and rule-based routing.
specific_methods: Revit API add-in development; Automated ceiling space demarcation map generation; Rule-based routing engine design; Illustrative example validation; Case study application
data_size: One illustrative sample project (7 rooms) and one seven-storey clubhouse case study (290,388 ft² / 26,978 m²)
future_research_directions: Complete Phase 2 development of rule-based routing engine; Eliminate 2D design process to enable a true BIM workflow; Integrate user-defined MEP routing rules into BIM authoring tool; Extend automated engine functionalities to streamline coordination
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Automation of manual task; Schedule compression
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery
key_findings: The Autoroute add-in's ceiling space demarcation map function reduced ceiling-zone analysis time from approximately one day (manual) to about nine minutes for a 60-room floor. By identifying congested ceiling zones early, the approach enabled all trades to agree on common routing rules before producing initial shop drawings, substantially reducing clashes and coordination iterations on the seven-storey clubhouse case study. Phase 2 (rule-based routing) was not yet implemented but is intended to fully automate coordination by eliminating 2D design steps.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer; Project manager
quantified_benefit: Ceiling space analysis for a 60-room floor reduced from ~1 day (manual) to ~9 minutes using the automated engine
research_sponsor: Hip Hing Construction Co Ltd
industry_involvement: The research was conducted and funded by Hip Hing Construction Co Ltd; the company's BIM team and building services team developed and tested the tool, and it was applied on a real seven-storey clubhouse project involving main contractor and sub-contractors.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that due to time limitations, only Phase 1 (ceiling space demarcation map generation) of the engine has been developed and validated, while Phase 2 (rule-based routing) remains under development. They note that the use of BIM and the demarcation map alone cannot save overall design and coordination time because the 2D design process is still involved, and full benefits require eliminating the 2D step.

Author full names: Ping Yung, Jun Wang, Xiangyu Wang, Ming Jin
Title: A BIM-enabled MEP coordination process for use in China
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) coordination is an important part of constructability review process. The rapid development of Building Information Modelling (BIM) could substantially facilitate this process by affording a visual and collaborative manner. However, BIM adoption in China has been hindered by the low management commitment and knowledge level. There is a need to develop a model to utilize BIM in China taking into consideration this current situation. This paper reports how design institutes without 3D modelling capabilities can work with modellers to perform MEP coordination with BIM. A BIM-enabled MEP coordination process for use in China is documented and further developed as one of the primary findings. IDEF0 language is used to illustrate the process in detail. It is found in the case study analysis that the use of BIM may not save overall design time as traditional 2D design was still used. However, it can reduce the costs of manual MEP coordination, the expected number of change orders, chance that significant number of change orders may occur, as well as MEP coordination related change orders as a percentage of total change orders.
Author Keywords: Building information modelling; China; Constructability; IDEF0; MEP coordination
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination as a unified activity including electrical (high voltage, low voltage) work as one of several trades, with specific electrical design team data extracted in the case study.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates a BIM-enabled process for MEP coordination, with BIM as the core digital technology.
relevance_notes: Paper is about BIM (not AI) for MEP coordination; electrical is one of four trades examined in the case study but not the primary focus.
research_problem: BIM adoption in China has been hindered by low management commitment and knowledge levels, and few local design institutes have 3D modelling capabilities. Previous literature on BIM focused on modelling technologies and example applications but gave little attention to integrating BIM-supported knowledge communication into ongoing construction working processes. There is a need for a documented MEP coordination process that fits Chinese design practice, where a single design institute performs architectural, structural, and MEP detail design, and where 2D design remains the norm.
research_objective: To document and develop a BIM-enabled MEP coordination process model, using IDEF0 language, suitable for use in China given the lack of local 3D modelling capabilities.
specific_methods: IDEF0 process modelling; Case study; Questionnaire with design team leaders; Cost-benefit analysis; Expert estimation of counterfactual scenarios
data_type: Project records; Cost records; Survey responses; BIM model
data_size: One high-rise building project (167,895 m2, 202 m tall, two towers plus 3-storey basement) with responses from 4 MEP design team leaders
future_research_directions: Remove the 2D design step as BIM becomes more widely adopted; Investigate whether MEP coordination within a single firm is more efficient than among multiple firms; Compare change order levels in BIM vs non-BIM projects with larger samples; Evaluate the proposed process on additional cases to verify its merits
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Cost reduction; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; RFI or change order management; Organizational adoption of technology
electrical_work_category: MEP coordination generally; Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance); Low voltage systems (fire alarm, security, access control)
key_findings: The BIM-enabled MEP coordination process did not shorten overall design time because 2D design was still used, but it saved manual MEP coordination labor estimated at RMB 456,000 (about 0.11% of total MEP cost). Manual coordination without BIM was estimated to consume 20-50% of total design time per trade. Using BIM was projected to reduce both the expected and maximum number of change orders, and lower MEP-coordination-related change orders as a percentage of total change orders. Total modelling cost of RMB 1.7 million was substantially less than potential savings from avoided change orders (estimated up to RMB 20 million).
contractor_role_impacted: Designer or engineer; BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Owner or client
quantified_benefit: Total 3D modelling cost was 0.17% of total construction cost (RMB 1.7M); saved manual MEP coordination cost RMB 456,000 (0.11% of MEP cost); potential avoided change order cost up to ~RMB 20 million (5% of MEP construction cost)
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: One of the authors is a BIM manager at Sichuan Southwest Project Management & Consultancy Co. Ltd., the project management firm on the case study project; four MEP design team leaders from the design institute provided data via questionnaire.
industry_references__references_details: Cites the US Construction Industry Institute (CII) definition of constructability (CII 1987) and references the Singapore Buildable Design Appraisal System.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that the study is based on only one case and that scenarios (particularly counterfactual estimates of change orders without BIM) were estimated based on personal experience, which could be biased. They note that care should be taken when interpreting results and suggest larger sample comparisons as future work.
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported

Author full names: Simge Girgin, Renate Fruchter, Martin Fischer
Title: A case study towards assessing the impact of mixed reality-based inspection and resolution of MEP issues during construction
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Despite advances in 3D clash detection during preconstruction, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) installations are still prone to the detection of unforeseen clashes during construction. These issues must be resolved as quickly as possible to prevent significant schedule delays. Through interviews and field observations, this case study investigates the impact of mixed reality (MR) on the inspection and resolution of field-detected MEP issues from product, organization, and process (POP) perspectives. For the product impact, preliminary findings from the field interviews show that MR-based inspection would increase the quality of MEP installation by identifying errors easily and resolving them faster. For the organizational impact, we modeled and compared the current (as-is) and MR-integrated (to-be) MEP field issue resolution workflows using Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) and determined that MR-based inspections can decrease the coordination overhead between MEP engineers and superintendents by up to 75%. This translates into at least a 50% faster resolution of an MEP issue for the process impact. The paper contributes to the practice of MR-based field inspection by providing a method to quantify potential time savings by integrating MR into the MEP field issue resolution workflow and field interview questions for MEP engineers and superintendents to further examine the use of MR during inspection activities in construction projects. Our observations of MEP superintendents and engineers during field inspection showed that not all building information visualized in MR is useful for their inspection tasks. We developed a classification for building information usefulness to help construction project managers who are deploying MR determine useful information for the task at hand that needs to be integrated into the 3D MR model for MR-based inspections.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modeling (BIM); Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN); construction; information usefulness; inspection; MEP; Mixed Reality (MR)
Funding Details: Chung Ang University in Seoul; Stanford University, SU, (2021-03); Stanford University, SU; Center for Integrated Facility Engineering, Stanford University, CIFE, SU
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses on MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) coordination and inspection collectively, with electrical components (receptacles, panelboards, conduit, cable) explicitly included as one of the three MEP systems tested in the field study.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally evaluates Mixed Reality (Microsoft HoloLens 2 Trimble XR10 with Spectar BIM software) integrated with BIM for field inspection, which is a core digital technology in the review scope.
relevance_notes: Strong on MR/BIM digital technology with explicit electrical work content (panelboards, receptacles, conduit, cable types), though framed as MEP rather than electrical-specific.
research_problem: Despite advances in 3D BIM clash detection during preconstruction, MEP installations still encounter unforeseen field clashes during construction that must be resolved quickly to avoid schedule delays. Superintendents typically lack BIM software expertise and require project engineers to assist them in navigating 3D models, increasing coordination overhead and delaying issue resolution. Prior MR studies in construction have largely been conducted in experimental settings rather than real projects, leaving construction managers without quantified guidance on whether and how to deploy MR for field inspection.
research_objective: To assess the product, organization, and process impact of MR-based inspection and resolution of field-detected MEP issues during construction through a real-project case study.
specific_methods: Semi-structured interviews; Field observations; Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) workflow modeling; As-is vs to-be workflow comparison; Qualitative line-by-line coding in NVivo 12; Project document analysis
data_type: Interview transcripts; Video; BIM model; Project records
data_size: One 454,000 sq ft life science construction project; interviews with 1 VDC engineer, 4 MEP project engineers, and 2 superintendents; 3 field tests; 48 building information data points reviewed (31 analyzed)
future_research_directions: Observe MR-based field inspection over the construction phase in real time; Validate the building information usefulness classification through multiple case studies; Empirically measure impact rather than analytical estimation; Develop dynamic MR-BIM functionalities to add/hide data by phase, user, and task; Examine MR effectiveness when designers are collocated vs geographically dispersed
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Schedule compression; Quality improvement
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Quality control; RFI or change order management
electrical_work_category: Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance); Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Data and communications (structured cabling); MEP coordination generally
key_findings: MR-based inspection was found to potentially increase MEP installation quality by enabling easier identification and faster resolution of errors. Workflow comparison estimated that MR can decrease coordination overhead between MEP engineers and superintendents by approximately 75% in low-complexity scenarios (no RFI), 50% with RFI, and 65% when an RFI plus designer field visit is required, translating to at least a 50% reduction in shortest total issue resolution duration (from 2-3 days down to 1 day). Of 48 building information data points reviewed in MR, 32% were useful, 7% neutral, 16% not useful, and 45% missing, indicating that effective MR deployment requires upfront integration of task-relevant building information into 3D models.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Project manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Up to 75% reduction in coordination overhead between MEP engineers and superintendents and at least 50% reduction in shortest total MEP field issue resolution duration
research_sponsor: Center for Integrated Facility Engineering (CIFE) at Stanford University (CIFE Seed 2021-03)
industry_involvement: DPR Construction granted access to a live 454,000 sq ft life science construction project; their VDC engineer, four MEP project engineers, and two superintendents participated in interviews and field tests, and Spectar (MR-based BIM software) engineers collaborated to prepare tailored 3D MR models.
industry_references__references_details: Cites buildingSMART (2007) Quick Guide to Business Process Modeling Notation (BPMN) as the basis for workflow modeling notation.
limitations_acknowledged: The case study was limited because the construction project was already in the commissioning phase, preventing real-time observation of MEP installation and inspection. Consequently, the as-is vs to-be workflow comparison was based on workflow data collected from interviews with the VDC engineer, so findings reflect analytical rather than empirical impact on coordination overhead and total resolution duration. The installation quality impact was based only on qualitative interviews with superintendents and project engineers.
theoretical_framework__framework_name: Product, Organization, Process (POP) Framework (Kunz and Fischer, CIFE, Stanford)

Author full names: Eduardo Hideki Tanaka, Lucimara de Almeida, Guilherme Sabino de Freitas Gouveia, Rafael Pedrosa Silva Clerici, Alex Henrique Fernandes Alves, Roberta Riciélly de Oliveira
Title: A collaborative, immersive, virtual reality environment for training electricians
Abstract: This paper presents the development of a multi-user, collaborative virtual reality environment for training electricians to work on the electric power distribution network. Typically, traditional trainings in this field have lots of constraints and don't allow learners to experience a wide set of different equipment that is installed in the network nor bad weather conditions and the different risk analysis that should be performed. With the proposed virtual reality environment, learners will be able to practice several realistic conditions without risking themselves.
Author Keywords: Electric power distribution system; Simulator; training; virtual reality
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses on training electricians who maintain electric power distribution networks (utility lineworkers), which is adjacent to but not identical to building-side electrical construction; however, the trade, safety practices (PPE/CPE), and tasks overlap meaningfully with electrical contractor concerns.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates a multi-user immersive virtual reality (VR) training environment with 3D modeling, motion controllers, and a web-based management system.
relevance_notes: The electricians trained are utility distribution-network electricians (COPEL power company), not Division 26 building electrical contractors; reviewer should decide if utility lineworker training is in-scope for the electrical construction review.
research_problem: Traditional training for electric power distribution network electricians is constrained by limited physical training centers, restricted access to the wide variety of network equipment from different manufacturers, and the inability to safely expose trainees to real-world conditions such as adverse weather, traffic, animals, and energized lines. Existing electrical simulators focus on substations or 2D diagrams and do not address distribution network components, do not support multi-user collaboration, and do not allow electricians to experience common field challenges. There is therefore a gap for a realistic, collaborative VR training tool tailored to distribution network electricians.
research_objective: To develop and evaluate a multi-user, collaborative, immersive virtual reality environment for training electricians to perform realistic distribution-network tasks safely.
specific_methods: Unstructured interviews; Online questionnaire with 7-point Likert scale; Persona development; Scrum agile development; Usability user testing; Nielsen heuristic evaluation; Observational study of training sessions
data_size: 93 questionnaire respondents (76 electricians); 21 usability test volunteers; 3 usability experts for heuristic evaluation; 122 3D models created
future_research_directions: Add new exercises with additional distribution equipment such as fuse savers; Create new scenarios for underground distribution networks; Investigate gamification elements (rankings, badges) to boost engagement; Conduct additional usability studies of the LPP-based penalty system; Conduct additional usability studies of the WMS reports
value_proposition: Training effectiveness; Safety improvement; Workforce wellbeing
pain_point_addressed: Skilled labor shortage or training; Safety or electrocution or arc flash
key_findings: The team developed a multi-user VR environment supporting tree pruning, tree cutting, and fuse switch operation exercises in urban and countryside scenarios with variable weather and time-of-day conditions, integrated with a web-based management system for instructors. Usability testing led to adopting both continuous (with reduced field of view) and teleportation locomotion; chainsaw gestures performed equally well sitting or standing but caused more motion sickness when standing, while hot-stick fuse-switch interactions produced the most user errors and fatigue. Heuristic evaluation of the virtual tablet found 19 usability issues (9 major, 7 minor, 3 cosmetic), of which 7 had been fixed at the time of writing. Survey results showed electricians and instructors expect future trainings to be more practical, collective, and collaborative than current ones.
contractor_role_impacted: Electrician or field worker; Field supervisor or foreman; Safety manager
quantified_benefit: 19 usability issues identified (9 major, 7 minor, 3 cosmetic) with 7 already fixed; 80.6% of 93 surveyed workers were over 30 years old
research_sponsor: ANEEL (Brazilian National Electric Energy Agency) R&D program, project PD-2866-0510/2019, with COPEL (Companhia Paranaense de Energia) as utility partner
industry_involvement: COPEL electricians, instructors, safety specialists, and engineers participated through unstructured interviews, brainstorming sessions to define exercises, periodic (every 4 months) review of 3D models, questionnaire responses, and user testing. The COPEL Life Protection Program (LPP) form was directly adopted as the basis for the simulator's penalty scoring system.
codes_addressed__codes_details: COPEL internal Life Protection Program (LPP) form and its penalty scoring system, plus COPEL Occupational Health and Safety Management standards, were used to define safety-fault triggers and severity scores in the simulator (e.g., -21 points for not using safety belt and lanyard).
industry_references__references_details: Cites ANEEL (Brazilian National Electric Energy Agency) as funding regulator and COPEL (Companhia Paranaense de Energia) utility internal standards including the Life Protection Program (LPP) and Occupational Health and Safety Management procedures.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors note that hot-stick interactions for opening and closing fuse switches were difficult and error-prone because they required precise gestures at significant virtual distance from the user, and that some users experienced motion sickness during the elevator-bucket vertical movement and during continuous locomotion. They also acknowledge that the COVID-19 pandemic forced a shift from user testing to heuristic evaluation for some usability assessments.

Author full names: Omar Alruwaythi, Paul Goodrum
Title: A difference in perspective: Impact of different formats of engineering information and spatial cognition on craft-worker eye-gaze patterns
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Individuals vary widely in their ability to translate complex spatial information into performing physical tasks. Much of the variability can be explained by their experience in using different information formats and differences in individual spatial cognition. Traditional delivery of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) designs rely on two-dimensional isometric drawings. Advancements in three-dimensional (3D) computer-aided design (CAD), augmented reality, virtual reality, and 3D printing have provided new format options for delivering engineering information; however, providing them to crafts for use at the construction workface remains relatively rare. The objective of this research is to understand how eye-gaze patterns of construction craft workers is influenced by information formats and spatial cognition when building a complex spatial task. A series of field trials with MEP workers was conducted to examine the influence of information format and spatial cognition on their eye-gaze patterns in building a scale-model pipe assembly. Participants were provided eye-tracking glasses along with one of three information formats: Two-dimensional (2D) isometric drawings, 2D isometric drawings supplemented with a 3D image of the assembly, and 2D isometric drawings supplemented with a 3D physical model of the assembly. Card rotation and cube comparison tests were administered to measure spatial cognition. The results of this paper reveal that the information format and spatial cognition significantly influenced workers' eye-gaze patterns; there are different gaze patterns for different information formats, and these differences in gaze patterns were associated with differences in spatial cognition abilities. Additionally, the improvement in performance when using different engineering information formats is associated with different eye-gaze patterns.
Author Keywords: Engineering information delivery; Eye tracking; Labor productivity; Spatial cognition
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The study recruits MEP (mechanical, electrical, and plumbing) craft workers and explicitly targets the MEP trades because isometric drawings are their primary information format, though participants were predominantly pipefitters.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper evaluates digital information delivery formats (3D CAD images, 3D printed models) and uses eye-tracking technology with mobile eye-tracking glasses, but does not employ AI or machine learning.
relevance_notes: Although MEP-framed, the actual participants appear to be primarily pipefitters and the assembly task is piping; electrical-specific applicability is by extension rather than direct demonstration.
research_problem: Traditional MEP design delivery relies on 2D isometric drawings, which are difficult for craft workers to interpret at the workface. Although prior research established correlations between information format, spatial cognition, and craft performance, the causal mechanism—how individuals with differing spatial cognition actually interface with different information formats—remained unexplained. Without understanding this causation, providing alternative information formats risks unintended performance tradeoffs across user groups.
research_objective: To understand how eye-gaze patterns of construction craft workers are influenced by information formats and spatial cognition when building a complex spatial task.
specific_methods: Controlled field trial experiment; Eye-tracking (semantic gaze mapping); Card rotation test; Cube comparison test; ANOVA; Heat map and gaze plot analysis; Video-based work sampling at 30-second intervals
data_size: 60 MEP craft workers (20 per information format condition)
future_research_directions: Define areas of interest by spatial complexity within 2D and 3D images; Investigate scan path sequencing across information formats; Examine how individuals use 3D information by design complexity level; Evaluate emerging 3D visualization technologies including tablets, AR, and VR; Test findings in dynamic real construction jobsite environments; Integrate plan reading and task training into craft worker training modules
value_proposition: Labor productivity; Quality improvement; Training effectiveness
pain_point_addressed: Labor productivity; Skilled labor shortage or training; Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: Information format and spatial cognition significantly influenced eye-gaze patterns, with workers using only 2D isometrics showing longer fixation times, more fixations, and lower performance than those given 2D plus 3D images or 3D physical models. Participants with lower spatial cognition performed significantly worse than higher spatial cognition peers when using 2D-only drawings, but performed equivalently when supplemented with 3D information. Eye-tracking revealed the causal mechanism: low spatial cognition individuals had statistically significantly more fixations, fixation time, and revisits on the 3D information specifically, indicating they relied more heavily on 3D content to compensate.
contractor_role_impacted: Field supervisor or foreman; Electrician or field worker; BIM coordinator or VDC manager
quantified_benefit: Average total assembly time reduced from 38:54 (2D iso) to 26:49 (2D+3D image) and 27:36 (2D+3D physical); direct work rose from 48.3% to 70.0%; errors dropped from 1.20 to 0.30/0.25
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Participants were recruited from industrial jobsites and local pipefitter union chapters in the Colorado Front Range, providing access to MEP craft workers for the field trials.
limitations_acknowledged: The model assembly used 12.7 mm PVC pipe at 1:12 scale, much smaller than typical industrial pipe, and focused only on measuring and assembling tasks. The study examined only two factors (information format and spatial cognition), whereas real construction sites involve many additional dynamic factors. The research did not analyze how individuals use 3D information differently by design complexity or examine scan path sequencing in detail, and trials were conducted in controlled office settings rather than active jobsites.
data_availability__availability_details: Data generated or analyzed during the study are available from the corresponding author by request.
software_platform: SMI BeGaze 3.4; SMI ETG 2 Wireless Analysis Pro

Author full names: Martin Michaud, Eva-Charlotte Forgues, Vincent Carignan, Daniel Forgues, Claudiane Ouellet-Plamondon
Title: A lean approach to optimize BIM information flow using value stream mapping
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Building Information Modelling (BIM) was introduced in the Architecture, Engineering and Construction (AEC) industry as a shared information platform that aims to improve productivity through better collaboration. The assumption is that a virtual integration of information among project stakeholders would reduce the issues around the fragmented nature of the processes that still prevail in the construction field. This paper aims to highlight the sources of waste in the information flows between an architecture firm, a Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) engineering firm, a general contractor (GC) and a MEP subcontractor (SC) in a BIM project - an aspect of waste little covered in the Lean literature. The focus is on the MEP process from early design to the final product. This research contributes to the identification of the main barriers to information flow, including the conflicts and waste sources that emerge from using BIM, as well as to the identification of emerging successes. Moreover, the findings offer practical implications by providing a visual of the patterns emerging from the use of BIM. Finally, by providing potential waste reduction strategies such as Value Stream Mapping (VSM) this work allows construction actors to identify and reduce sources of waste in their processes.
Author Keywords: Auto-confrontation; BIM; Collaboration; Information flows; Waste
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses on the MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) process in a BIM project, with electrical work included as part of the MEP subcontractor's scope alongside mechanical and plumbing.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally engages with BIM as a digital technology, examining BIM information flows, BIM Execution Plans, and BIM coordination platforms (Aconex, BIM 360 Glue, Revit).
relevance_notes: Electrical is treated as part of MEP rather than as a standalone focus; specific electrical work issues (e.g., electricity installation team absent from meetings) are mentioned but the paper's lens is MEP-wide.
research_problem: Despite BIM being introduced to improve collaboration and information sharing in AEC projects, significant waste persists in information flows between project stakeholders, an aspect little covered in the Lean literature. There is a lack of qualitative research identifying sources of waste in BIM information processing during the design phase, particularly for the complex MEP coordination process. Existing studies focus on workflow and material flow waste but neglect information flow waste and the conflicts that emerge from using BIM.
research_objective: To identify conflicting and successful areas in BIM information flows between MEP stakeholders using an empirical approach through collaborative mapping of stakeholders' individual perceptions.
specific_methods: Auto-confrontation technique; Semi-structured interviews; Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) mapping; Value Stream Mapping (VSM); Comparative process map analysis
data_type: Interview transcripts; BIM model; Project records
data_size: One CAD $265.7M PPP jail replacement project; two 1.5-hour interviews each with four stakeholders (architect PM, MEP engineering PM, MEP contractor, MEP BIM coordinator)
future_research_directions: Develop or adapt VSM tools to better identify information flow waste; Test proposed waste reduction strategies in future projects; Generate future state maps to remove identified sources of waste; Expand study to multiple projects to enable generalization; Account for varying BIM maturity levels among stakeholders
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Automation of manual task; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: Four sources of waste in BIM information flows were identified: overproduction of information, rework, transfer of information problems, and difficulties understanding how technology affects practices. These wastes stem from causes including the persistence of document-centric push approaches, semantic gaps, decentralization between design and construction, specialty lenses, interoperability gaps, lack of common vision, and organizational issues. Success factors observed included process standardization via a BIM Execution Plan and long-term relationships among stakeholders, which improved coordination over time and across subsequent projects.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Designer or engineer; Field supervisor or foreman
quantified_benefit: Not reported
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Four industry stakeholders from a real PPP project (architect, MEP engineer, MEP contractor, and general contractor's MEP BIM coordinator) participated in interviews and collaborative process mapping.
limitations_acknowledged: Only one project was studied, limiting the data available to develop and validate solutions. Generalization is difficult because all construction projects differ, and the BIM maturity level varied among stakeholders, particularly in the construction phase, which may have accentuated the observed issues.
theoretical_framework__framework_name: Lean Construction / Transformation-Flow-Value (TFV) theory and Value Stream Mapping
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported
software_platform: Revit; AutoCAD; Tekla; Aconex; BIM 360 Glue; BIM 360 Field

Author full names: Hongzhe Yue, Qian Wang, Yang Su, Hai Fang, Jack C. P. Cheng, Mingyu Zhang
Title: A point cloud dataset and deep learning method for semantic segmentation of underground garages
Abstract: Reconstructing as-built building information modeling from point clouds in underground garages holds great potential for enhancing facility management. However, this task is particularly challenging due to the complex and diverse architectural and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) components present in such environments. Moreover, there is no publicly available dataset and no dedicated semantic segmentation algorithm for underground garages environments. To address this gap, this paper first builds an expert-labeled underground garage dataset, which includes 10 categories of components. Subsequently, this paper introduces GarageNet, a deep learning (DL) network designed for semantic segmentation of point clouds for scenes including both building and MEP components. GarageNet integrates a residual squeeze-and-excitation module to enhance layer and channel connection and a multi-head attention feature aggregation module to capture local geometric structures. The results indicate that the GarageNet achieved a high overall accuracy of 95.09% and a mean intersection over union of 87.15%, surpassing other DL algorithms such as PointNext. Ablation studies and cross-validation further demonstrate the model's reliability and generalization capability.

Additionally, no dedicated semantic segmentation algorithm exists for the coexisting large architectural and small irregular MEP components found in such environments, which hinders as-built BIM reconstruction for facility management.

Author full names: Gangwen Yan, Yinghui Yang, Huizhong Zhang, Zhenwei Li, Song Chen, Xuefeng Zhao, Zhe Sun, Xiongtao Fan, Meng Zhang, Lingli Huang, Liang Liu
Title: A review of life cycle construction process and cutting-edge technology in prefabricated MEP installation engineering
Abstract: Prefabricated installation, a pivotal study in the realm of contemporary construction practices, delves into the utilization of prefabrication within mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems. Despite its ascending prominence, the domain grapples with ambiguities in application pathways, uncertain developmental trajectories, and the absence of a holistic technical paradigm. This research endeavors to bridge these gaps by conducting a thorough and multidimensional investigation into the current landscape of prefabricated MEP installation initiatives. This study meticulously dissects the paradigm from five critical vantage points: historical evolution, standards and regulations, life cycle analysis, technological applications, and corporate implementation strategies. At present, there is still a lack of standards and specifications specifically for the field of assembled MEP installation. The analysis reveals a trend towards intelligent and sustainable installation practices in prefabricated MEP projects. The research predominantly focuses on the design, production, and installation stages. Notably, building information modeling (BIM) emerges as the most prominent technology, followed by the Internet of Things (IoT) and 3D laser scanning, with extended reality (XR) technologies gaining traction. Large, state-owned construction firms are spearheading innovative applications in this realm. In summary, this paper provides an overview and outlook for the development direction and the application of cutting-edge technologies in prefabricated MEP installation projects, with the aim of supporting the industry's advancement.

Author full names: Ruijun Deng, Xiaoliang Li, Yuhua Tian
Title: A review: The application of path optimization algorithms in building mechanical, electrical, and plumbing pipe design
Abstract: This review systematically integrates recent advancements in path optimization algorithms for the automated layout of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems within complex building environments. A hybrid optimization framework is introduced, combining the global search capability of Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) with the local refinement efficiency of the A* algorithm, enhanced by dynamic weight adjustment and context-aware mechanisms. Simulation experiments based on a hospital BIM model demonstrate that the proposed approach improves design efficiency by approximately 25–35% and reduces conflict incidence by around 40%. The framework further incorporates Building Information Modeling (BIM) and real-time clash detection enabled by IoT devices to enable scalable, multi-objective optimization in high-density spatial configurations. The potential of generative artificial intelligence—such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and diffusion models—is also explored for generating initial pipeline layouts and enhancing spatial adaptability. To support low-carbon building initiatives, the framework is adaptable to LEED-compliant sustainable MEP design practices. Despite notable progress, challenges remain in algorithmic scalability, dynamic constraint modeling, and multi-objective trade-offs. This review identifies research gaps in diameter-aware layout optimization and BIM-driven multi-scale generative modeling, and outlines future directions toward intelligent, high-performance MEP system design in future sustainable buildings.
Author Keywords: A* algorithm; BIM-integrated design; MEP system optimization; multi-algorithm collaborative optimization; path optimization algorithms

Author full names: Yichao Sun, Qingxi Zhao, Zhexiao Zhu, Youyi Bi, Jiangshan Liu
Title: A safety training system for electrical engineering construction based on virtual reality and social interactive learning mechanism
Source title: Journal of Control, Automation and Electrical Systems
Abstract: As electrical construction expands and electric systems grow more complex, non-compliance and operational errors can lead to serious consequences. Therefore, systematic safety education and training are key approaches to improving electrical personnel's safety knowledge and skills, as well as reducing the incidence of human-related accidents. However, in the practice of safety education and training within electrical enterprises, there are still issues such as insufficient precision in training needs analysis, relatively weak training resource development, traditional training methods, and insufficient depth in training evaluation and feedback. To address these issues, this paper establishes an electric power construction safety training system architecture from the perspective of skill operations and safety protection, considering the physical characteristics of the electrical grid. A electrical safety training system based on virtual reality technology and social learning mechanisms has been designed. The system integrates visualization interaction technology, Unity3D technology, and real-world physical operation scenarios with virtual simulation entities on the Android platform, enabling functions such as virtual training, realistic training, and online remote training. The design and development of this training system are of great significance for improving the skill levels and safety operation levels of electrical workers, thereby assisting electrical construction enterprises in achieving safe and stable production.
Author Keywords: Electrical engineering construction; Interactive learning; Safety training system; Social learning; Virtual reality
Funding Details: National Natural Science Foundation of China, NNSFC, (52575300)

Author full names: A.S. Nazmul Huda, Soib Taib, Mohd Shawal Jadin, Dahaman Ishak
Title: A semi-automatic approach for thermographic inspection of electrical installations within buildings
Abstract: Maintaining the reliability of electrical installation has become part of the energy efficiency practices in building. The degradation of electrical installations can cause overheating, which can lead to subsequent failure of the equipments that can potentially result in unplanned power outages, possible injury and fire hazard. In addition, the efficiency of an electrical system becomes low prior to failure, thus energy is spent generating heat and causing unnecessary energy loses. Therefore, early prevention is required to avoid this situation by monitoring the reliability of the electrical installations through energy audit practices. This article proposes a semi-automatic approach for evaluating the thermal condition of electrical installations within the building in Malaysia by analyzing its infrared image. Initially the interest regions of the images are manually segmented. Then the statistical features of first order histogram and gray level co-occurrence matrix features as well as the differences of feature parameters between hot and reference regions are extracted from segmented regions. Principle component analysis is applied for the best features selection and at the final stage, the condition of electrical equipments will be classified using multilayered perceptron neural network. The performances of multilayered perceptron networks have been compared and tested with various training algorithms. The classification accuracy of multilayered perceptron networks are also compared with discriminant analysis classifier and it is found that the multilayered perceptron network using Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm gives the best testing performance. The result shows that the maximum testing accuracy 78.5% was obtained.
Author Keywords: Building; Conditions classification; Electrical installation; Infrared image; Multilayered perceptron; Statistical features
Funding Details: Universiti Sains Malaysia

Author full names: Chu-Hsuan Lee, Meng-Han Tsai, Shih-Chung Kang
Title: A visual tool for accessibility study of pipeline maintenance during design
Abstract: Background: Pipeline maintenance is becoming an important issue in modern construction and building information model (BIM) research. An understanding of pipeline accessibility considerations in terms of operation and maintenance is essential for planning and management. Previous studies have highlighted the complexity of multi-pipes including mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) pipelines and the importance of information visualization, but few have proposed a way to consider accessibility problems during operation and maintenance. Methods: Therefore, this study develops a systematic method to evaluate accessibility with respect to pipeline maintenance. We first divided pipeline accessibility into three categories: (1) visual accessibility—the visibility for an inspector to view; (2) approachable accessibility—the difficulty for an inspector to approach; and (3) operational accessibility—the pipeline that can be operated by the inspectors. We created mathematical models and discussed the ergonomic details about each category. We then developed a user interface, VAO Checker, in which V, A and O stand for visual, approachable and operational respectively, to display visual information about pipeline accessibility. Through instantaneous analysis, the system visualizes the accessibility of the pipelines. We visually represent the intersection and union of these three categories to illustrate the varying accessibility of pipe elements. Results: A usability test was conducted to validate the system's effectiveness. The results of the usability analysis show that users have higher correctness when using VAO Checker than 2D plan drawing and 3D model, and they evaluate the performance of this tool better than 2D plan drawing. Conclusion: Pipeline designers can benefit by using this tool to sketch a suitable traffic flow for engineers to investigate. Furthermore, the substantial amount of information saved in the layout database could be referenced for future optimization.
Author Keywords: and Plumbing (MEP); Building information model (BIM); Electrical; Information Visualization; Mechanical; Pipeline accessibility; Pipeline maintenance
Funding Details: Taiwan's Ministry of Economic Affairs, (101-EC-17-A-15-S1-223)

Previous pipe-routing algorithms focus on physical and economic constraints (collision avoidance, length, turns) but largely ignore operational and maintenance accessibility constraints. There is no systematic, visual method for designers to evaluate whether pipelines and their valves, meters, and equipment can be seen, approached, and operated by maintenance engineers.

key_findings: The VAO Checker tool achieved a user success rate of 64.3% in identifying accessibility problems, compared to 56.4% for 3D models and 40.1% for 2D plan drawings, with the difference between 2D and VAO being statistically significant (p=0.000). NASA-TLX performance scores showed VAO Checker (48.0) outperformed 2D plan drawing (36.0) but slightly underperformed 3D models (53.8), partly due to first-person view disorientation. The tool successfully visualized three accessibility categories (visual, approachable, operational) and was deemed useful by participants for designing complex pipeline environments such as chiller machinery rooms.

quantified_benefit: VAO Checker achieved 64.3% problem-identification success rate, 1.6 times higher than 2D plan drawing (40.1%) and 1.14 times higher than 3D model (56.4%)

Author full names: Hamdi Bashir, Sadeque Hamdan, Udechukwu Ojiako, Salah Haridy, Mohammad Shamsuzzaman, Hassan Ahmed Al Zarooni
Title: A weighted fuzzy social network analysis-based approach for modeling and analyzing relationships among risk factors affecting project delays
Abstract: In the last decade, it has been increasingly recognized that efforts to address project delays without understanding the relationships among risk factors (RFs) could be futile. In light of the limitations of previous research that has addressed this issue, this study seeks to contribute to the literature by developing a novel weighted fuzzy social network analysis (SNA)-based approach, which accounts for the likelihood of occurrence and the impact of RFs on one another. To validate its practicality, the approach is applied to a demonstrative study in which the causes of delays in real-world electrical installation projects were modeled and analyzed. In addition to providing a holistic view of relationships among RFs, the proposed approach enables engineering managers to identify the root causes of delays and their corresponding critical propagation paths. Compared to other approaches in the literature, the value of the approach lies in its simplicity and utility for supporting engineering managers in developing effective risk-mitigation plans to minimize the severity of project delays.
Author Keywords: Decision making & risk management; modeling; network; program & project management; project delays; risk factors
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The demonstrative case study explicitly applies the proposed approach to modeling and analyzing risk factors causing delays in real-world electrical installation projects carried out by a major utility company.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper applies fuzzy set theory and social network analysis (computational decision-support techniques) implemented via NetMiner software, but does not use AI/ML in a learning sense or other digital construction technologies like BIM.
relevance_notes: Electrical installation is the specific application domain, making this directly relevant to electrical contractors despite the methodological focus being on fuzzy SNA rather than mainstream AI.
research_problem: Project delays are a persistent problem across industries, but most prior research ranks risk factors (RFs) as if they were independent, ignoring domino effects among them. Recent studies addressing RF interrelationships have limitations: SEM requires large samples and distributional assumptions, ISM-MICMAC removes transitivity hiding propagation paths, and existing fuzzy approaches account for either likelihood or impact but not both. There is a gap for an approach that captures vagueness in RF relationships while modeling both likelihood of occurrence and impact, and that identifies critical risk propagation paths.
research_objective: To develop a weighted fuzzy social network analysis-based approach for modeling and analyzing relationships among risk factors affecting project delays, accounting for both likelihood of occurrence and impact.
specific_methods: Social network analysis; Fuzzy set theory; Triangular fuzzy numbers; Expert panel/brainstorming; Best non-fuzzy performance defuzzification; In-degree centrality; Betweenness centrality; Path analysis
data_size: 13 risk factors and expert judgments from 5 project managers
future_research_directions: Use additional SNA metrics beyond in-degree and betweenness centrality; Reduce the number of pairwise comparisons required from experts; Model dynamic and time-related behavior of risk factors; Test the approach in more real-world studies across different contexts
value_proposition: Decision support; Schedule compression
pain_point_addressed: Scheduling
electrical_work_category: Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance); Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices)
key_findings: The approach was demonstrated on electrical installation projects with an average 40% delay. Three root cause RFs were identified: poor schedule management by contractor, lack of owner experience, and poor qualifications of contractor's technical staff, initiating 24 propagation paths. The most critical path (TWP=1.93) was 8->2->6->4->9->10->11->14, and conflict between owners and contractors (RF6) had the highest betweenness centrality (~0.13), making it a critical mediator despite not being a root cause.
contractor_role_impacted: Project manager; Business owner or executive
quantified_benefit: Identified critical propagation path with total weighted impact of 1.93; electrical installation projects had an average delay of 40%
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: An expert panel of five project managers from a major utility company that carries out electrical installation projects provided risk factor identification and pairwise judgments on likelihood and impact relationships.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that only two SNA metrics (in-degree and betweenness centrality) were used to prioritize RFs. Experts must perform a large number of pairwise comparisons, which may challenge implementation. The approach does not model dynamic or time-related behavior of RFs, and was demonstrated using only one real-world study within a single company.

Author full names: Mengtian Yin, Weilun Duan, Zhuoqian Wu, Mudan Wang, Llewellyn Tang, Ray CC Cheung, Ioannis Brilakis
Title: Adaptive detection of equipment components in MEP construction drawings via graph-enhanced siamese vision
Abstract: Building Information Models (BIMs) of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems are critical for facility operation and maintenance. Previous studies have explored the automatic MEP BIM reconstruction from construction drawings, yet detecting equipment components (ECs) remains challenging due to heterogeneous drawing styles and enterprise-specific conventions. To address the challenge, this paper proposes a novel self-adaptive solution for automatic EC detection in real-world MEP drawings using graph-enhanced self-supervised computer vision models. Both query objects (i.e., EC samples) and drawings are transformed into graph-based representations, with multiple features extracted. A graph-based retrieval process with Principal Component Analysis filtering is then employed to localize regions of interest, after which ECs are identified via a hybrid similarity measure that integrates graph distance with a self-supervised Siamese network. The method is evaluated on a total of 291 EC instances across 18 object types from real-world MEP drawings. For the 13 object types (192 instances) included in training, it achieved a precision of 88.6%, recall of 89.5%, and F1 score of 0.89. For the 5 unseen object types (99 instances), the method maintained strong performance with a precision of 98.9%, recall of 90.9%, and F1 score of 0.95, showing that the proposed approach generalizes well to new EC categories and diverse drawing conventions. The research contributes to MEP BIM theory by formalizing an adaptive detection process for automatically reconstructing equipment objects from engineering drawings, reducing manual modeling time and providing reliable digital models for facility management.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Computer-aided design (CAD); Construction drawing recognition; Facility operation and maintenance; Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) systems
Funding Details: Horizon 2020 Framework Programme; H2020 Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, MSCA, (101034337); H2020 Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, MSCA

Existing pattern-matching and template-matching methods cannot adapt to unseen drawing styles, while supervised deep learning methods require labor-intensive manual annotation. Additionally, current vision-based detection models discard the rich vector and semantic information embedded in CAD drawings by treating them as raster images.

On 99 unseen instances across 5 untrained object types, the method maintained strong performance with 98.9% precision, 90.9% recall, and F1 score of 0.95, demonstrating strong generalization. The method significantly outperformed two baselines: vision-based template matching (F1=0.37) and graph-based pattern matching (F1=0.62), with average processing time of 11.60 seconds per drawing.

Author full names: Odey Alshboul, Ali Shehadeh
Title: Adaptive integration of BIM and Navisworks for real-time clash detection using the XGBoost algorithm
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Integrating building information modeling (BIM) with advanced clash detection technologies, exemplified by Navisworks, has become crucial for effectively managing complex construction projects across architectural, civil, and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) disciplines. Traditional clash detection methods remain reactive, leading to costly delays and inefficiencies. This study addresses this gap by presenting an adaptive integration framework that employs the extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) machine learning algorithm for real-time predictive clash detection. Through direct interfacing with BIM models, our system offers dynamic updates and proactive conflict anticipation, allowing for instant corrective actions. Performance evaluation across diverse BIM projects shows that our framework significantly improved accuracy (0.62 to 0.92), recall (0.65 to 0.93), F1 score (0.65 to 0.92), and receiver operating characteristics (ROC) (0.66 to 0.91). These advancements not only optimize project coordination and efficiency but also dramatically lower the frequency of costly delays and rework, setting a new benchmark in automated clash detection within the construction field. Future research should discover the integration of extended reality (XR) technologies for immersive clash visualization, further enhancing collaboration and decision-making in BIM workflows.
Author Keywords: Adaptive learning; Building information modeling; Extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost); Navisworks; Project efficiency; Real-time clash detection

False positives and noncritical clashes overwhelm coordinators, while complex interdependencies between architectural, civil, and MEP disciplines demand more predictive approaches. Existing methodologies do not adequately address the need for real-time, adaptive, and proactive clash anticipation across diverse project complexities.

The system reportedly reduced identified clashes by 45%, lowered resolution time by 55%, and enhanced clash prediction accuracy by approximately 40%. XGBoost outperformed LightGBM and CatBoost across all metrics, and was successfully validated on the Green Public Park, Ride and Connect building project in Irbid, Jordan, detecting and resolving multiple high-severity hard clashes.

Author full names: Martin Urbieta, Matias Urbieta, Guillermo Burriel
Title: AI-driven extraction of electrical circuits from floorplans for BIM
Abstract: BIM solutions require a digital model as a foundation to optimize processes such as maintenance, infrastructure renovation, or demolition. However, a vast number of analog building plans are archived by public entities managing urban development, and manually converting these plans into digital models, which is prohibitively expensive. To address this gap, the paper introduces an approach for organizations who need to convert large datasets of legacy electrical floorplans into a BIM. The approach leverages a Machine Learning model for instance segmentation to detect electrical features, and the line-segment detection model DeepLSD for extracting cable traces. To support model training, a new dataset, referred as IPVBA-ELEC, is provided. The approach assembles circuits by establishing semantic relationships between circuit components and wires, and store them in an IFC file. Case studies were evaluated using quantitative and qualitative techniques yielding promising results and encouraging further research of additional MEP domains.

Author full names: Miyoung Uhm, Ghang Lee, Boyoung Jeon
Title: An analysis of BIM jobs and competencies based on the use of terms in the industry
Abstract: The emergence of building information modeling (BIM) has generated several BIM jobs. However, despite opinions by BIM experts, questions regarding BIM jobs and their competencies still have no clear solution. This paper addresses this question by the collection and analysis of 242 online job postings, written in English, from the US, the UK, and China. These 242 job postings comprised a total of 32,495 words, from which 35 types of job titles and 5,998 terms related to job competency were extracted. Sequentially, the 35 job types were classified into eight BIM job types by analyzing the relations between the job titles using the role and position analysis of social network analysis. The eight BIM job types were BIM project manager, director, BIM manager, BIM coordinator, BIM designer, senior architect, BIM mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) coordinator, and BIM technician. The 5,998 competency-related terms were categorized into 43 competency elements using the O*NET classification as a framework for analysis. The 43 competencies were then subcategorized into essential, common, and job-specific competencies for the eight BIM job types. The findings of this paper could contribute to the research, industry, and academia by a) providing researchers with a scientific foundation for conducting studies related to BIM jobs and competence in the future; b) setting up guidelines for recruiting and training BIM experts in the industry; and c) allowing universities to develop BIM-related courses depending on their educational goals.
Author Keywords: BIM jobs and competency; Building information modeling (BIM); Essential, common and job-specific competency for BIM; O*NET classification; Social network analysis (SNA)

Author full names: Bonsang Koo, Jonghoon Kim, Jung-In Kim
Title: An empirical study of MEP workspace modeling approaches for 4D model-based time-space conflict analyses: Case study on the international linear collider project
Abstract: The use of 4D model-based time space conflict analysis to detect potential workspace conflicts can be vital in projects with highly compacted Mechanical/Electrical/Piping (MEP) systems. However, manually modeling individual workspace requirements can be time consuming and error prone. Whereas existing research have focused on formalizing workspace types and conflict taxonomies, determining the appropriate 3D modeling approach available in commercial CAD applications have not been examined in detail. This research addresses this need by performing a case study using the International Linear Collider project, which requires installing MEP components in heavily congested areas and in limited time. Three modeling approaches - isotropic tolerance, solid and parametric modeling - are used to model workspaces and subsequently evaluated with respect to the accuracy and efficiency in generating workspaces, and also the ease in updating workspace requirements. Results indicate that parametric modeling, which enables automatic update of workspaces, provides the greatest leverage in model reuse and flexibility.
Author Keywords: 3D workspace representation; 4D model; Building Information Modeling; parametric modeling; solid modeling; time space conflict analysis
Funding Details: Seoul National University of Science and Technology
