relevance_notes: Case study setting is a particle accelerator (International Linear Collider) rather than a typical building project. Electrical work is one of three MEP categories rather than the primary subject, which may matter for the review's electrical contractor audience.
research_problem: Projects with high-density Mechanical, Electrical, and Piping (MEP) systems require 4D model-based time-space conflict analysis to detect workspace conflicts before construction, but manually modeling each workspace requirement on a 3D model is tedious, error-prone, and time-consuming. Existing research has formalized workspace types and conflict taxonomies, yet the most efficient 3D modeling approach available in commercial CAD applications has not been examined in detail. Without an efficient modeling approach, 4D-based workspace analysis becomes practically prohibitive on full-scale projects with thousands of components and frequent design changes.
research_objective: To examine and compare three 3D modeling methods (isotropic tolerance, solid, and parametric) for representing MEP workspaces in 4D time-space conflict analysis on the International Linear Collider project.
specific_methods: Comparative case study; Time logging of modeling effort; Semi-structured interviews with project manager and foremen; Visual confirmation by practitioners; Akinci et al. workspace and conflict taxonomy; Clash detection using Navisworks Clash Detective
data_type: BIM model; CAD drawings; Specifications; Interview transcripts
data_size: Two RF tunnel sections (76 m total), 73 components, 212 individual workspaces modeled, 236 workspace conflicts identified
future_research_directions: Test a mixed solid and parametric modeling approach for large projects; Develop formal guidance for defining parametric constraints; Evaluate modeling approaches with first-time practitioners; Validate findings beyond a single case study
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Schedule compression; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Scheduling
electrical_work_category: MEP coordination generally; Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance)
key_findings: Of the three modeling methods, parametric modeling was most suited to the project's needs. Isotropic tolerance modeling correctly represented only 63 of 212 workspaces and could not classify workspace types, while solid and parametric modeling both correctly represented all 212 workspaces and identified 236 workspace conflicts. Parametric modeling took 1 hour longer to set up initially (6 hours versus 5 hours for solid modeling) but only 25 minutes to regenerate workspaces after design changes, compared with 2 hours for solid modeling. Scaling to the full ILC project of 20,000 components with an assumed 20 percent design change rate, parametric modeling would save approximately 6,333 modeling hours.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Field supervisor or foreman
quantified_benefit: Parametric modeling reduced workspace regeneration time from 120 minutes to 25 minutes (about 79 percent) compared with solid modeling, projecting 6,333 modeling hours saved across the full ILC project
research_sponsor: Seoul National University of Science and Technology
industry_involvement: Project manager and foremen of the specialty contractors were interviewed to gather workspace requirements, schedule logic, and durations, and they visually confirmed the accuracy of the modeled workspaces and the identified workspace conflicts.
limitations_acknowledged: Only three modelers conducted the case study, so the logged times are not intended as absolute or general benchmarks. The authors note it is difficult to draw a concrete conclusion about which modeling method is predominantly better from a single study. Parametric modeling itself has limitations: first-time practitioners may struggle to apply constraints, and rules can be under- or over-constrained because there is no formal guidance on the correct number or method of constraints.
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported
software_platform: AutoCAD; Navisworks (Timeliner and Clash Detective); Primavera P3; CATIA; Rhinoceros

Author full names: Fernanda Leite, Asli Akcamete, Burcu Akinci, Guzide Atasoy, Semiha Kiziltas
Title: Analysis of modeling effort and impact of different levels of detail in building information models
Abstract: The main objectives of this paper are to evaluate the modeling effort associated with generating building information modeling (BIM) at different levels of detail (LoD) and the impact of LoD in a project in supporting mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) design coordination. Results show an increase in total modeling time ranging from doubling the modeling effort to eleven folding it, when going from one LoD to another. When comparing modeling time per object, rates ranged from 0.2 (decreased modeling time) to 1.56 (increased modeling time). Based on the experiments done in MEP design coordination, it was observed that the automatic clash detection process using BIMs, with its consistently higher recall rate, provides a more complete identification of clashes, with the cost of false positives (low precision). This study showed that additional modeling effort can lead to more comprehensive analyses and better decision support during design and construction.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modeling; Level of detail impact; Modeling effort
Funding Details: Pennsylvania Infrastructure Technology Alliance
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP design coordination including electrical systems (cable trays, lights, conduits) as one of several trades, with electrical subcontractors participating in clash detection analyses.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally focuses on Building Information Modeling (BIM) and automatic clash detection, which are core digital technologies in the review's scope.
relevance_notes: Electrical work is treated as one of several MEP trades rather than the central focus, but the BIM-based clash detection findings are directly relevant to electrical contractors performing MEP coordination.
research_problem: While benefits of BIM are widely discussed in the AEC/FM industry, there is a lack of research investigating the modeling effort required to generate BIMs at different levels of detail (LoD) and the impact of LoD choices on downstream project tasks. Practitioners lack guidance on how much modeling effort is justified for specific uses such as MEP design coordination, and on how LoD affects the precision and completeness of automated clash detection compared to traditional 2D manual coordination.
research_objective: To evaluate the modeling effort associated with generating BIMs at different levels of detail and the impact of LoD on supporting MEP design coordination through clash detection.
specific_methods: Comparative case study analysis; Modeling time tracking; Object count analysis; Automatic clash detection; Manual 2D overlay clash detection; Precision and recall evaluation; Observation of coordination meetings
data_type: BIM model; CAD drawings; Project records
data_size: Two construction projects: a 5-storey commercial building (189,000 sq ft) and a 9-storey academic building (210,000 sq ft); five months of weekly MEP coordination meeting observations
future_research_directions: Investigate BIM LoD requirements of various stakeholders for different activities; Develop larger set of case studies for LoD evaluation; Use different software systems to evaluate software bias; Use modelers with different experience levels; Integrate BIM more fully into the product development process
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Decision support; Quality improvement
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery
key_findings: Total modeling time increased between 2x and 11x when going from precise to fabrication LoD, while modeling time per object ranged from 0.2 (decreased) to 1.56 (increased), showing that more detail does not require proportionally more effort. Automatic clash detection using BIM consistently achieved higher recall (more complete clash identification) but lower precision (more false positives) than manual 2D coordination, with recall reaching 1.00 for ductwork-plumbing and plumbing-fire protection pairs. Missing objects in the BIM (such as cable trays and hangers) caused false negatives, indicating that LoD must match the intended use to avoid field-detected clashes.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Modeling time increased 2-11x from precise to fabrication LoD; automatic clash detection recall reached 1.00 for some trade pairs vs. 0.09-0.75 for manual 2D coordination
research_sponsor: Pennsylvania Infrastructure Technology Alliance (PITA)
industry_involvement: Two general contractors provided access to active construction projects; subcontractors (ductwork, electrical, plumbing, fire protection, heating) allowed researchers to observe weekly coordination meetings for five months; a third-party modeling company developed one project's BIM.
industry_references__references_details: References Associated General Contractors (AGC), National Institute for Building Sciences (NIBS), National Building Information Modeling Standard (NBIMS), US General Services Administration (GSA), and US Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) BIM guidelines and adoption mandates.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that both projects' models were developed in the same software system, so software bias was not evaluated. The studied BIMs were not fully integrated into the product development process (one was modeled by the research team after construction started, the other by a third party), which may have limited the realized benefits compared to a model developed collaboratively from early design.
theoretical_framework__framework_name: Vico Software Model Progression Specification for Levels of Detail (conceptual, approximate geometry, precise geometry, fabrication, as-built); information retrieval precision and recall metrics
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported

Author full names: Liang-Ting Tsai, Hung-Lin Chi, Tzong-Hann Wu, Shih-Chung Kang
Title: AR-based automatic pipeline planning coordination for on-site mechanical, electrical and plumbing system conflict resolution
Abstract: Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) system conflicts are complicated problems given the involved multidisciplinary nature and constraint-bearing coordination. Although Building Information Modeling (BIM) shows benefit to resolving the conflicts before construction, the on-site pipeline inspection and further adjustment are inevitably necessary due to practical issues caused by deviations with plans. Given this, the research aims at developing an on-site pipeline inspection and automatic coordination approach by using Augmented Reality (AR) and a grid-based path-planning algorithm. The approach allows the site practitioners to compare the newly added pipe layout plan with the existing pipelines and re-plan the pipe layout where encountered conflicts, further obtaining a solution without clashes. The method considers MEP system constraints in practice and searches conflict-free pipe layout plans in near real-time. Furthermore, the path coordination process also considers MEP design criteria, including its function, construction, and maintenance. The planned pipe layout is displayed through AR to link the information from the virtual model to the real world. The performance tests are conducted in virtual scenarios with different grid cell sizes and priorities of pipes to evaluate the planning results. The test results show that under room and aisle space scenarios, the implemented approach can obtain path solutions with approximately 10 s, and most of them comply with the pre-defined MEP design requirements. Through this study, automatic pipe planning with constraints is proved to be feasible to provide an effective on-site conflict resolution approach to improve the MEP construction process and future maintenance.
Author Keywords: AR; Conflict resolution; MEP system; Path coordination; Pipeline planning
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination broadly with electrical pipes/conduits as one of several systems considered alongside firing, pressurized, and gravity-driven water pipes.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops an AR-based system integrated with BIM and a grid-based A* path-planning algorithm for on-site MEP conflict resolution.
relevance_notes: Electrical systems are explicitly modeled as a distinct pipe category with relation constraints (e.g., electrical pipes must avoid water pipes above), making this relevant to electrical conduit routing coordination.
research_problem: Although BIM helps resolve MEP conflicts before construction, on-site pipeline inspection and adjustment remain necessary due to deviations between plans and as-built conditions. Up to 78% of BIM clash detections may be ineffective, manual coordination is time-consuming, and constructability and maintainability issues are hard to address from 2D drawings or 3D models alone. Existing AR applications rarely support detailed on-site MEP coordination because portable input interfaces are limited and re-routing is tedious.
research_objective: To develop an on-site pipeline inspection and automatic coordination approach using AR visualization and a grid-based path-planning algorithm to resolve MEP conflicts.
specific_methods: Modified A* path-planning algorithm; Grid-based spatial discretization; Constraint-based penalty weighting; Priority sorting heuristics; AR visualization with Visual Inertial Odometry; Performance testing across grid cell sizes and priority orders
data_size: Two virtual test scenarios: room space with 12 pipes (6 firing, 2 electrical, 2 pressurized, 2 gravity) and S-shape aisle with 13 pipes (5 firing, 4 electrical, 4 pressurized); tested across 6 grid cell sizes and 6 priority sorting approaches
future_research_directions: Develop multi-criteria sorting method with weighting mechanism; Balance grid cell size and searching range using optimization rather than only heuristics; Integrate with current BIM tools or automatic modeling techniques; Validate on real project cases beyond virtual scenarios; Find optimal solutions beyond single-priority sequential planning
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Decision support; Automation of manual task
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; RFI or change order management
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); MEP coordination generally
key_findings: The MEP A* planner produced conflict-free pipe layouts complying with predefined design constraints in approximately 10 seconds per pipe at grid cell sizes of 0.15 to 0.2 meters. Cell sizes of 0.1 m gave higher accuracy but required up to 28-45 seconds per pipe (too slow), while cell sizes above 0.25 m sometimes failed to find paths or violated constraints. Sorting pipes by cross-section consistently produced the shortest paths and fewest elbows while satisfying all constraints, making it the recommended priority criterion.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Path planning solutions obtained in approximately 10 seconds per pipe at 0.15-0.2 m grid cell sizes; re-planning completed within 1 minute compared to ~5 minutes for design-stage tools and ~2 hours for manual experienced engineers
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Continental Engineer Corporation general managers and engineers were interviewed to determine system requirements for on-site MEP system conflict resolution.
limitations_acknowledged: The approach only obtains path solutions from a single priority sorting consideration at a time, achieving sequential planning rather than brute-force search, so the optimal solution may not appear in the discussed sorting approaches. Multi-criteria sorting with weighting is not yet supported. Validation was limited to virtual scenarios rather than real construction projects.
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported
software_platform: Unity3D; Autodesk Revit; ARKit; iPhone 6S (iOS)

Author full names: Mohamed Marzouk, Mohamed Zaher
Title: Artificial intelligence exploitation in facility management using deep learning
Abstract: Purpose: This paper aims to apply a methodology that is capable to classify and localize mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) elements to assist facility managers. Furthermore, it assists in decreasing the technical complexity and sophistication of different systems to the facility management (FM) team. Design/methodology/approach: This research exploits artificial intelligence (AI) in FM operations through proposing a new system that uses a deep learning pre-trained model for transfer learning. The model can identify new MEP elements through image classification with a deep convolutional neural network using a support vector machine (SVM) technique under supervised learning. Also, an expert system is developed and integrated with an Android application to the proposed system to identify the required maintenance for the identified elements. FM team can reach the identified assets with bluetooth tracker devices to perform the required maintenance. Findings: The proposed system aids facility managers in their tasks and decreases the maintenance costs of facilities by maintaining, upgrading, operating assets cost-effectively using the proposed system. Research limitations/implications: The paper considers three fire protection systems for proactive maintenance, where other structural or architectural systems can also significantly affect the level of service and cost expensive repairs and maintenance. Also, the proposed system relies on different platforms that required to be consolidated for facility technicians and managers end-users. Therefore, the authors will consider these limitations and expand the study as a case study in future work. Originality/value: This paper assists in a proactive manner to decrease the lack of knowledge of the required maintenance to MEP elements that leads to a lower life cycle cost. These MEP elements have a big share in the operation and maintenance costs of building facilities.
Author Keywords: Artificial intelligence; Deep learning; Expert system; Facilities management; Neural networks; Supervised learning
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP facility management broadly with focus on fire protection systems (FM200, fire extinguisher, fire hose cabinet) rather than electrical construction specifically; electrical is mentioned only as one of many MEP categories.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies deep learning (CNN with transfer learning on AlexNet), an expert system, and a mobile/Bluetooth tracking application for facility management.
relevance_notes: Paper is about facility management of MEP elements with a fire protection proof-of-concept; electrical content is limited to taxonomy. May be borderline for an electrical construction review.
research_problem: Facility managers face increasing complexity across many MEP subsystems where operation and maintenance costs can reach 60% of total cost, and manual classification, localization, and identification of required maintenance for MEP assets is error-prone, time-consuming, and labor-intensive. Lack of knowledge about required maintenance leads to reactive maintenance, sudden operational failures, and higher life cycle costs. The authors identify a gap in research applying CNN-based AI to assist facility managers in classifying, locating, and maintaining MEP elements.
research_objective: To develop a system that uses deep learning, an expert system, and Bluetooth tracking to classify MEP elements, identify required maintenance, and locate assets to assist facility managers.
specific_methods: Convolutional neural network; Transfer learning; Support vector machine; Supervised learning; Expert system shell (decision tree); Confusion matrix evaluation; Stochastic gradient descent with momentum
data_size: 81 images of three fire protection systems (FM200, fire extinguisher, fire hose cabinet); 80% training / 20% testing split
future_research_directions: Consider reactive maintenance in addition to proactive maintenance; Integrate platforms into a single mobile application; Enable real-time object classification without computers; Provide direct access to drawings, specifications, and manuals; Validate the approach through case study and expert interviews; Expand beyond three fire protection elements to other MEP and structural/architectural systems

value_proposition: Cost reduction; Decision support; Automation of manual task

key_findings: Transfer learning on the AlexNet CNN was completed in 8:27 minutes and produced a confusion matrix indicating accurate classification of all test images across the three fire protection classes (FM200, fire extinguisher, fire hose cabinet), with training loss decreasing toward zero. The integrated system combining CNN classification, Bluetooth tracking, expert system, and an Android application ('FM Expert') was demonstrated as capable of identifying MEP assets, locating them, and retrieving required proactive maintenance instructions.
contractor_role_impacted: Project manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Owner or client
quantified_benefit: 100% classification accuracy on the test set across three fire protection classes (training completed in 8:27 minutes)
research_sponsor: Not reported

They also note that the proposed system relies on different platforms that need to be consolidated for end-users, and that validation through a case study has not yet been performed.

Author full names: Ercüment N. Dizdar, Oğuz Koçar
Title: Artificial neural network-based risk assessment for occupational accidents in the shipbuilding industry in Turkey
Abstract: Accidents in the workplace are critical issues that necessitate attention from environmental safety and health perspectives to enhance operational safety. The elevated rate of workplace accidents in Turkey underscores deficiencies in reliability analysis studies. Effective planning of preventive measures, considering factors such as location and timing, is pivotal in accident prevention. Sociological and regional disparities, alongside technical factors such as service type, working hours, and age, contribute significantly to accident causation. Reliability analysis studies aim to identify potential causes of accidents, enabling early detection of hazardous situations and combinations. This proactive approach allows responsible personnel to implement measures that mitigate or eliminate specific types of accidents, thereby safeguarding both lives and business assets. This study utilizes Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to forecast potential occupational accidents in the shipbuilding industry before they occur. Analyzing data from 146 occupational accidents involving ship electricians between 2012 and 2016, out of a total of 1165 occupational accidents in the industry, the study estimates potential accidents for 2017. The results demonstrate that ANN achieves high accuracy in predicting occupational accidents.
Author Keywords: Accident prediction; Artificial neural networks; Occupational safety; Reliability analysis; Risk assessment
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper specifically focuses on occupational accidents involving ship electricians, a specific electrical worker group, though in the shipbuilding industry rather than building construction.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) to predict occupational accidents.
relevance_notes: The paper concerns ship electricians in shipbuilding industry rather than electrical contractors in building construction; reviewer should decide if shipbuilding electrical work is in scope for the review.
research_problem: Occupational accidents in Turkey's shipbuilding industry remain frequent and severe, with deficiencies in reliability analysis studies that would enable proactive prevention. Existing accident analysis methods do not adequately account for the multifactorial nature of accidents (work station, age, experience) to predict them before they occur. There is a need for predictive models that can forecast potential occupational accidents specifically for ship electricians, who face frequent hazards.
research_objective: To use Artificial Neural Networks to forecast potential occupational accidents involving ship electricians in the shipbuilding industry before they occur.
specific_methods: Artificial Neural Network (ANN); Multilayer perceptron; Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm; Backpropagation; Log-sigmoid and tan-sigmoid activation functions; Coefficient of determination (R²); RMSE evaluation
data_size: 146 occupational accidents involving ship electricians (out of 1,165 total) from 23 shipyards over 2012-2016; 106 records used after filtering (84 training, 22 testing)
value_proposition: Safety improvement; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Safety or electrocution or arc flash
key_findings: Two ANN models achieved high predictive performance for accident type prediction. The 3×13×5×1 model achieved 97% training accuracy and 99% testing accuracy, while the 3×29×1 model achieved 99% training and 93% testing accuracy. The Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm with log-sigmoid/tan-sigmoid activation functions outperformed alternatives, demonstrating that ANN can effectively predict the type of occupational accident based on work station, experience, and age inputs.
contractor_role_impacted: Safety manager; Electrician or field worker; Project manager
quantified_benefit: 97-99% training accuracy and 93-99% testing accuracy for predicting accident type
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Accident data was obtained from 23 different shipyard companies in the Turkish shipbuilding industry; no formal advisory panel or task force is mentioned.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that the difference between ANN estimation results and actual values can partly be attributed to socio-economic factors that are not included in the occupational accident records and therefore could not be evaluated. They also note that the analysis results alone are insufficient to determine which modeling approach fits best to a specific case.

Author full names: Jad Chalhoub, Steven K. Ayer, Kieren H. McCord
Title: Augmented reality to enable users to identify deviations for model reconciliation
Abstract: Effective use of Building Information Modeling (BIM) during operation typically requires modeled content to accurately match the built spaces, which necessitates effective field verification techniques to ensure that BIM content matches the actual built conditions. Some contractors lever-age laser scanning and other reality‐capture technologies to verify modeled content prior to turno-ver, but these approaches can be time‐ and resource‐intensive. Augmented reality (AR) enables users to view BIM content overlaid on their field of view of the built space. Research suggests potential for using AR for tasks related to field verification, but a study that systematically explores the specific types of deviations that can be detected with this technology is missing from the current liter-ature. This paper tasks participants with using immersive AR to identify deviations from BIM in a ceiling plenum space that includes installed Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) compo-nents, which would typically be included in a coordinated BIM. The results suggest that AR can enable users to effectively identify large deviations and missing building elements. However, the results do not indicate that AR effectively enables users to identify small deviations and can poten-tially lead to identifying false positive observations, where accurately constructed elements are per-ceived as deviating from BIM. These results suggest that immersive AR can effectively be used to check whether recently built elements conform to the intended BIM in instances where speed of verification is more important than adhering to strict tolerances. For instances where accuracy (less than two inches) is critical, the results of this work suggest that AR can be used to help determine where in the building more accurate, but more resource‐intensive, reality‐capture technologies should be used.
Author Keywords: Augmented reality; Deviation detection; Laser scanning; Quality control
Funding Details: National Science Foundation, NSF, (IIS‐1566274)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The study was conducted in partnership with a large electrical subcontractor and focuses on MEP systems including electrical conduits, but addresses MEP broadly rather than electrical-specific work.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally evaluates augmented reality (AR) combined with BIM and laser scanning for field verification of constructed MEP systems.
relevance_notes: Industry partner is an electrical subcontractor (Rosendin Electric), and three of four deliberate deviations were on electrical conduits, giving the paper meaningful electrical relevance despite framing as MEP.
research_problem: Field verification to ensure BIM matches as-built conditions is currently resource-intensive, relying on laser scanning and photogrammetry that are time- and labor-consuming. Augmented reality has been proposed as a faster alternative, but prior literature lacks a systematic exploration of which specific types of deviations between BIM and built conditions can actually be detected by AR users in realistic MEP/plenum environments.
research_objective: To investigate the types of BIM-to-built deviations that AR users can detect in installed MEP ceiling plenum systems, and the frequency of false positive observations.
specific_methods: Controlled human-subjects experiment; Think-aloud protocol; NASA-TLX cognitive workload assessment; Pre- and post-session questionnaires; Laser scanning for ground-truth model; Descriptive statistics
data_type: Point cloud; BIM model; Survey responses; Video
data_size: 27 graduate student participants verifying one hallway with 4 introduced deviations
future_research_directions: Test AR field verification with experienced QA/QC practitioners; Evaluate hybrid AR plus laser scanning workflows in practice; Investigate marker-less and automatic model registration for AR; Assess setup time and productivity of AR on active construction sites; Explore mitigation of parallax effects through training or display tuning
digital_technology: Augmented reality; BIM; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Mixed reality; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Quality improvement; Decision support; Automation of manual task
pain_point_addressed: Quality control; Coordination or clash detection; Commissioning or closeout
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); MEP coordination generally
key_findings: AR enabled high detection rates for large deviations (96%) and missing elements (74%), but only 41% of participants detected a small (under two inches) deviation. Some participants misclassified deviations (e.g., labeling a large shift as a missing element), and 40% reported at least one false positive, all of which were small perceived shifts likely caused by AR parallax effects. The authors conclude AR is well suited for quick field checks and for guiding where laser scanning should be focused, but not for detecting sub-two-inch deviations.
contractor_role_impacted: Field supervisor or foreman; BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager
quantified_benefit: 96% detection of large deviations, 74% detection of missing elements, 41% detection of small (<2 in) deviations
research_sponsor: National Science Foundation (NSF) Grant No. IIS-1566274
industry_involvement: Rosendin Electric, a large electrical subcontractor in the southwestern US, partnered on the study by performing the laser scan, generating the as-built BIM, and providing input throughout preparation and testing.
limitations_acknowledged: The participants were graduate students rather than experienced QA/QC practitioners, though the authors argue this likely yields conservative results. The approach requires an existing detailed BIM, and the AR technology used (HoloLens 1) suffers from tracking and parallax issues that could increase setup time and were not factored into productivity assessment. Author full names: Patrick Dallasega, Felix Schulze, Andrea Revolti
Title: Augmented reality to overcome visual management implementation barriers in construction: A MEP case study
Abstract: Visual Management (VM) is an important Lean method to enhance information flow and reduce waste in construction. However, its adoption is hindered by several barriers. Scientific works mention that emerging technologies can support or replace conventional VM practices, but empirical evidence is missing. Based on an in-depth literature review, we derived the research questions (RQs), arguing if Augmented Reality (AR) could mitigate VM implementation barriers. Thus, a corresponding AR measurement model was developed. Through a case study of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) installations in a multi-story apartment building, the RQs were answered using an AR head-mounted display (HMD). To gather the necessary empirical evidence, the data was collected through direct observations on-site and through semi-structured interviews. The study findings show that (1) AR provided time savings and generally satisfactory accuracy levels. (2) AR demonstrably reduced the training effort to better support MEP marking work. (3) The use of AR reduced the resistance to change to adopt VM practices, although concerns were raised about poor ergonomics and work safety risks. Future research activities should consist of investigating the potential of other emerging technologies to overcome the common Lean implementation barriers in construction.
Author Keywords: Augmented reality; barriers; KPIs; lean construction; MEP; visual management
Funding Details: Mader GmbH

While scientific works mention emerging technologies like AR could support or replace conventional VM practices, empirical evidence is missing, and there is no measurement model to identify the benefits of AR for construction projects. Practical use cases of AR in construction are needed to justify investment in the technology.

key_findings: AR-supported marking work delivered time savings of approximately 18% for HVS, 58% for underfloor heating, and 84% for electrical work compared to conventional methods. Average accuracy deviations were 3.47 cm (HVS), 3.76 cm (UFH), and 1.80 cm (electrical), which were generally satisfactory except in bathrooms where 0.5 cm precision was required. AR reduced training effort (U/A ratio improved by an average 18.7%) and reduced resistance to adopting VM, though concerns were raised about ergonomics (neck strain from HMD weight) and work safety (impaired field of vision, trip hazards).

quantified_benefit: 84% time reduction for electrical marking work (7 minutes vs 45 minutes); 18.7% average improvement in understanding/action ratio; accuracy of 1.80 cm for electrical marking
research_sponsor: Department of Innovation, Research, University and Museums of the Autonomous Province of Bozen/Bolzano (covered Open Access publication costs); Mader GmbH provided industry contribution
industry_involvement: The case study was conducted with Mader GmbH, a medium-sized MEP contractor that provided the construction project, four foremen, and a BIM coordinator who participated as test subjects performing actual marking work using the AR HMD on a live residential project.
limitations_acknowledged: Accuracy deviations were not differentiated between those caused by AR technology, the BIM model, and the human operator, and registering accuracy was not specifically measured. Only ACC_XY was measured (not ACC_Z) due to BIM model inconsistencies in object heights. The semi-structured interview results reflect subjective perceptions, the scope was limited to MEP marking work, and participant selection based on 'strong affinity for technology' may have influenced evaluations.
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported
software_platform: Graphisoft Archicad 24; BIM Holoview; Microsoft HoloLens 2; Trimble XR10

Author full names: Luping Li, Xin Xu, Xing Su, Xiaoxuan Zhou, Han Lin, Hubo Cai
Title: Automated and semantic-rich BIM modeling of MEP systems using monocular cameras
Source title: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Abstract: Accurate and updated as-built building information modeling (BIM) has become an effective tool for managing mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems due to its capabilities in enhancing visualization and data interoperability. Video-based 3D reconstruction methods offer a cost-effective and portable solution for creating as-built BIM models. However, the process of extracting images from video often results in low-quality input data due to issues such as blurriness and distortion, and the low accuracy and density of reconstructed point clouds results in challenges for segmentation and detailed modeling. Using monocular cameras only, this paper presents a novel, automated BIM modeling approach that overcomes these challenges. The newly designed method consists of three compartments: an adaptive frame extraction method that efficiently generates high-quality image sequences from video; the key image identification and semantic transfer techniques that simplify semantic segmentation by converting it into a 2D task on selected images; and the undirected graph-based modeling approach combined with prior knowledge that can produce semantically rich BIM models. The method was tested across multiple scenarios. Results demonstrated its reliability in generating accurate as-built BIM for MEP systems from videos. The method fills an important gap in automated BIM modeling to provide accurate as-built information for managing MEP systems.
Funding Details: Center for Balance Architecture; Zhejiang University, NCKU; National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (71971196); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly but the case studies and modeling focus on fire sprinkler pipes (plumbing/mechanical) rather than electrical installations specifically.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies computer vision, photogrammetry, point cloud processing, and BIM automation using monocular cameras.
relevance_notes: Paper covers MEP modeling generically but experimental validation is on fire sprinkler pipes. Electrical systems are included under the MEP umbrella but not specifically targeted. Worth manual review to decide if MEP-general methods count for the electrical construction review.
research_problem: Accurate as-built BIM is needed to manage MEP systems, but existing automated modeling approaches rely on expensive terrestrial laser scanning that is difficult to use in concealed spaces. Image-based methods using monocular cameras are cheaper and more portable but suffer from low-quality video frames, sparse and inaccurate reconstructed point clouds, lack of physical scale, and difficulty performing semantic segmentation. Additionally, existing methods produce only geometric BIM and overlook semantic information such as materials, manufacturers, and cost needed for O&M.
research_objective: To develop an automated, semantic-rich BIM modeling approach for MEP pipeline systems using only monocular camera video as input.
specific_methods: Structure from Motion (SfM); Multi-view Stereo (MVS); Adaptive frame extraction; Principal Component Analysis (PCA); K-Nearest Neighbors clustering; Shi-Tomasi corner detection; DBSCAN denoising; Undirected graph modeling
data_type: Video; 2D images; Point cloud; BIM model
data_size: Six representative pipeline scenarios in an underground garage; videos at 4K resolution, 60fps; reconstructed point clouds ranging from ~40,000 to 2.7 million points
future_research_directions: Partition large-scale scenes into smaller scenes for individual modeling and integration; Design more comprehensive relative relationship detection algorithms; Handle pipeline systems with more complex topological relationships; Address non-coaxial alignments and specific angular separations between pipes
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Cost reduction; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Commissioning or closeout; Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: The proposed adaptive frame extraction with NP ranges of 100-150 and 150-200 outperformed fixed-step extraction across six scenarios, achieving reconstruction accuracy as low as 4.3mm in real-world scale. The semantic transfer method successfully segmented reconstructed point clouds into pipeline instances even with only 10% of images manually labeled. The graph-based modeling approach accurately reconstructed BIM models with correct pipeline topology and semantic information across all six test scenarios.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager
quantified_benefit: Reconstruction accuracy of 4.3-5.4mm achieved in best-performing configuration; only 10% of images require semantic labeling to achieve comparable segmentation performance
research_sponsor: Center for Balance Architecture, Zhejiang University and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 71971196)
industry_involvement: None reported
limitations_acknowledged: The method is limited to smaller, controlled scenes because 3D reconstruction efficiency decreases with larger numbers of input photos (2-3 hours processing time, 80% on reconstruction). The reconstructed point cloud quality makes it difficult to extract complex spatial relationships between pipelines such as non-coaxial alignments or specific angular separations. The modeling approach simplifies pipe relationships to collinear and perpendicular only, so it may not handle intricate topological features.
data_availability__availability_details: Code is available on GitHub (specific URL not provided in text); data available from corresponding author upon reasonable request
software_platform: COLMAP

Author full names: Qian Zhao, Hao Shi, Liangchen Zhou, Guonian Lv
Title: Automated BIM generation for MEP systems from CAD data using multi-drawing graph integration
Abstract: Building information modeling (BIM) of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems is essential for building facility management. Computer-aided-design (CAD) data are detailed sources for MEP BIM modeling. However, existing methods for MEP BIM are complex, leading to heavy reliance on manual intervention. This paper addresses this challenge by proposing an approach for generating MEP BIM models from CAD data. Graph structures are introduced to represent MEP systems, and multiple graph structures converted from CAD drawings are utilized to match pipeline components and aggregate dispersed information across various drawings. Based on the integrated pipeline graph, missing information is inferred and completed considering the relationships between components, ensuring detailed and accurate modeling results. Experiments on an actual factory case demonstrate the reliability and efficiency of this approach. This paper contributes to the MEP BIM theory by providing a perspective on interpreting MEP CAD data and a robust technical route of CAD-to-BIM conversion for MEP systems.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Computer-aided design (CAD); Graph-based methods; Industry foundation classes (IFC); Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) systems as a unified category, with electrical treated as one of three subsystems alongside mechanical and plumbing, though the validation experiment focuses primarily on plumbing data.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops an automated CAD-to-BIM conversion methodology using graph-based information integration, with BIM as the primary output technology.
relevance_notes: MEP-wide methodology paper that includes electrical in scope but is not electrical-specific. The validation case is plumbing-focused, though the framework applies to all three MEP subsystems.
research_problem: Existing BIM modeling methods for MEP systems are complex and rely heavily on manual intervention. As-built methods using LiDAR or sensors suffer from occlusion and cannot capture pipelines hidden inside building structures, while CAD-based methods typically process one drawing at a time and fail to integrate information distributed across multiple complementary views (floor plans, system drawings, legend tables). No generalized method exists for multi-drawing information integration in MEP CAD-to-BIM conversion, leading to incomplete models and limited automation efficiency.
research_objective: To propose an automated approach for generating MEP BIM models from CAD data using graph structures and multi-drawing information integration.
specific_methods: Graph-based representation of pipeline systems; Multi-drawing graph integration; Vertex similarity detection for component matching; Identifier-based component matching; Rule-based information inference and completion; Parametric modeling (swept solid representation); IFC-based BIM model generation; Prototype system validation on a real factory case
data_size: One 3-floor factory project; final BIM contains 37,745 components (>20,000 pipe segments, >16,000 fittings, >1,000 nodes/equipment)
future_research_directions: Fully automate information extraction using deep learning for symbol recognition; Validate the approach on datasets following non-Chinese drafting conventions; Incorporate data-driven approaches in CAD-to-BIM conversion for MEP systems; Replace rule-based graph operations with learned models
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: The prototype reconstructed a BIM model containing 37,745 MEP components with nearly 100% accuracy against the source drawings (zero deviation in pipe segment count, node count, and equipment count; 1.83% deviation in total pipe-segment length, attributed to length adjustments where fittings were inferred and inserted). For the plumbing system alone, the prototype completed modeling in roughly 38 minutes versus more than 5 hours for the same task in Revit by the same professionals, an order-of-magnitude efficiency gain. Graph-based integration across floor plans and system drawings successfully aggregated dispersed location, height, geometry, and attribute information into a unified pipeline graph.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Plumbing modeling time reduced from >5 hours (manual Revit) to ~38 minutes (prototype); nearly 100% reconstruction accuracy on 37,745 components; 1.83% deviation in total pipe length
research_sponsor: National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant no. 2022YFC3803601)
industry_involvement: An actual factory project provided the MEP CAD drawings used for validation; no advisory panel, task force, or industry partner role beyond data access is reported.
industry_references__references_details: BIM Forum Level of Development (LOD) Specification cited as the reference for granularity (LOD500 requirements for as-built MEP models).
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that the information-extraction stage still requires some manual intervention (layer mapping, annotation reconstruction, and review), albeit far less than fully manual modeling. They also note that the experimental data are limited to engineering drawings from mainland China following national drafting conventions, so generalizability to other regional standards is unverified. Finally, the graph operations rely on rule-based constraints derived from prior knowledge rather than data-driven learning.
data_availability__availability_details: The authors state they do not have permission to share data.
software_platform: AutoCAD 2014; Revit; BIMvision; .NET Framework 4.7.2
key_findings themes: AI & Machine Learning for MEP Automation

Author full names: Dena Shamsollahi, Osama Moselhi, Khashayar Khorasani
Title: Automated detection and segmentation of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing components in indoor environments by using the YOLACT++ architecture
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Indoor construction environments, with their high density and detailed components, are complex areas for progress monitoring and reporting. Traditional manual monitoring systems, often constrained by poor lighting and accessibility, are inaccurate and time-consuming. With recent technological advancements, deep learning-based object recognition models have achieved considerable attention in construction. This paper introduces a novel method for progress monitoring and reporting of construction operations, employing digital imaging and the You Only Look At CoefficienTs (YOLACT++) deep learning algorithm to automatically recognize mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) components in challenging indoor settings. Data augmentation techniques and transfer learning were applied to improve the model's generalization and adaptability. The study distinctively focuses on complex components in complicated indoor environments, a less explored area in current research that mainly centered on outdoor or simpler indoor settings. To achieve this, the study enhanced the dataset quality by generating synthetic images that closely represent actual indoor conditions including different lighting, object complexity and scale, occlusion, clutter, and viewpoints. This study also evaluated different mixes of synthetic and real images to determine the optimum combination for effective training. Moving beyond commonly used algorithms such as Mask R-CNN and You Only Look Once (YOLO), the method applied in this work is the YOLACT++ with deformable convolutional neural networks v2 (DCNv2), enhancing the model's ability to handle objects with different scales, postures, rotations, and viewpoints in the images that are essential in indoor environments. The model is validated on a large test dataset, including real images from construction sites, to cover different indoor scenarios. The model achieved a precision of 84.80% and a recall of 85.58% for HVAC duct detection and a precision of 86.87% and a recall of 73.93% for pipe detection, demonstrating its effectiveness under challenging conditions. This method contributes to more accurate automated progress monitoring in indoor environments by reducing manual and error prone inspections.
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP components broadly but the demonstrated target objects are HVAC ducts and pipes; electrical components are mentioned only as part of the MEP acronym and are not actually detected or evaluated.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates a deep learning instance segmentation model (YOLACT++) integrated with BIM-generated synthetic imagery for automated progress monitoring.
relevance_notes: Although framed as MEP recognition, the actual implementation only targets HVAC ducts and pipes (mechanical/plumbing); no electrical components such as conduit, cable tray, or panels are detected. Borderline for inclusion in an electrical-focused review.
research_problem: Indoor construction environments are dense, cluttered, poorly lit, and contain detailed MEP components, making manual progress monitoring slow, error-prone, and inaccurate. Existing deep learning recognition studies focus mainly on outdoor sites or simple indoor objects, with limited investigation of complex MEP components under realistic indoor challenges. Available real construction image datasets are scarce, and synthetic image generation has not been systematically optimized for indoor MEP recognition, particularly regarding the optimal mix of synthetic and real images for training.
research_objective: To develop a methodology using the YOLACT++ instance segmentation model trained on an optimized mix of synthetic and real images to automatically detect and segment MEP components (HVAC ducts and pipes) in challenging indoor construction environments.
specific_methods: YOLACT++ instance segmentation; Deformable Convolutional Networks v2 (DCNv2); Transfer learning (ImageNet pretrained weights); Synthetic image generation from BIM models; Data augmentation (color and geometric transformations); Performance evaluation via mAP, precision, recall, FPS
data_size: 3,135 training images and 497 test images (mix of real photos from two hospital sites and BIM-rendered synthetic images); 8 dataset variants with synthetic ratios from 25% to 60%
future_research_directions: Automate synthetic image generation from BIM models; Increase dataset size and class diversity; Explore semi-supervised or self-supervised learning to reduce annotation effort; Investigate effects of specific synthetic image modifications (lighting, scale, occlusion, viewpoint); Integrate model with progress measurement frameworks and real-time locating systems; Apply preprocessing techniques for image enhancement under low-light and blurry conditions
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Decision support; Quality improvement
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Quality control
key_findings: The YOLACT++ model trained on a dataset with 60% synthetic images achieved the best detection performance, with precision/recall of 84.80%/85.58% for HVAC ducts and 86.87%/73.93% for pipes. The dataset with 55% synthetic images achieved the best mask prediction performance (mAPmask 75.23%) and was identified as the most balanced. Increasing synthetic image ratio generally improved object detection but did not consistently improve mask segmentation, and the model achieved approximately 4.69 to 4.91 FPS on cloud GPU.
contractor_role_impacted: Project manager; BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman
quantified_benefit: Precision 84.80% / recall 85.58% for HVAC duct detection and precision 86.87% / recall 73.93% for pipe detection; mAPmask up to 75.23%
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Pomerleau Inc. authorized use of previously collected images from a Montréal hospital site; an individual provided access to a Tehran hospital site for additional image collection. No formal advisory panel reported.
industry_references__references_details: References the National Institute of Building Sciences (NIBS) for publicly available 3D BIM models used to generate synthetic images.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that synthetic image generation from BIM models was manual and time-consuming. They note manual data labeling is labor-intensive and that the effects of specific synthetic image modifications (lighting, complexity, scale, occlusion, viewpoint) on model performance were not investigated. Prediction speed (~5 FPS) was lower than reported in the original YOLACT++ paper due to limited cloud GPU resources.

Author full names: Yi Tan, Silin Li, Qian Wang
Title: Automated geometric quality inspection of prefabricated housing units using BIM and LiDAR
Abstract: Traditional quality inspection of prefabricated components is labor intensive, time-consuming, and error prone. This study developed an automated geometric quality inspection technique for prefabricated housing units using building information modeling (BIM) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR). The proposed technique collects the 3D laser scanned data of the prefabricated unit using a LiDAR which contains accurate as-built surface geometries of the prefabricated unit. On the other hand, the BIM model of the prefabricated unit contains the as-designed geometries of the unit. The scanned data and BIM model are then automatically processed to inspect the geometric quality of individual elements of the prefabricated units including both structural and mechanical elements, as well as electrical and plumbing (MEP) elements. To validate the proposed technique, experiments were conducted on two prefabricated bathroom units (PBUs). The inspection results showed that the proposed technique can provide accurate quality inspection results with 0.7 mm and 0.9 mm accuracy for structural and MEP elements, respectively. In addition, the experiments also showed that the proposed technique greatly improves the inspection efficiency regarding time and labor.
Author Keywords: BIM; Geometric quality inspection; LiDAR; Prefabricated housing units
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses geometric quality inspection of prefabricated bathroom units including MEP elements (cylindrical pipes), but electrical work is mentioned only peripherally as part of the broader MEP category and the validation focuses on structural elements and plumbing pipes rather than electrical installations.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and applies digital technologies including BIM and LiDAR/laser scanning with point cloud processing algorithms (RANSAC, region growing, MSAC) for automated quality inspection.
relevance_notes: Paper is primarily about MEP (with plumbing pipes as the validated MEP elements) and structural prefabrication; electrical work is not specifically addressed despite MEP framing. Borderline for an electrical-focused review.
research_problem: Traditional manual quality inspection of prefabricated housing components using measurement tapes, straight edges, and slip gauges is labor-intensive, time-consuming, and error-prone, especially as the number of components grows. Despite previous studies on LiDAR-based inspection of individual precast elements, there is a lack of research on automated geometric quality inspection of entire prefabricated housing units that contain multiple structural and MEP elements together.
research_objective: To develop an automated geometric quality inspection technique for prefabricated housing units using BIM and LiDAR that inspects both structural and MEP elements.
specific_methods: RANSAC plane fitting; Region growing segmentation; Scan-BIM registration; MSAC cylinder fitting; Least squares fitting; Edge extraction from point clouds; Experimental validation against manual measurement
data_size: Two prefabricated bathroom units (PBUs), each approximately 2.5 m x 1.8 m x 2.4 m, scanned with 0.018 degree angular resolution
future_research_directions: Investigate inspection of entire PBU with multiple scanning locations; Address scanning of top and bottom of prefabricated units; Extend technique to PPVC units with larger sizes and more elements; Inspect more types of MEP elements beyond cylindrical pipes
value_proposition: Quality improvement; Automation of manual task; Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Quality control; Prefabrication feasibility
electrical_work_category: Prefabricated electrical assemblies; MEP coordination generally
key_findings: The proposed technique achieved averaged inspection discrepancies of 0.7 mm for structural elements (range 0.2 to 1.6 mm) and 0.9 mm for MEP elements (range 0.1 to 2.3 mm) compared to manual ground-truth measurements, well within the 6 mm tolerance specified by Singapore's CONQUAS standards. Inspection time per PBU was reduced from 30 minutes with two workers (manual) to 12 minutes with one worker (automated), demonstrating both accuracy and efficiency gains.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 0.7 mm average inspection discrepancy for structural elements and 0.9 mm for MEP elements; inspection time reduced from 30 min/2 workers to 12 min/1 worker per PBU
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Site engineers from prefabricators and contractors were interviewed to determine required scanned data accuracy and spatial resolution; experiments were conducted on PBUs from a Singapore prefabrication factory.
codes_addressed__codes_details: Construction Quality Assessment System (CONQUAS) stipulated by Singapore's Building and Construction Authority (BCA), used to define inspection checklist items and tolerance values for dimension, opening dimension/position, straightness, squareness, twist, and flatness.
industry_references__references_details: Singapore Building and Construction Authority (BCA) publications on Prefabricated Prefinished Volumetric Construction and Prefabricated Bathroom Unit good industry practices; Housing and Development Board (HDB) of Singapore; UK Buildoffsite organization.
limitations_acknowledged: Only a part of each PBU was scanned rather than the entire unit, and scanning the top and bottom is difficult; multi-station scanning of complete units would be more complex. Only PBUs were tested rather than larger PPVC units, and only cylindrical pipes were tested as MEP elements rather than a broader range of MEP types.

Author full names: Xuanfeng Xie, Jianliang Zhou, Xuehai Fu, Ruoyi Zhang, Hui Zhu, Quanxi Bao
Title: Automated rule checking for MEP systems based on BIM and KBMS
Abstract: Due to the growing complexity of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) designs and the rules that govern them, performing rule checks manually has become expensive. However, MEP-based rule checking has not received adequate attention compared to automated rule checking in other domains. Based on Knowledge Management and Building Information Modeling (BIM), an automated rule checking system integrated knowledge base management system (KBMS) for model information expansion, information extraction, system integrity checking, and element spacing checking was developed. MEP rules for automated rule checking were collected, optimized, and stored in the MEP knowledge base. The KBMS facilitates the management of MEP rules in the knowledge base. A Revit plug-in of MEP rule checking system was developed including functions of KBMS, Model Integrity Checking, Elements Space Checking, and Locating the non-compliant element in model view. This study integrated both KBMS and BIM technologies to achieve automated rule checking for MEP. This simplifies the process of rule checking of MEP systems in an automated manner.
Author Keywords: integrity checking; KBMS; MEP; rule checking; space checking
Funding Details: National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (71871116, 72171224); Humanities and Social Sciences Youth Foundation, Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China, (19YJAZH122)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) with electrical checking as one of three sub-tabs in the rule-checking system, though the case studies focus primarily on plumbing.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops a BIM-based rule checking system integrated with a knowledge base management system (KBMS), implemented as a Revit plug-in.
relevance_notes: Although the system supports electrical checking, the validation case studies focus on plumbing systems; electrical-specific application is not demonstrated.
research_problem: MEP designs and their governing rules have grown increasingly complex, making manual rule checking expensive and error-prone. MEP-based automated rule checking has received inadequate attention compared to other domains, and few studies have conducted MEP pipeline coordination based on a knowledge base. Existing BIM models lack the function-based information needed for rule checking, and there is no integrated system combining knowledge management with automated rule checking for MEP.
research_objective: To realize automated rule checking for MEP by integrating a knowledge base management system (KBMS) with a BIM-based rule checking system implemented as a Revit plug-in.
specific_methods: Production rule representation; Knowledge base design (relational database); Revit API secondary development; SQL querying; Recursion and looping algorithms for connectivity; Case study validation
data_size: One eight-story comprehensive office building plumbing model with 15 integrity errors, 15 slope-related property errors, and 10 element spacing errors manually set
future_research_directions: Automatic rule interpretation using NLP and ontology; Development of BIM standards for object standardization; Complex spatial relationship analysis and large-scale reasoning; Machine learning and text mining for rule extraction; Usability surveys and focus group evaluation of the system; Predicting designer actions from labeling errors
digital_technology: BIM; Cloud computing; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing; Robotics
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Automation of manual task; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; NEC or code compliance; Quality control
key_findings: The developed Revit plug-in successfully detected all manually-introduced errors in three case studies: 15 system integrity errors (unconnected devices in the indoor fire hydrant system), 15 slope property errors in plumbing pipes, and 10 element spacing errors (which generated 14 error messages because some element pairs violated multiple codes). The system automatically locates and highlights non-compliant components in the model view and provides a textual report citing the relevant code basis. The integration of KBMS allows users to query, edit, add, and delete MEP rules, supporting knowledge management alongside compliance checking.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Detected 100% of manually set errors (15 integrity, 15 property, 10 spacing) in case studies
research_sponsor: National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 72171224, 71871116) and Humanities and Social Sciences Foundation of China's Education Ministry (Grant 19YJAZH122)
industry_involvement: One co-author is affiliated with The Third Construction Co., Ltd. of China Construction Eighth Engineering Division, but no formal industry advisory panel, task force, or industry partnership for data provision is described.
codes_addressed__codes_details: Chinese standards including 'Standard for design delivery of building information modeling' (GB/T51301-2018) for MEP system classification, 'Standard for graphic expression of building information modeling' (JGJ/T 448-2018) for pipe naming conventions, and 'Standard for the distribution of water supply and drainage' (GB50015-2019) for pipe slope and spacing requirements.
limitations_acknowledged: The MEP knowledge base requires considerable manual effort to convert textual rules into computer-processable language due to the large number and varied expressions of relevant codes. The system cannot assess annotations when models are incorrectly labeled or have missing information, and preparing the model requires extensive domain expertise. Author full names: Shabtai Isaac, Maxim Shimanovich
Title: Automated scheduling and control of mechanical and electrical works with BIM
Abstract: Current planning tools provide insufficient support for the scheduling and control of Mechanical and Electrical works in construction projects. Consequently, installation activities often deviate from project plans. However, BIM data on the spatial layout of Mechanical and Electrical systems can be used for more detailed and accurate planning of their installation, and to identify points where work progress should be tracked. This paper presents a novel method for the automated scheduling and control of Mechanical and Electrical Works. A topological analysis of the precise position of components is used to define relationships between the installation tasks, and to identify the optimal schedule. The definition of specific control points supports effective progress tracking to ensure that tasks adhere in practice to this schedule.
Author Keywords: Automation; BIM; Mechanical and electrical works; Optimization; Scheduling
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses M&E (Mechanical and Electrical) systems installation broadly, with electrical and communication systems being one of five subcontractor scopes in the case study, but it treats electrical as part of the unified MEP category rather than focusing specifically on electrical work.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies BIM combined with topological analysis algorithms and optimization to automate scheduling and control of M&E installation works.
relevance_notes: Electrical is one of several MEP trades in the case study; paper's contribution applies to electrical contractors but not exclusively focused on them.
research_problem: Current planning tools provide insufficient support for the scheduling and control of Mechanical and Electrical (M&E) works in construction projects, causing installation activities to frequently deviate from project plans. Existing CPM-based scheduling methods cannot leverage automated tracking technologies because they assume constant work rates and only allow control at activity end-points. Previous BIM-based scheduling research has focused on structural and envelope components rather than the spatially complex M&E systems with dense layouts and multiple subcontractors working in close proximity.
research_objective: To develop an automated method that uses BIM spatial data to schedule and control M&E installation tasks through topological analysis, optimization, and definition of control points.
specific_methods: Topological analysis; Boolean spatial-temporal relationships; Clustering analysis; Location-based scheduling; Optimization algorithm (recursive exhaustive search); Case study validation; Semi-structured interviews with practitioners
data_size: Single-story public building with 90 m of corridors and 14 spaces; preliminary study with 9 practitioner interviews
future_research_directions: Practical application of the method during project execution; Integration with automated tracking technologies on site; Verify contribution to reducing clashes and improving productivity; Develop more flexible interactive method for dynamic project conditions; Explore design for installation to facilitate efficient installation; Support deployment of automated and robotic installation equipment
value_proposition: Schedule compression; Coordination or clash reduction; Automation of manual task
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Scheduling; Material handling or logistics
key_findings: The proposed method was applied to a public building case study and produced a 47-day schedule, a reduction of more than 35% from the original 75-day duration achieved with conventional planning. The reduction was attributed to enabling multiple crews to work simultaneously in close proximity without safety hazards by accounting for detailed spatial constraints, work paths, work areas, and temporary storage needs. The method also defines control points at interfaces of sub-processes to support real-time progress tracking.
contractor_role_impacted: Project manager; BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: More than 35% reduction in installation duration (47 days vs. 75 days) in case study
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Collaboration with Maimoni Engineering and Consulting (Israeli BIM consultancy), where the first author worked as a voluntary intern; preliminary study included interviews with 9 experienced practitioners.
limitations_acknowledged: The method has not yet been used in practice during the execution of a project, so additional research is required to confirm its suitability for enabling automated project tracking and to verify its actual contribution to reducing clashes, improving productivity, and reducing waste. The list of constraints addressed is not exhaustive, and the design was accepted as-is without considering modifications to facilitate easier installation.

Author full names: Dejiang Wang, Yuanhao Fang
Title: Automatic BIM reconstruction for existing building MEP systems from drawing recognition
Abstract: Aging buildings pose a significant concern for many large developed cities, and the operation and maintenance (O&M) of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems becomes critical. Building Information Modeling (BIM) facilitates efficient O&M for MEP. However, these numerous aging buildings were constructed without BIM, making BIM reconstruction a monumental undertaking. This research proposes an automatic approach for generating BIM based on 2D drawings. Semantic segmentation was utilized to identify MEP components in the drawings, trained on a custom-made MEP dataset, achieving an mIoU of 92.18%. Coordinates and dimensions of components were extracted through contour detection and bounding box detection, with pixel-level accuracy. To ensure that the generated components in BIM strictly adhere to the specifications outlined in the drawings, all model types were predefined in Revit by loading families, and an MEP component dictionary was built to match dimensions and model types. This research aims to automatically and efficiently generate BIM for MEP systems from 2D drawings, significantly reducing labor requirements and demonstrating broad application potential in the large-scale O&M of numerous aging buildings.
Author Keywords: building information modeling (BIM) generation; extraction of coordinates and dimensions; mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems; semantic segmentation; type matching
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly but the demonstrated components (ducts, AHX, VRF, exhaust fans) are primarily HVAC/mechanical; electrical systems are mentioned only as part of the MEP acronym without specific electrical component recognition.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies deep learning semantic segmentation (K-Net, DeepLabV3+, Segformer), computer vision, OCR, and BIM (Revit/Dynamo) to automate model reconstruction from 2D drawings.
relevance_notes: Although framed as MEP, the experimental validation focuses on HVAC components (ducts, air handlers, VRF units, exhaust fans) with no explicit electrical components like panels, conduit, or lighting; reviewers should decide if methodology transferability to electrical drawings justifies inclusion.
research_problem: Many aging buildings lack BIM models because they predate BIM adoption (widespread only after 2012) and even predate CAD, leaving only scanned paper drawings. Existing automatic BIM reconstruction methods from 2D drawings suffer from insufficient coordinate accuracy for MEP interdependencies and overlook equipment specifications, design modulus, and Z-axis heights when generating component dimensions from semantic segmentation masks.
research_objective: To develop an automatic approach that generates BIM models of MEP systems from 2D drawings using semantic segmentation combined with computer vision and a predefined component dictionary to ensure accurate coordinates and specification-compliant dimensions.
specific_methods: Semantic segmentation; Sliding window cropping and stitching; Gaussian-Laplacian contour detection; Minimum bounding box detection; OCR-based annotation recognition; Nearest neighbor matching; Coordinate transformation; mIoU evaluation
data_size: 3,900 augmented images (from 195 annotated source images) split 8:1:1 for training/validation/test; 187 components across 6 classes
future_research_directions: Expand dataset classes and component dictionary model types; Enhance recognition against complex drawing backgrounds; Automatically detect collisions and disconnections between components; Support VR and AR-based inspection using generated BIM
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Decision support; Cost reduction
pain_point_addressed: Commissioning or closeout; Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: K-Net achieved the best performance among five tested networks with mIoU of 92.18% and overall accuracy of 98.46% on the custom MEP dataset, while all non-lightweight networks exceeded 89% mIoU. Gaussian-Laplacian contour detection with minimum bounding boxes yielded pixel-level coordinate extraction (maximum error ~5 mm at 2.5 mm/pixel scale). The pixel-counting approach combined with the MEP component dictionary successfully matched components to their correct specification types, and Dynamo successfully generated the Revit model of ducts, equipment, and fittings for a floor.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 92.18% mIoU and 98.46% overall accuracy for MEP component semantic segmentation using K-Net
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: None reported; the study used a set of studio drawings conforming to national design specifications as the data source with no named industry partner.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that semantic segmentation mask boundaries do not align perfectly with actual component contours, with typical errors of 1-2 pixels leading to coordinate shifts. Image scale is critical (at 1 pixel = 2.5 mm, maximum error is ~5 mm), and relative errors between components could affect MEP model connectivity, so collision and connectivity detection should be performed on generated models.
