Author full names: Jinxin Hu, Quanxi Bao, Tuanjie Zhou, Kun Li, Liang Shang, Jicang Zhang, Xuehai Fu
Title: Automatic generation construction shop design model of the MEP hanger based on BIM
Abstract: Due to the growing complexity of mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) functions, the design, production and installation of hanger based on MEP has become low efficiency in traditional ways. Compared with the Computer-Aided Design (CAD) approach, the MEP hanger three-dimensional (3D) design approach has not received adequate attention in the process of engineering practice. Based on Building Information Modeling (BIM) and Revit secondary development technology, an automatic generation construction shop design model system of MEP hanger (MEP hanger-CDM) for the quick placement of hanger components, automatic grouping of hanger components and hanger component constructability checking was developed. By conducting investigations with the construction companies and construction sites in China, four basic forms of MEP hanger layouts were identified to accommodate different types of MEP layouts. In addition, the design requirements of the family of LOD 400 BIM models were summarized along with the analysis of user requirements. The results of the case studies indicate a 70% reduction in the manpower number on the MEP hanger shop design and an 85.9% reduction in the number of working hours. Moreover, the efficiency of the hanger construction model design and the accuracy of the output of material lists and shop drawings could be improved. Thus, the process of designing MEP hanger could be optimized in a visualized method.
Author Keywords: BIM; construction shop design model; MEP hanger; MEP hanger-CDM system
Funding Details: UK Research and Innovation, UKRI, (103680); UK Research and Innovation, UKRI; Humanities and Social Sciences Youth Foundation, Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China, (19YJAZH122); Humanities and Social Sciences Youth Foundation, Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China; National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (72171224); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC

research_problem: MEP hanger design, production, and installation has become inefficient using traditional 2D CAD methods, especially in complex spaces where MEP components are dense and 2D drawings cannot adequately express spatial relationships. Even with BIM software, manual creation of MEP hanger layout models is repetitive and inefficient, and existing tools lack support for automated placement, grouping, and constructability checking of LOD 400 hanger models. There is also a lack of unified design standards for high-precision component families needed for shop-level design.

future_research_directions: Extend system to handle uncommon MEP hanger forms such as steel-beam-based hangers; Automate output of shop drawings, material lists, and calculation sheets; Enable modeling operations directly in 3D view rather than section view; Apply deep learning and graph theory to improve modeling capabilities; Develop BIM Forward Design integration; Integrate BIM with blockchain for Level 3 BIM collaboration
digital_technology: BIM; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Labor productivity; Coordination or clash reduction
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Labor productivity; Prefabrication feasibility
key_findings: The MEP hanger-CDM system reduced manpower requirements by 70% (from 10 to 3 workers) and reduced total working time by 85.9% (from 18 days 10 minutes to 2.5 days 38 minutes 10 seconds) compared to the existing work process. Four basic forms of MEP hangers were identified that can satisfy 85% of MEP hanger design requirements alone or in combination. The system enabled quick placement, automatic grouping, and constructability checking of hanger components, and has been deployed in subway stations, parking lots, and underground markets in China.

research_sponsor: National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 72171224) and Humanities and Social Sciences Foundation of China's Education Ministry (Grant 19YJAZH122)

Author full names: Atul Khanzode, Martin Fischer, Deen Reed
Title: Benefits and lessons learned of implementing building virtual design and construction (VDC) technologies for coordination of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems on a large healthcare project
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Coordination of Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) systems is a huge challenge for many technical projects such as Healthcare projects, Bio-tech projects and projects in the area of Advanced Technology. The use of Building Information Modeling (BIM) or Virtual Design and Construction (VDC) tools and processes promises to address the challenges of the MEP coordination process. This case study presents the use of BIM / VDC tools and processes for the coordination of MEP systems on a $96.9M healthcare project in Northern California, USA. discuss the challenges project team members faced in implementing the BIM / VDC tools and processes for MEP coordination, the specific quantitative and qualitative benefits from the use of BIM / VDC tools and processes that each project team member recognized and the lessons that the project team learned by implementing BIM / VDC tools and processes for the coordination of MEP systems. Some of the challenges we discuss include the creation and organization of the MEP coordination process using BIM / VDC tools, creation of the guidelines for the most efficient use of BIM / VDC tools for the process of conflict identification and resolution between the MEP subcontractors, and aligning the contractual interests of the coordination team to meet the overall project schedule. Some of the benefits that the project team achieved by using the BIM /VDC tools and processes for the coordination of the MEP systems include labor savings ranging from 20 to 30 % for all the MEP subcontractors, 100% pre-fabrication for the plumbing contractor, only one recorded injury throughout the installation of MEP systems over a 250,000 square feet project area, less than 0.2% rework for the whole project for the mechanical subcontractor, zero conflicts in the field installation of the systems and only a handful of requests for information for the coordination of the MEP systems between contractors and the designers, and 6 months' savings on the schedule and about $9M savings in cost for the overall project. The lessons the team learned include the level and type of details team members need to include to achieve benefits from the use of BIM / VDC tools for the coordination of MEP systems.
Author Keywords: 3D CAD; 4D CAD; Building Information Modeling (BIM); Change Orders; Design Coordination; Electrical; Mechanical; MEP Coordination; Plumbing Systems (MEP Systems); Prefabrication; Productivity; RFI; Virtual Design and Construction (VDC)

Traditional 2D Sequential Composite Overlay Process (SCOP) leads to conflicts identified in the field, rework, RFIs, change orders, and reduced productivity. Prior research had proposed computer-aided methods but few project studies measured the real benefits of using BIM/VDC tools for MEP coordination on large complex projects.

key_findings: Use of BIM/VDC tools for MEP coordination yielded only 2 of 233 RFIs related to field conflicts, zero change orders due to field conflicts, and only one recordable injury over 203,448 work-hours. The mechanical contractor achieved 5-25% field labor productivity improvement and less than 0.2% rework, with 100% prefabrication of plumbing and medium/low pressure ductwork. The collaborative VDC approach saved approximately $9M and 6 months versus a traditional design-bid-build delivery, and the owner mandated VDC for all future projects in their $6 billion program.

Author full names: Brian C. Lines, Jake B. Smithwick
Title: Best practices for organizational change management within electrical contractors
Source title: International Journal of Construction Education and Research
Abstract: Few studies have investigated best practices for organizational change management (OCM) within the electrical contracting industry. This study had two research questions: first, to determine whether there is a relationship between electrical contractor usage of OCM practices and successful change adoption; second, to identify whether electrical contractors have recommendations for how to effectively implement OCM practices within their companies. The first research question was assessed via a national survey of 94 electrical contractors who had recently adopted an organizational change. The second question was explored through 11 in-depth interviews of electrical contractors who had implemented organizational changes such as prefabrication, management team realignment, new software, paperless mobile technology, payroll automation, and entering into a new market. Results showed OCM practices were associated with successful change adoption. OCM practices that had a direct association with successful change adoption included change agent effectiveness, a realistic timescale, providing change-related training, senior leadership commitment, measuring performance benchmarks, providing sufficient resources, and effective change message communication. Interviewees recommended best practices for executing these OCM practices. Contributions include being the first study to investigate OCM practices specifically within the electrical contracting industry and identification of how practitioners can implement OCM practices for their change initiatives.
Author Keywords: Electrical contractors; organizational change management; prefabrication
Funding Details: National Electrical Contractors Association; ELECTRI International
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper specifically focuses on electrical contractors (NECA members) and their organizational change initiatives.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: Digital technologies (paperless mobile tech, payroll automation, new software, BIM) are mentioned only as examples of organizational changes; the paper's focus is on change management practices, not on AI or digital technology itself.

research_problem: Despite numerous organizational change opportunities in the electrical contracting industry (such as prefabrication, BIM, IPD, and new technologies), prior research has focused on the technical aspects of these changes rather than the organizational change management (OCM) process. No prior studies have investigated which OCM practices are most effective for facilitating successful change adoption specifically within electrical contracting firms.

data_size: 94 survey responses and 11 in-depth executive interviews

value_proposition: Decision support; Other
pain_point_addressed: Organizational adoption of technology; Prefabrication feasibility
key_findings: All seven OCM practices were significantly and directly correlated with successful change adoption. Change agent effectiveness had the strongest correlation (rs = 0.633), followed by realistic timescale (rs = 0.551) and job-specific training (rs = 0.522). Interviews revealed that organizational change in electrical contractors typically requires about 6 years (range 3-10 years) across four phases: planning/preparation, pilot testing, expansion, and ongoing operations. Nearly half of interviewed contractors had to terminate resistant employees to successfully adopt the change.

Author full names: Rogelio Palomera-Arias, Rui Liu
Title: BIM laboratory exercises for a MEP systems course in a construction science and management program
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Recognizing the need of Construction Science and Management students to be proficient with the latest information technology developments in the architecture, engineering and construction (AEC) industry, the curriculum at the Construction Science and Management (CSM) program at the University of Texas at San Antonio was modified to include Building Information Modeling (BIM) courses. Starting with the 2014-2015 academic year BIM courses became part of the degree requirements. One of the courses requiring BIM is the Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) Systems course. This paper focuses on the BIM based laboratory exercises developed for the MEP Systems course, the specific topics covered, and the organization of the course as it was modified to accommodate the inclusion of BIM. The differences with previous offerings of the course without the BIM component are described in the paper. It also discusses the perceived benefits and disadvantages of using BIM as part of the teaching process, as well as the difficulties and drawbacks encountered while using the software. In addition to presenting the instructor experiences, the paper also discusses the results and observations stemming from student surveys and evaluations. The student surveys were used specifically to assess the effectiveness of the BIM exercise in presenting the MEP topics. Additionally, the surveys provided student opinions and suggestions on how to improve the BIM component of the course, whereas the course evaluations only provided general feedback on the instructor teaching and course delivery. Lastly, the paper discusses future additions, changes and improvements to the course laboratory BIM exercises, as well as other changes to the course organization and topics covered that might be to ease the inclusion of BIM into the classroom.
Author Keywords: BIM; Course development; Laboratory exercises; MEP systems
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper covers MEP systems education with electrical power and lighting as one of three system types addressed via dedicated BIM laboratory exercises.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally focuses on integrating Building Information Modeling (BIM) using Autodesk Revit into MEP coursework.

While other ACCE-accredited programs have introduced BIM courses, no published BIM laboratory exercises focusing specifically on MEP systems were available in the literature. The paper addresses how to design and integrate BIM-based laboratory exercises into an MEP systems course to develop practical modeling skills alongside traditional MEP knowledge.

electrical_work_category: Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance); Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Lighting and controls; MEP coordination generally
key_findings: Survey results showed that 70% of students agreed or strongly agreed that using BIM in the MEP course was a good idea, and 86% reported that BIM exercises were relevant to the construction management profession. Student-perceived BIM proficiency improved, with the share rating themselves as advanced or average rising from 19% before the course to 49% after. Students felt BIM helped them understand HVAC and plumbing/fire protection systems but was less effective for understanding electrical systems, where only 46% felt BIM enhanced their understanding. Identified challenges included the steep Revit learning curve, extensive workspace setup requirements, limitations of stock MEP families, and the inability to easily model branch-circuit conduits.

Author full names: Zhen-Zhong Hu, Pei-Long Tian, Sun-Wei Li, Jian-Ping Zhang
Title: BIM-based integrated delivery technologies for intelligent MEP management in the operation and maintenance phase
Abstract: Incomplete building information in delivery and the lack of compatible tools for Operation and Maintenance (O&M) have hindered the development of the intelligent management of Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) systems. In fact, the information related to the O&M management of the MEP system conventionally comes from the completion documents in the forms of hard copies or unstructured digital files, making it hard to search for useful information in the "sea" of documents and drawings. Therefore, digitalization of information is an urgent task to facilitate the intelligent management of the MEP system. As a project deliverable, the as-built information model shall not only contain geometrical information and necessary construction-related data, but also built-in information useful for the intelligent O&M management. In the present study, based on the Building Information Modeling/Model (BIM) technology, a set of solutions including the automatic establishment of the logic chain for MEP systems, an equipment grouping and labeling scheme and an algorithm to transform BIM information to GIS map model, is proposed to digitalize and integrate the MEP-related information into the as-built model. Subsequently, a cross-platform O&M management system is developed using the MEP-related information in the as-built model to run routine O&M tasks and to effectively response to MEP-related emergencies. The developed system is applied to aid the O&M management of MEP engineering in a real project, showing that the developed system facilitates the intelligent O&M management and guarantees the security of the MEP system and its subsystems.
Author Keywords: As-built; BIM; Facility management; MEP; Operation and maintenance
Funding Details: Stanford Engineering; National Natural Science Foundation of China, (51478249); National Natural Science Foundation of China; Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation, (Z151100002115054); Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation

As-built BIMs delivered to owners lack key MEP-related information such as logical relationships among components, equipment grouping/labeling schemes, and GIS-compatible data needed for facility management.

Author full names: Nanying Liang, Yu Pin Ang, Kaiyun Yeo, Xiao Wu, Yuan Xie, Yiyu Cai
Title: BIMBot for autonomous laser scanning in built environments
Abstract: Accurate and complete 3D point clouds are essential in creating as-built building information modeling (BIM) models, although there are challenges in automating the process for 3D point cloud creation in complex environments. In this paper, an autonomous scanning system named BIMBot is introduced, which integrates advanced light detection and ranging (LiDAR) technology with robotics to create 3D point clouds. Using our specially developed algorithmic pipeline for point cloud processing, iterative registration refinement, and next best view (NBV) calculation, this system facilitates an efficient, accurate, and fully autonomous scanning process. The BIMBot's performance was validated using a case study in a campus laboratory, featuring complex structural and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) elements. The experimental results showed that the autonomous scanning system produced 3D point cloud mappings in fewer scans than the manual method while maintaining comparable detail and accuracy, demonstrating its potential for wider application in complex built environments.
Author Keywords: 3D point cloud creation; autoscanning system; next best view
Funding Details: RIE2020 Industry Alignment Fund; Surbana Jurong, SJ

Existing autonomous solutions either require human-guided initial walkthroughs, produce low-quality SLAM-based point clouds unsuitable for BIM reconstruction, or lack flexibility in scanning routes. There is a need for a fully autonomous TLS-based scanning system that minimizes human intervention while maintaining high point cloud quality.

The autonomous system demonstrated comparable detail and accuracy to manual scanning, with visual inspection showing slightly sharper contours of pipes and ducts due to fewer outliers in registration.

Author full names: Jun Wang, Xiangyu Wang, Wenchi Shou, Heap-Yih Chong, Jun Guo
Title: Building information modeling-based integration of MEP layout designs and constructability
Abstract: Building information modeling (BIM) has demonstrated its advantages in improving a mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) layout in the design stage. Unfortunately, BIM applications normally stop prior to the construction stage. The MEP layout design may no longer fit properly during the installation process when there are as-built deviations between structures and the MEP layout. The purpose of this paper is to develop a practical BIM framework for integrating the MEP layout from preliminary design to construction stage. In this framework, BIM models were categorized into five levels of details: 3D MEP preliminary design model, 3D MEP detailed design model, 3D MEP construction design model, MEP construction model and MEP prefabrication model. Four types of coordination steps have been formulated to solve the design and constructability issues. A case study was adopted to validate the practical BIM framework. The results are very encouraging and demonstrate the significant value of the proposed BIM approach. In addition, one issue of collision detection is discussed because 78% collisions are ineffective when using the BIM tools for collision detection; and a significant number of effective collisions (102 collisions) are still detected by the designers and contractors manually. Ultimately, the research renders a practical insight into improving the MEP design and constructability using the developed BIM framework.
Author Keywords: BIM; Constructability; Design; Integration; Layout; MEP

There is no integrated process spanning preliminary design through construction that addresses constructability and as-built deviations, limiting the realization of BIM benefits for MEP coordination.

Of 2,257 automated clash detections, 78% were ineffective, and an additional 102 effective collisions were detected manually by designers and contractors, indicating limitations in current automated clash detection.

Author full names: Tadeusz Daszczyński, Michał Ostapowski, Aleksander Szerner
Title: Clash cost analysis in electrical installations based on BIM technologies
Abstract: One of the core features of delivering a Building Information Modelling (BIM) project is design coordination and clash detection. In the traditional way of conducting investments, the design process consists in the separate work of individual industry specialists on an individual part of the project and checking its compliance with coordination checkpoints. Building information modelling/management is emerging as an innovative way to virtually design and manage projects. Clashes are one of the most problematic elements in construction. They can interfere in the decision-making process by generating unexpected costs of additional materials, work and actions. The purpose of the following paper is to analyze the impact of BIM on limiting cost of clashes and also to check how clashes affect the unforeseen costs of a construction investment and how the BIM methodology can help to reduce them, especially in electrical installations. Basic construction collisions with algorithms of their solutions were presented. The possible financial benefits associated with the use of the BIM approach have been described using mathematical relationships.
Author Keywords: AEC; BIM; Clash analysis; Cost analysis; Electrical installations
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper explicitly frames clash cost analysis around electrical installations, though much of the analytical content applies to MEP coordination broadly with electrical as a featured example.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally addresses BIM methodology and digital twin concepts as the primary technology for clash detection and cost analysis.
relevance_notes: Paper is largely conceptual/theoretical with formula-based cost modeling rather than empirical validation; treats electrical as a focal example within MEP coordination.
research_problem: Clashes between building systems generate unexpected costs of materials, labor, and rework, and are particularly problematic in electrical installations where cable routing must coordinate with HVAC and plumbing. Although BIM is widely promoted for clash detection, the literature lacks clear, justified cost-benefit analysis quantifying how BIM reduces clash-related costs, especially for electrical installations. Investors and users need evidence-based justification for the additional time and budget required to implement BIM.
research_objective: To analyze how BIM limits the cost of clashes and how the BIM methodology can reduce unforeseen construction investment costs, particularly in electrical installations.
specific_methods: Literature review; Workflow diagramming; Mathematical cost modeling; Clash categorization (minor/medium/major)
data_size: Not reported
future_research_directions: Empirical validation of the theoretical cost models; Compare traditional 2D technology with BIM on real projects; Apply analysis to underground garage installation models; Extend economic-technical analysis of construction collision costs
value_proposition: Cost reduction; Coordination or clash reduction; Schedule compression
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery; RFI or change order management
electrical_work_category: MEP coordination generally; Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices)
key_findings: The authors develop workflow diagrams and cost equations for three clash categories (minor, medium, major) showing that without BIM, clash costs include searching for solutions, consultations, dismantling, delivery delays, and penalties, while with BIM these costs collapse to a single component (material plus labor for implementation). They cite case examples including the Leeds Arena project (~£350,000 savings) and Holder Construction's Aquarium Hilton Garden Inn (~$84,500 net savings, 590 clashes detected, $800,000 total savings; average BIM ROI of 634%). They conclude BIM eliminates expenses related to demolition, design changes, and financial penalties by relocating clash resolution to the pre-construction stage.

Author full names: Anderson O. Akponeware, Zulfikar A. Adamu
Title: Clash detection or clash avoidance? An investigation into coordination problems in 3D BIM
Abstract: Early collaboration is crucial if the final design is to be clash-free, and automation processes through Building Information Modelling (BIM) have the capacity to reduce clashes through 3D design coordination. Yet, current design practices are still dependent on clash detection and contemporary literature presents several reasons for this. This paper investigates the root causes of clashes with respect to achieving "clash avoidance" as proposed in PAS 1192-2 design phase specifications for BIM in the UK. Empirical data from BIM coordinators around the world was collected and analyzed using explanatory sequential mixed-methods. It was found that: (i) isolated working was the prime cause of high occurrences of clashes linked to mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) 3D BIM systems; (ii) there is a link between non-BIM specific training (or the professional qualifications) of design practitioners with the high incidence of clashes; and (iii) the current structure of cloud-based common data environments (CDEs) does not facilitate clash avoidance and in fact, encourages isolated working in the early design stage by creating "digital information silos". A conceptual framework for an open work in progress (OWIP) has been proposed to address this problem. These findings point to the need for more transparency during collaboration through CDE where designers from multidisciplinary backgrounds can engage in concurrent co-creation. This transparent and inclusive process could have consequences on how future architectural, engineering and construction (AEC) professionals are trained.;
Author Keywords: Clash avoidance; Clash detection; Co-creation; Common data environment; Design coordination; Mep coordination
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination broadly (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) with electrical as one of several systems involved in clash detection, but does not focus specifically on electrical contractors or electrical-only work.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally engages with BIM, common data environments (CDEs), and 3D coordination technology, proposing a conceptual framework for an Open Work in Progress (OWIP) within cloud-based CDEs.
relevance_notes: Electrical work is treated as part of the broader MEP coordination problem rather than as a primary focus; useful for the review's coverage of BIM-based coordination affecting electrical contractors.
research_problem: Despite PAS 1192-2 specifying clash avoidance as a proactive measure, current BIM design practice remains dependent on reactive clash detection, with high incidences of MEP-related clashes. The root causes of clashes and the structural limitations of common data environments (CDEs) that prevent clash avoidance have not been empirically investigated. Specifically, it is unclear whether isolated working, designer training, and the WIP structure of CDEs inhibit true co-creation during early design.

data_size: 116 questionnaire responses and 6 semi-structured interviews
future_research_directions: Validate the proposed Open Work in Progress (OWIP) framework empirically; Investigate cloud-based co-creation capabilities for design coordination; Examine implications of OWIP on AEC professional training; Explore data security and IPR issues in shared design workspaces; Study how broadband and interoperability affect cloud-based co-creation

pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Organizational adoption of technology; VDC or BIM cost recovery
key_findings: Working in isolation was the most cited cause of clashes (mean 0.72), with a statistically significant positive correlation between isolated working and MEP-related (Structural/HVAC) clashes. A significant negative correlation was found between BIM-specific qualifications and professional design errors, indicating that less BIM-trained designers commit more errors. The current WIP structure of CDEs was found to encourage 'digital information silos' and over-the-wall collaboration, prompting the authors to propose an Open Work in Progress (OWIP) framework that merges WIP and SHARED containers to enable concurrent co-creation.

Author full names: Duygu Utkucu, Huaquan Ying, Zijian Wang, Rafael Sacks
Title: Classification of architectural and MEP BIM objects for building performance evaluation
Abstract: Numerous factors in current workflows reduce the completeness, accuracy, and reliability of the information exchanged between Building Information Modelling (BIM) authoring tools and building performance simulation and analysis software. Automated classification of BIM objects could ameliorate most of the interoperability problems. However, architectural, structural, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP), and other building objects have a wide range of shapes, features, and properties, which makes identifying them reliably and accurately with any single AI tool difficult. This study explored methods and techniques for automated classification of architectural and MEP BIM objects that can significantly reduce or eliminate the manual effort required to correct data exchange and interoperability issues in pre-processing building models for performance evaluation. A series of ensemble models using a stacking mechanism of four classification tools (semantic keyword search, rule-based inferencing, machine learning using object geometry features, and deep learning using visual shape features) were implemented and tested. A BIM object dataset with 3,410 instances belonging to 40 classes was compiled for training and testing the models. The dataset contains diverse architecture and MEP objects with high-quality selected instances and is available for public use. We found that an ensemble learning approach that exploits the various types of information available in instance models may exhibit superior object classification performance than any individual tool. The ensemble model applying all four tools achieved 91.0 % accuracy (F1 score 0.88), suggesting that automated classification using ensemble learning is an effective strategy.
Author Keywords: Building information model; Building object classification; Building performance evaluation; Ensemble learning; Semantic enrichment
Funding Details: Horizon 2020 Framework Programme, H2020; Horizon 2020; H2020 Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, MSCA, (860555); H2020 Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, MSCA
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP BIM object classification including a substantial set of electrical objects (sockets, switches, light fixtures, sensors, alarms, cable carriers, thermostats) but treats electrical as one of several MEP systems rather than the primary focus.

State-of-the-art automated BIM object classification methods typically use a single AI tool with a limited subset of features, which cannot reliably handle the wide range of shapes, properties, and relationships of architectural and MEP objects needed for performance analysis. There is also a lack of fine-grained, openly available BIM object datasets covering MEP performance-relevant classes.

Author full names: Siyu Chen, Anthony Lau, Timson Yeung, Kim Nyberg, Rafael Sacks
Title: Comparing concurrent and sequential practices in cloud-BIM interdisciplinary collaborative design: an experimental study
Abstract: Effective interdisciplinary collaboration is crucial for success in building design, yet current sequential practices often hamper collaboration and lead to suboptimal outcomes. This study examines interdisciplinary collaboration during the detailed design phase within a Cloud-based Building Information Modeling (Cloud-based BIM) environment, aiming to identify effective practices and offer improvement recommendations. Following two initial experiments to refine the settings, five experiments involving architects, structural, and Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) designers compared concurrent and sequential collaborative design practices in terms of project efficiency, quality, and team dynamics. Results show that concurrent practices enable earlier issue identification, greater interaction, and faster resolution, but also lead to more clashes due to increased issue discovery. Problem identification, solution development, and execution behaviors are key contributors to the effectiveness of concurrent design. The study suggests that Cloud-based BIM platforms with real-time communication can support concurrent workflow and collaborative behaviors, improving design efficiency and quality. These findings may extend to other interdisciplinary design environments reliant on collaborative workflows.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modeling (BIM); collaborative design; concurrent collaboration; interdisciplinary design collaboration

While Cloud-based BIM and Concurrent Engineering principles promise improvements, empirical evidence on how concurrent versus sequential practices actually affect design efficiency, quality, and team dynamics during the detailed design phase is lacking. Prior work has focused on tool development or interviews rather than controlled comparison of collaborative practices.

key_findings: Concurrent practices (CP) groups identified design issues earlier (Weeks 1-2 vs Week 3 for sequential) and resolved individual issues faster (4.69 vs 5.91 min/issue), while spending 65.7% of meeting time on issue discussion versus 47.7% for sequential. CP groups identified more issues (avg 31 vs 16) and more clashes (avg 21 vs 12), and had greater team interaction (58 vs 39 questions/session). Overall design quality was similar (CP 74% vs SP 73%), suggesting that effectiveness of CP depends on collaborative behaviors such as proactive problem identification and creative brainstorming, not the workflow alone.

Author full names: M. Fikret Ercan, Ricky Ben Wang
Title: Computer vision-based inspection system for worker training in build and construction industry
Abstract: Recently computer vision has been applied in various fields of engineering successfully ranging from manufacturing to autonomous cars. A key player in this development is the achievements of the latest object detection and classification architectures. In this study, we utilized computer vision and the latest object detection techniques for an automated assessment system. It is developed to reduce the person-hours involved in worker training assessment. In our local building and construction industry, workers are required to be certificated for their technical skills in order to qualify working in this industry. For the qualification, they are required to go through a training and assessment process. During the assessment, trainees implement an assembly such as electrical wiring and wall-trunking by referring to technical drawings provided. Trainees' work quality and correctness are then examined by a team of experts manually and visually, which is a time-consuming process. The system described in this paper aims to automate the assessment process to reduce the significant person-hours required during the assessment. We employed computer vision techniques to measure the dimensions, orientation, and position of the wall assembly produced hence speeding up the assessment process. A number of key parts and components are analyzed and their discrepancies from the technical drawing are reported as the assessment result. The performance of the developed system depends on the accurate detection of the wall assembly objects and their corner points. Corner points are used as reference points for the measurements, considering the shape of objects, in this particular application. However, conventional corner detection algorithms are founded upon pixel-based operations and they return many redundant or false corner points. In this study, we employed a hybrid approach using deep learning and conventional corner detection algorithms. Deep learning is employed to detect the whereabouts of objects as well as their reference corner points in the image. We then perform a search within these locations for potential corner points returned from the conventional corner detector algorithm. This approach resulted in highly accurate detection of reference points for measurements and evaluation of the assembly.
Author Keywords: computer vision; corner detection; deep learning; quality construction
Funding Details: Singapore Polytechnic, (03-11000-36-J723)

The work quality is then examined manually and visually by a team of experts, which is an arduous and time-consuming process requiring significant person-hours. Additionally, conventional corner detection algorithms used in computer vision are pixel-based and return many redundant or false corner points, making accurate automated measurement difficult in less-controlled environments.

key_findings: The hybrid approach combining Minimum Eigen Value corner detection with YOLOv2 deep learning (ResNet-50 backbone) achieved IoU scores of 0.933 for Assembly A and 0.940 for Assembly B in detecting wall assemblies. Corner detection accuracy averaged 92.2% across 20 test images. Under good lighting conditions, geometric measurement errors were generally below 1% for most edges, with a 0.2% average error for the critical protruding angle measurements in Assembly B, demonstrating the system can replace manual visual assessment.

Author full names: Zhen-Zhong Hu, Jian-Ping Zhang, Fang-Qiang Yu, Pei-Long Tian, Xue-Song Xiang
Title: Construction and facility management of large MEP projects using a multi-Scale building information model
Abstract: Several challenges have been found in the current applications of building information modelling/model (BIM) technology in large-scale mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) projects, such as the huge modelling workloads of MEP models and details, untapped potential in supporting cooperative construction management with multiple participants and insufficient functions for intelligent facility management. This paper proposes a multi-scale solution to address the insufficiencies of the current applications in the construction and facility management of MEP projects. Particularly, a practical multi-scale BIM consisting of several macro-, micro- and schematic-scale information models is described in detail with the required information of the MEP components according to the schema of industrial foundation classes. Based on this model, the paper presents a BIM-based construction management system to provide virtual construction scenes with appropriate scales for various participants to communicate and cooperate, as well as a BIM-based facility management system to share information delivered from previous phases and improve the efficiency and safety of MEP management during the operation and maintenance period. The application in a real-world airport terminal illustrates that the proposed model and two systems can support collaborative construction management and facility management with multi-scale functionalities among participants. This paper proposes a series of feasible models and techniques to promote BIM application in large MEP projects.
Author Keywords: BIM; Construction management; Facility management; MEP; Multi-scale
Funding Details: National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (51278274, 51478249); National High-tech Research and Development Program, (2013AA041307); Beijing Science and Technology Planning Project

The 4D-BIM_MEP reduced check-in zone construction duration from 40-50 days to 30 days, reduced integrated MEP box installation by 6 days each, cut daily coordination meeting time from 2 hours to about 1 hour every two days, avoided approximately 85% of schedule conflicts, and saved roughly 2 million in cost. Author full names: Xuefeng Zhao, Junbo Li, Qiantai Yang, Xiongtao Fan, Shengyao Liu, Langcheng Cao, Yibing Wang, Zelin Zhang
Title: Corridor MEP pipeline integration and hanger-layout optimization using BIM-based genetic partitioning and performance analysis
Abstract: In recent years, with the increasing demands for architectural design, the complexity of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems has also increased, making the modular construction of integrated MEP systems a focal point of research in the industry. However, traditional two-dimensional (2D) design methods have numerous limitations in the modular design of MEP systems, making it difficult to achieve effective system integration and coordination. This often results in inefficiencies, information inconsistencies, and frequent design conflicts. To address these issues, this study explored how to leverage building information modeling (BIM) technology to improve the efficiency of MEP system integration, modular division, and optimization. Based on the BIM technology, an automated system for MEP modular division and optimization was developed. By utilizing high level of integration and visualization capabilities of BIM, information from various disciplines can be integrated into a single three-dimensional (3D) model, enabling standardized module division, automated optimization, and the design of internal supports and hangers. The results of the study indicate that the proposed genetic algorithm-based MEP modular partitioning method significantly improves the efficiency of pipeline automation partitioning, achieving an approximately 45%–65% increase in partitioning efficiency compared to traditional methods. In addition, the proposed method successfully avoids conflicts with connector locations and further demonstrates the broad applicability of BIM technology in the design and modular construction of complex building MEP systems.
Author Keywords: automated optimization system; building information modeling; integrated MEP systems; modular design; secondary development technology
Funding Details: Second Prize for Beijing Science and Technology Award; Architectural Society of China; School-Enterprise Cooperation Fund, (40004016202304)

Furthermore, BIM-based MEP work has emphasized clash detection but insufficiently addresses automated optimization for modular partitioning, hanger selection, and layout, limiting overall modular construction efficiency and accuracy.

Author full names: Ajibade A. Aibinu, Dharma Dassanayake, Toong-Khuan Chan, Ram Thangaraj
Title: Cost estimation for electric light and power elements during building design: A neural network approach
Abstract: Purpose: The study reported in this paper proposed the use of artificial neural networks (ANN) as viable alternative to regression for predicting the cost of building services elements at the early stage of design. The purpose of this paper is to develop, test and validate ANN models for predicting the costs of electrical services components. Design/Methodology/Approach: The research is based on data mining of over 200 building projects in the office of a medium size electrical contractor. Of the over 200 projects examined, 71 usable data were found and used for the ANN modeling. Regression models were also explored using IBM Statistical Package for Social Sciences Statistics Software 21, for the purpose of comparison with the ANN models. Findings: The findings show that the cost forecasting models based on ANN algorithm are more viable alternative to regression models for predicting the costs of light wiring, power wiring and cable pathways. The ANN prediction errors achieved are 6.4, 4.5 and 4.5 per cent for the three models developed whereas the regression models were insignificant. They did not fit any of the known regression distributions. Practical implications: The validated ANN models were converted to a desktop application (user interface) package - "Intelligent Estimator." The application is important because it can be used by construction professionals to reliably and quickly forecast the costs of power wiring, light wiring and cable pathways using building variables that are readily available or measurable during design stage, i.e. fully enclosed covered area, unenclosed covered area, internal perimeter length and number of floors. Originality/value: Previous studies have concluded that the methods of estimating the budget for building structure and fabric work are inappropriate for use with mechanical and electrical services. Thus, this study is unique because it applied the ANN modeling technique, for the first time, to cost modeling of electrical services components for building using real world data. The analysis shows that ANN is a better alternative to regression models for predicting cost of services elements because the relationship between cost and the cost drivers are non-linear and distribution types are unknown.
Author Keywords: Cost estimates; Estimating; Modelling; Multiple regression analysis; Neural net; Prediction

Prior literature concluded that methods used to estimate building structure and fabric costs are inappropriate for mechanical and electrical services. Existing parametric (regression) models assume linear relationships and normally distributed data, which do not match the subtle, non-linear, unknown distributions of electrical services cost data.

Author full names: Hongzhe Yue, Qian Wang, Yangzhi Yan, Guanying Huang
Title: Deep learning-based point cloud completion for MEP components
Abstract: Point clouds are increasingly leveraged for as-built model reconstruction of facilities. However, point clouds of Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) systems often experience extensive occlusions, which heavily affect the performance of model reconstruction. To address this challenge, this paper explores deep learning (DL)-based point cloud completion algorithms to complete occluded MEP point clouds. Due to the limited availability of datasets, parametric BIM modeling and occlusion simulation are used to generate synthetic point cloud datasets of MEP components. Based on generated datasets, the effectiveness of five different DL algorithms and five distinct training strategies for point cloud completion are investigated. The results indicate that: (1) The PoinTr model with a pre-training strategy achieved the best Chamfer Distance (CD) and F-score, demonstrating effective completion even with 75 % missing point clouds. 2) Applying the proposed point cloud completion method to three practical tasks further demonstrates the algorithm's applicability.
Author Keywords: Deep learning; MEP; Occlusion simulation; Point cloud completion
Funding Details: ProtoTech Solutions; National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKRDPC, (2023YFC3804300); National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKRDPC; Start-up Research Fund of Southeast University, (RF1028623126); Science and Technology Planning Project of Jiangsu Province of China, (BZ2024058); SEU Innovation Capability Enhancement Plan for Doctoral Students, (CXJH_SEU 25101)

Traditional point cloud completion methods rely on manual feature fitting and only work for regular shapes, failing on complex MEP components. Additionally, there is a lack of point cloud completion datasets specifically tailored for MEP components, and it is unclear which deep learning models and training strategies improve completion accuracy in this domain.

Joint training strategies outperformed individual training, and joint training with diverse samples reduced CD-L2 by approximately 82% on unseen components. limitations_acknowledged: The method has not been validated in large-scale, real-world MEP projects and was tested primarily in controlled environments. The synthetic datasets and angle occlusion simulation may not fully capture the variability of real-world occlusions, noise, and unpredictable conditions. The completion quality depends on whether similar component categories were included in pre-training, with poorer performance on unseen categories.

Author full names: Tharindu Samarasinghe, Tharaka Gunawardena, Priyan Mendis, Massoud Sofi, Lu Aye
Title: Dependency structure matrix and hierarchical clustering based algorithm for optimum module identification in MEP systems
Abstract: Modular prefabrication of mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) systems has become more prevalent during the last decade with the growth of the prefabricated construction industry. However, it is currently accomplished only for smaller systems where integrated packaged units are applied in heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) and other building services installations. The term 'optimum modularity' is rarely accurately used in the industry due to lack of an efficient modularisation method. In this investigation, an automated efficient modularisation algorithm has been developed using an approach which combines fuzzy logic, Dependency Structure Matrix (DSM) and Hierarchical Clustering (HC) methods. The developed algorithm achieves minimum total installation cost by identifying the optimum number modules and module division points based on assembly cost and the handling cost of each module. It is shown that the optimum modularity for a system is highly dependent on the module dimensions and the module division point.
Author Keywords: Building information modelling; Hierarchical Clustering; MEP plantrooms; Modular prefabrication; Optimum modularity
Funding Details: Australian Research Council, ARC, (IC150100023)

research_problem: Modular prefabrication of MEP systems is currently limited to small packaged units, and there is no efficient modularisation method to identify the optimum number of modules and module division points when plants must be located within a building. The industry relies on heuristic, experience-based decisions, and existing modularisation literature does not adequately address onsite handling constraints and assembly cost trade-offs for MEP central plants.

key_findings: The algorithm successfully identified an optimum module configuration of six modules for a 22-component chilled water plant section, consistent with the design engineers' practical solution. Single linkage and Centroid linkage methods produced the most practical clustering results, while other linkage methods (Complete, Average, Ward, Weighted) produced less practical results with 14 modules. The study demonstrated that minimum number of modules does not necessarily yield minimum installation cost; an optimum modularity point exists that balances assembly cost and handling cost, and this is highly dependent on module dimensions and division points.

Author full names: Jae Hyuk Park, Ghang Lee
Title: Design coordination strategies in a 2D and BIM mixed-project environment: social dynamics and productivity
Abstract: Despite the rapid spread of building information modelling (BIM), the majority of BIM projects are still conducted in a mixed-project environment: two-dimensional (2D) drawing and BIM. The design-coordination productivity and information-exchange patterns are analysed for a unique case where two towers, A and B, of a hospital project deployed two different design coordination strategies in a 2D and BIM mixed-construction environment. The tower A strategy coordinated designs using drawings as the main source of information and confirmed them using BIM (BIM-assisted coordination), whereas the tower B strategy coordinated designs using BIM and confirmed them using drawings (BIM-led coordination). The coordination productivity was 228% faster for tower B than for tower A. The frequency of design changes was much lower for tower B (0.42 times/drawing) than for tower A (2.13 times/drawing). As the result, the design coordination for tower A was delayed by 9.3 months, whereas tower B was completed rapidly and without any delay. A social network analysis revealed that the BIM-led coordination was supported by the relatively even distribution of information, the reduced control of an mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) engineer over a project, and higher accessibility to the information for every project participant.
Author Keywords: building information modelling (BIM); collaboration; data exchange; design management; productivity; social networks
Funding Details: Architecture & Urban Development Research Program; Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport, MOLIT

Author full names: Yasmín Hernández, Miguel Pérez-Ramírez, Ramón Zatarain-Cabada, Lucía Barrón-Estrada, Giner Alor-Hernández
Title: Designing empathetic animated agents for a B-learning training environment within the electrical domain
Abstract: Electrical tests involve high risk; therefore utility companies require highly qualified electricians and efficient training. Recently, training for electrical tests has been supported by virtual reality systems; nonetheless, these training systems are not yet adaptive. We propose a b-learning model to support adaptive and distance training. The adaptation is based on a representation of the knowledge and affect of the trainee. One of the main components is an animated pedagogical agent who guides trainees and provides instruction. The agent deploys different facial expressions conveying emotion and empathy to trainees. We incorporated these constructs into the learning model aiming to achieve credibility, social interaction, and user engagement, and thus improve learning. To develop the agent, we included some of the basic characteristics of electricians: work uniform and safety gear, such as helmets, gloves, and glasses, among other features. We believe that by representing the tutor as an electrician, the training environment will be better accepted by operators. The focus of this paper is a study that we conducted to gather knowledge about the expressivity and appearance of the animated agent. The participants evaluated the facial expressions of the agent and stated their point of view regarding the incorporation of the animated agent into the training system. The gathered knowledge is being used to enhance the animated agent before completing its integration to the training environment.
Author Keywords: Affective student model; Blended learning; Electrical tests; Empathetic agents; Virtual reality

Existing virtual reality training systems support traditional instruction but are not adaptive to trainees' knowledge or emotional states and depend on fixed course schedules. There is a need for blended, adaptive, intelligent training that recognizes trainee affect and provides empathetic guidance.

Author full names: Yongqi Wang, Limao Zhang, Hongbo Yu, Robert L.K. Tiong
Title: Detecting logical relationships in mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems with BIM using graph matching
Abstract: The increasingly complex design has gained difficulty in conducting the rule compliance checking for the Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) system in the design phase. Useful rule-checking systems could contribute to a quicker project delivery time. Currently, an efficient method for checking the logical relationship is still lacking. This study aims to propose an MEP rule checking framework using the subgraph matching technology. First, the MEP components in the BIM model are extracted by utilizing the application programming interface (API), and a graph database is established with point-based and curve-based instances being nodes and relationships, respectively. Second, the graph database is simplified to increase the speed of graph matching. Third, the rules, which regulate how the MEP components should be connected, are represented by a knowledge graph. Finally, rule checking is achieved by comparing the graph database against the knowledge graph, and the critical path in a sub-system is detected by calculating the betweenness centrality. A case study with a rail station is used to evaluate the approach where the overall model checking and rule checking are conducted on the original and simplified graph databases sequentially. The results show that the proposed approach could achieve the rule compliance checking at a high speed, and 6 unconnected instances along with 155 problematic pipe fittings have been found. Besides, the critical path for the selected ACS system is from the water-cooled chiller to the condenser water pump. The proposed framework could help in the overall model checking and rule checking process, improving the efficiency of BIM engineers. This research demonstrates that converting a BIM model into a graph database can benefit conventional BIM analysis methods by incorporating advanced technologies (e.g., artificial intelligence) to enable a more flexible and accurate MEP design process.
Author Keywords: Critical path; Graph database; Graph matching; Logical relationships; Rule compliance checking
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly, with electrical components (cable trays, conduits, wires, electrical equipment, lighting) treated as part of the unified MEP scope but the case study focuses on an air-conditioning system rather than electrical specifically.

While topological relationships between MEP components can be visually detected, logical relationships within sub-systems are not explicitly available to users even with BIM. Existing IFC-based extraction methods suffer from information loss and require deep schema knowledge, and an efficient method for checking logical relationships is still lacking.

Graph simplification reduced nodes by 53.6% and relationships by 50.8%, decreasing total processing time by 12.8% (from 210.05s to 183.14s) while rule checking time alone was reduced by over 90% (from 70.22s to 1.42s). Betweenness centrality analysis identified the water-cooled chiller to condenser water pump path as the critical path in the ACS sub-system, with the WCC having the highest betweenness centrality.

Author full names: Tomohiro Mizoguchi
Title: Detection of building equipment from mobile laser scanning point clouds using reflection intensity correction for detailed BIM generation †
Abstract: Highlights: What are the main findings? Accurate automatic extraction of building equipment using corrected reflectance intensity from MLS point clouds Capable of detecting even small and flat objects with almost no omission What is the implication of the main finding? Use of corrected laser intensity is effective for detailed BIM reconstruction from point clouds including building equipment The Building Information Model (BIM) has been increasingly adopted for building maintenance and management. For existing buildings lacking prior digital models, a BIM is often generated from 3D scanned point clouds. In recent years, the automatic construction of simple BIMs comprising major structural elements, such as floors, walls, ceilings, and columns, has become feasible. However, the automated generation of detailed BIMs that incorporate building equipment, such as electrical installations and safety systems, remains a significant challenge, despite their essential role in facility maintenance. This process not only enriches the information content of the BIM but also provides a foundation for evaluating building safety and hazard levels, as well as for supporting evacuation planning and disaster-preparedness simulations. Such equipment is typically attached to ceilings or walls and is difficult to detect due to its small surface area and thin geometric profile. This paper proposes a method for detecting building equipment based on laser reflection intensity, with the objective of facilitating the automatic construction of detailed BIMs from point clouds acquired by mobile laser scanners (MLSs). The proposed approach first corrects the reflection intensity by eliminating the effects of distance and incidence angle using polynomial approximation, thereby normalizing the intensity values for surfaces composed of identical materials. Given that the corrected intensity approximately follows a normal distribution, outliers are extracted as candidate points for building equipment via thresholding. Subsequently, the point cloud is converted into a 2D image representation, and equipment regions are extracted using morphological operations and connected component labeling. Experiments conducted on point clouds of building ceilings and walls demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a high detection accuracy for various types of building equipment.
Author Keywords: 3D modeling; building equipment; building information model; laser scanning; point cloud
Funding Details: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science, KAKENHI, (JP24K08978); Japan Society for the Promotion of Science, KAKENHI
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses detection of building equipment including electrical fixtures (lights, speakers, switches, smoke detectors) for BIM generation, but treats them as one of many ceiling/wall objects rather than focusing on electrical construction work or contractors.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies laser scanning, point cloud processing, and BIM generation technologies, which are core digital technologies in the review's scope.
relevance_notes: Paper is methodologically focused on point cloud processing for BIM, with electrical equipment as incidental detection targets among other ceiling/wall fixtures. Borderline for an electrical-construction-focused review.
research_problem: Automatic generation of basic BIMs comprising structural elements like floors, walls, and ceilings from point clouds is now feasible, but generating detailed BIMs that include building equipment (lights, speakers, fire alarms, sprinklers, signs) remains unsolved. Such equipment is attached to ceilings and walls with small surface areas and minimal thickness, making it difficult to detect using only geometric features of point clouds. Existing CNN-based approaches require large amounts of training data, motivating an approach using laser reflection intensity instead.
research_objective: To develop a method for detecting building equipment from MLS point clouds using corrected laser reflection intensity to enable automatic generation of detailed BIMs.
specific_methods: Polynomial approximation for intensity correction; Least-squares fitting; Thresholding based on normal distribution; Planar projection to 2D image; Morphological operations (opening and closing); Connected-component labeling
data_size: Five datasets ranging from ~393,200 to ~1,286,807 points after downsampling, covering wall and ceiling areas of 42-125 m²
future_research_directions: Address over-detection and fragmentation through region merging and splitting; Incorporate 3D shape connectivity and geometric primitive fitting; Assess detection performance for each equipment category; Use hyperspectral LiDAR systems for improved detection and classification; Integrate corrected intensity with orthophoto color information; Apply robust regression methods (RANSAC, Huber loss) for polynomial fitting
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Quality improvement; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Commissioning or closeout
electrical_work_category: Lighting and controls; Low voltage systems (fire alarm, security, access control); Not specified
key_findings: The proposed reflection intensity correction successfully normalized point cloud intensity values on uniform wall and ceiling surfaces, with standard deviations decreasing after correction (e.g., from 605.5 to 309.3 for Ouster LiDAR data). The method detected building equipment with virtually no missed detections (Recall = 1.0) across multiple datasets including a small ~10 cm sprinkler. However, over-detection was a significant issue, with Precision ranging from 0.11 to 0.76 depending on background uniformity, and single equipment items were sometimes fragmented into multiple labeled regions or merged with neighbors.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 100% recall (no missed detections) on wall/ceiling datasets; 90-100% correct detection of equipment with Precision of 0.11-0.76

industry_involvement: KRAFTIA Corporation and KUMONOS Corporation supported execution of the study, as noted in acknowledgments.
limitations_acknowledged: The method assumes the target surface is composed of uniform material; when background variations exist (such as concrete wall stains or panel gaps), non-equipment regions can be falsely detected, leading to high over-detection rates. The image processing stage causes single equipment items to be fragmented or adjacent items to be merged, requiring refined connection criteria. The method requires scanning at relatively constant distance from the target surface.

Author full names: Yan Yan, Chaonan Wang, Fan Zhang, Lei Wu, Jingyi Zang, Tianqi Liu, Shu Tang, Zihao Ye
Title: Development and implementation of a system for electrical engineering BIM detailed design in construction projects
Abstract: Electrical engineering design in construction projects often faces challenges such as inefficient model connectivity, prolonged drawing reviews, and complex cable layout optimization, especially when high-precision Building Information Modeling (BIM) models are required. These issues hinder efficiency and accuracy in electrical system design. Recent studies emphasize the need for more efficient workflows in BIM-based electrical engineering design. Traditional tools rely on manual inputs and high-precision models, causing inefficiencies in time and resources. Previous research highlights the importance of automating processes like cable routing and system validation, but few address intelligent optimization or automation integration in BIM systems. This study develops a comprehensive system for BIM-based electrical engineering design. The proposed Revit plugin generates optimized results without the need for high-precision models. Key stages are detailed, including rapid model connection, streamlined drawing review, and advanced cable optimization. Validation in real-world projects shows that the system improves design and construction efficiency, enhances cable installation quality, and achieves substantial cost savings. The system delivers dual benefits of increased productivity and superior quality in electrical construction projects, marking a significant advancement in BIM-based electrical engineering design.
Author Keywords: building information modeling (BIM); cable routing optimization; detailed design; electrical model; Revit plugin
Funding Details: China Construction Eighth Engineering Division, CCEED; National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKPs, (2024-1-01); National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKPs

Current research focuses on geometric routing while neglecting electrical performance, and tools require excessive manual input and high-LOD models. There is a lack of integrated systems that combine model connection, parameter validation, and cable detailed design within a single automated workflow.

Author full names: P.E.D. Love, J. Zhou, J. Matthews, C.-P. Sing, O. Olatunji, B. Carey
Title: Discussion of "state of practice of building information modeling in the electrical construction industry" by awad s. Hanna, michael yeutter, and diane g. Aoun
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Awad S. Hanna, Michael Yeutter, and Diane G. Aoun discuss state of practice of Building Information Modeling (BIM) in the electrical construction industry. The discussers not only complement their scholarly works, but also provide a needed contextual backdrop that will enable an ameliorated understanding of electrical systems in the construction industry. They note that the authors' study did not present an operational definition of BIM, and this renders their results as superficial and lacking context. It also means that the work eschews providing directions for future research in this fertile area.
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The discussion focuses specifically on BIM adoption and CAD-to-BIM transition in the electrical construction industry and electrical contractors' practices.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally discusses BIM and CAD as digital technologies for electrical design, documentation, and clash detection.
relevance_notes: This is a Discussion/Closure piece (not original empirical research) commenting on Hanna et al. (2014); reviewers may want to treat it as a commentary rather than a primary study.
research_problem: The discussers argue that Hanna et al.'s original survey on BIM adoption in electrical construction lacked an operational definition of BIM, did not assess electrical contractors' roles in contributing to a federated model, and presented findings without sufficient context. They also highlight that electrical systems lack scale and geometry so cannot be fully visualized in 3D, creating a continued reliance on CAD despite its inefficiencies such as replicated information and documentation errors. The commentary aims to contextualize why electrical contractors have been reluctant to move from CAD to object-oriented BIM software.
research_objective: To provide contextual critique of Hanna et al.'s findings on BIM in electrical construction and explain the need to transition from CAD to object-oriented BIM software.
specific_methods: Critical commentary; Literature-based argumentation
data_size: Not reported
future_research_directions: Use case studies rather than surveys to examine BIM benefits and challenges; Contextualize BIM findings to actual electrical contractor practice; Investigate how and why BIM is used by electrical contractors; Quantify cost and time benefits of object-oriented software over CAD; Explore soft and semantic clash detection adoption among electrical contractors
digital_technology: BIM; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Quality improvement; Automation of manual task
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Organizational adoption of technology; RFI or change order management
electrical_work_category: MEP coordination generally; Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices)
key_findings: The discussers argue the original study lacks an operational definition of BIM and context for its findings, leaving readers to assume BIM is merely software rather than a collaborative process. They contend that CAD-based electrical documentation suffers from 1:n information redundancy that propagates errors and RFIs, and that object-oriented BIM tools like Revit MEP can create 1:1 models that reduce errors. They also note that electrical systems lack scale and geometry limiting 3D visualization, and that hard clashes dominate practice while soft and semantic clash detection remain underused.

Author full names: Seungchan Baek, Jongsung Won, Sejun Jang
Title: Economic integrated structural framing for BIM-based prefabricated mechanical, electrical, and plumbing racks
Abstract: Prefabrication, one of the methods to increase productivity by moving construction activity to factory work, has evolved into multi-trade prefabrication. Although previous studies have introduced the merits and directions of multi-trade prefabrication technology, various design and installation techniques must be developed for the economical application of multi-trade prefabrication. This study aims to provide an economical design for prefabricated mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) rack (PMR) structural framing. We proposed five types of PMR structural framing techniques and analyzed their required channel material and labor inputs through a pilot test. The efficiency of PMR structural frames was examined by adjusting the supporting interval, moving the duct outside, and removing the upper framing. Economics and productivity analysis revealed that removing the upper framing method was the most effective when the coordination period was secured. Adjusting the supporting intervals is also an economical design option. The findings of this study can help enhance the economic feasibility of prefabrication and modularization of construction and their widespread utilization.
Author Keywords: modular construction; multi-trade; prefabrication; structural framing
Funding Details: Ministry of Education, MOE, (2022R1C1C1005963); Ministry of Education, MOE; National Research Foundation of Korea, NRF
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses prefabricated MEP racks that integrate electrical (cable trays) along with mechanical and plumbing systems, treating electrical as one component of multi-trade prefabrication rather than the primary focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper uses BIM as a core tool for designing and coordinating prefabricated MEP racks, but BIM is a contextual enabler rather than the primary technological contribution being evaluated.
relevance_notes: Paper is squarely in the MEP prefabrication and BIM coordination space relevant to electrical contractors involved in multi-trade prefab, though electrical is not the sole focus.
research_problem: Multi-trade prefabrication, particularly prefabricated MEP racks (PMRs), offers productivity and safety benefits but increases costs due to additional structural framing required to integrate ducts, pipes, and trays into transportable units. Prior studies have introduced PMR benefits but have not provided detailed design guidance or economic comparison of alternative structural framing options. There is a lack of systematic analysis of how design choices for the supporting framework affect design effort, material consumption, and fabrication labor.
research_objective: To propose and economically evaluate five PMR structural framing design alternatives in order to identify the most cost-effective framing approach for BIM-based multi-trade prefabrication.
specific_methods: Comparative case study; Pilot/mock-up testing; Cost component analysis (design, material, fabrication); BIM-based design; Per-cubic-meter normalization
data_type: BIM model; Project records; Cost records
data_size: Five PMR framing types applied across five corridor cases totaling 36 modules (e.g., 90 m, 18 m, 18 m, 18 m, 72 m corridors) in office and hospital projects
future_research_directions: Expand sample size beyond limited case studies; Investigate regional cost variations affecting framing economics; Explore automation in fabrication to reduce labor cost; Evaluate transportation and on-site installation cost effects; Test structural framing under varied lateral load conditions
value_proposition: Cost reduction; Coordination or clash reduction; Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Prefabrication feasibility; Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery
electrical_work_category: Prefabricated electrical assemblies; MEP coordination generally
key_findings: Structural framing alone accounted for 7.2% to 12.0% of total construction cost across the five PMR designs. Type E (lower-support-only framing at 1.5 m intervals) was most economical at 169.44 USD/m3, followed by Type B (cube frames at 2 m intervals) at 198.20 USD/m3, while Type D (concrete-fixed) was least economical at 327.74 USD/m3. Adjusting supporting intervals and removing upper framing were identified as the most effective strategies, with material cost ranging 24-42% and fabrication cost 25-50% of total framing cost.

quantified_benefit: Type E framing reduced total structural framing cost to 169.44 USD/m3 versus 327.74 USD/m3 for Type D, representing approximately 48% cost reduction; framing accounts for 7.2-12.0% of total construction cost
