Author full names: Jad Chalhoub, Steven K. Ayer
Title: Effect of varying task attributes on augmented reality aided point layout
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: Augmented Reality (AR) has been shown to enable several construction tasks and supplement the use of Building Information Modeling (BIM) on construction sites. However, little effort has been made to evaluate the effect of specific task attributes and model-related factors on the accuracy and performance of current generation AR devices. To address this knowledge gap, the authors identified a commonly used AR device and conducted an experiment related to electrical construction point layout tasks. Furthermore, the effects of several task attributes and content variations were explored. The results suggest that the AR device can display content with a locational accuracy of five centimeters from intended design, equally distributed along the X-axis and Y-axis on the design plane. The location of the content is more accurate the closer it is to the paper marker used to locate content in space. Additionally, increased amounts of content shown and variations in types of content displayed significantly affect the accuracy of the points along the Y-axis (elevation). This paper provides an empirical understanding of certain capabilities and limitations of current AR devices using industry practitioners. The research enables practitioners to better plan for the use of AR in different construction and engineering tasks, and guide future research to develop use cases around the strengths of the technology.
Author Keywords: Augmented Reality; HoloLens; Point Layout; Task Variables
Funding Details: National Science Foundation, NSF, (IIS-1566274); ELECTRI International
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper specifically studies AR-aided point layout for electrical device installation, conducted with an electrical subcontractor and electrical practitioners as participants.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally investigates Augmented Reality (Microsoft HoloLens) integrated with BIM for construction layout tasks.
research_problem: While AR has been proposed for various construction tasks, most research remains in the proof-of-concept stage and focuses on identifying use cases. The effects of specific task attributes (such as content density, elevation variation, and content diversity) and model-related factors on the accuracy and performance of current generation AR devices have not been empirically identified, leaving practitioners without guidance on when AR is suitable for specific construction layout tasks.
research_objective: To quantify how task variables affect the performance of practitioners using AR for electrical point layout from accuracy, time, and mental workload perspectives.
specific_methods: Controlled within-subjects experiment with randomized design order; NASA-TLX cognitive workload survey; Shapiro-Wilk normality test; Paired t-test; Paired Mann-Whitney test; Linear regression; Video analysis for time measurement
data_type: BIM model; Survey responses; Sensor readings; Video
data_size: 734 laid-out points across 4 design variations completed by electrical practitioners over 6 days
future_research_directions: Test AR performance on active construction sites with varied lighting and noise; Explore additional task variations beyond elevation, density, and diversity; Investigate task variables uniquely related to AR as adoption grows; Evaluate next-generation AR hardware and software for accuracy improvements; Develop use cases that align with AR strengths identified
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Decision support; Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Labor productivity; Quality control; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: The HoloLens AR device displayed content with locational accuracy of approximately 5 cm from intended design, roughly equal along X and Y axes. Accuracy decreased parabolically as distance from the paper marker increased (Pearson r=0.5849). Layout was significantly faster (~8 seconds per device) when devices were at the same elevation or all of the same type, and Y-axis accuracy was 22% better with 5 devices versus 10 devices. Cognitive workload was largely unaffected by task variations except for increased effort with more devices.
contractor_role_impacted: Field supervisor or foreman; Electrician or field worker; BIM coordinator or VDC manager
quantified_benefit: Approximately 5 cm locational accuracy from intended design; accuracy degrades with squared distance from marker (R-squared = 0.3421)
research_sponsor: National Science Foundation (Grant No. IIS-1566274) and ELECTRI International
industry_involvement: A large electrical subcontractor (Rosendin Electric) in the US Southwest partnered on the research, with their design team creating BIM models, project managers and BIM modelers being interviewed to identify relevant task variations, and current company practitioners serving as experiment participants at the company's regional headquarters.

The experiment took place in a finished conference room rather than an active construction site, so effects of real site conditions (lighting, noise, congestion, temperature) on AR performance and human behavior remain unknown.

Author full names: Radinal Fadli, Herman Dwi Surjono, Ratna Candra Sari, Fivia Eliza, Muhammad Hakiki, Yayuk Hidayah, Moch Bruri Triyono, Agariadne Dwinggo Samala
Title: Effectiveness of mobile virtual laboratory based on project-based learning to build constructivism thinking
Source title: International Journal of Interactive Mobile Technologies
Abstract: Constructivism as a theoretical basis in education is key, considering that this approach views learning as an active process where students play a role in constructing their own knowledge. Apart from that, the development of mobile and virtual technology is increasingly growing in the educational environment. This research explores the effectiveness of this approach in building constructivist thinking in learning electrical installation practices. The method used in this research is the 4D Define, Design, Develop and Disseminate model. The research instruments include an expert review validity questionnaire and an evaluation instrument for students' perceptions of the effectiveness of the Mobile Virtual Laboratory Based on Project-Based Learning in building constructivism. The research results show that the Mobile Virtual Laboratory is valid based on expert assessment, in addition there has been a significant increase in students' understanding of concepts and practical skills, with a high level of satisfaction with the use of the Mobile Virtual Laboratory. So, it can be concluded that the Mobile Virtual Laboratory based on Project-Based Learning can be an effective tool to support constructivist learning in electrical measurement practice. The results of this research open opportunities for further research on how Artificial Intelligence can build constructive thinking.
Author Keywords: constrictivism; electrical measurement; mobile virtual laboratory; project-based learning
Funding Details: Universitas Negeri Yogyakarta, UNY; Higher Education Financing Agency; KEMENDIKBUDRISTEK; Lembaga Pengelola Dana Pendidikan, LPDP; Ministry of Education, Culture, Research and Technology; BPPT; Education Fund Management Agency

Author full names: Liying Zheng, Christopher Pan, Kiana Kia, Allen Chan, Mina Salehi, Andrew Moorehead, Jeong Ho Kim
Title: Effects of shoulder-assist exoskeletons on muscle activity and balance during a wire pulling task on a simulated scissor lift
Abstract: Construction workers, especially electricians, frequently perform overhead tasks on elevated platforms, increasing their risk of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) and falls. This study evaluated the effects of three passive upper extremity exoskeletons (PUEEs) on shoulder muscle activity and postural balance during a simulated wire pulling task on a motion platform replicating scissor lift dynamics. Nine male participants completed tasks at two force levels (65 N and 130 N), with and without exoskeletons. Electromyography, motion capture, and force plate data were used to assess shoulder muscle activation, joint kinematics, and postural stability. The tested PUEEs significantly reduced peak activity in key shoulder muscles—including upper trapezius (−17 % to −38 %) and much of deltoid (−1 % to −49 %)—without increasing load in the back or lower limbs. Postural stability improved with the tested PUEEs (5 %–25 %), particularly with the heavy ones, though distinct kinematic changes were observed across models. These results suggest that PUEEs may reduce biomechanical strain and improve balance during static or quasi-static overhead work, but task-specific demands and device characteristics should be carefully considered.
Funding Details: Oregon State University, OSU; National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, NIOSH, (9390DVV); National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, NIOSH

research_problem: Electricians frequently perform overhead tasks like wire pulling on elevated, unstable platforms such as scissor lifts, exposing them to elevated risks of musculoskeletal disorders and falls. While passive upper extremity exoskeletons (PUEEs) have shown promise for reducing shoulder loading in stable settings, prior research has also documented secondary effects such as reduced postural stability and altered kinematics. No prior research has examined whether these secondary effects compromise worker safety when PUEEs are used at height on unstable standing surfaces like scissor lifts.

key_findings: The tested PUEEs significantly reduced peak activity in upper trapezius muscles (17% to 38% reduction) and deltoid muscles (1% to 49% reduction) without increasing load on the back or lower limbs. Postural stability improved by 5% to 25% with PUEEs, with the heaviest model (PUEE3) showing the most pronounced improvement. However, distinct kinematic changes (shoulder flexion and elbow flexion alterations) varied across exoskeleton models, indicating that device characteristics and task-specific demands must be carefully considered.

Author full names: Bita Astaneh Asl, Wendy Nora Rummerfield, Carrie Sturts Dossick
Title: Effects of virtual reality on complex building system recall
Abstract: Multidisciplinary design and construction teams are challenged to communicate and coordinate across complex building systems, including architectural, structural, mechanical, electrical, and piping (MEP). To support this coordination, disciplinary 3D models are combined and coordinated before installation. Studies show that besides the use of 3D models, industry professionals sketch building components to discuss coordination issues and find resolutions that require them to recall the building components in the model. In current practices, 3D models are explored with Building Information Modeling (BIM) tools presented on 2D screens, while Virtual Reality (VR) can provide users with an immersive environment to explore. This paper presents the results of an experiment that studied the effects of VR's immersive environment on the participants' complex MEP system recall compared to BIM via sketching. The comparison criteria were the 3D geometry properties of the piping system and the users' self-awareness in the model categorized under color, shape, dimension, piping, and viewpoint. The results showed significant improvement in recall of shape, dimension, and piping when the model was explored in VR.
Author Keywords: building information modeling (BIM); memory recall; MEP; virtual reality (VR)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination broadly but focuses primarily on piping systems in a mechanical room, with electrical mentioned only as part of the generic MEP acronym.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally evaluates Virtual Reality (VR) and BIM as digital technologies for 3D model recall in construction coordination.

research_problem: AEC professionals sketch building components during 3D coordination to discuss design issues, which requires recalling the model from memory. Current BIM tools present 3D models on 2D screens, which may not optimally support memory recall for sketching. It is unknown whether immersive VR can better support users' ability to recall complex MEP system configurations compared to BIM on desktop screens.
research_objective: To examine whether VR's immersive environment improves users' recall of complex MEP systems compared to BIM representation on a 2D screen.
specific_methods: Controlled experiment; Questionnaire-based assessment; Point-based scoring system; Generalized estimation equation (GEE) modeling; Wald tests with robust standard errors
data_size: 24 graduate student participants split into two groups of 12
future_research_directions: Conduct studies with larger sample sizes and various MEP configurations; Measure visual acuity prior to experiments with smaller MEP components; Use hardware with adjustable lens spacing and eye tracking capability; Conduct similar controlled study with 6DOF VR hardware; Conduct studies with AEC industry professionals rather than students
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: Participants using VR showed significant improvements in recall of shape (27% increase), dimension (26% increase), and piping routes (16% increase) compared to BIM on desktop. Object color recall was similar across both platforms. User self-awareness (viewpoint) showed higher average recall in VR but was not statistically significant. Overall, immersive VR supported better memory recall of complex MEP systems for sketching purposes.

quantified_benefit: 27% improvement in shape recall, 26% improvement in dimension recall, and 16% improvement in pipeline recall with VR versus BIM

Author full names: Ali Shehadeh, Odey Alshboul, Madhar M. Taamneh, Aiman Q. Jaradat, Ahmad H. Alomari
Title: Enhanced clash detection in building information modeling: Leveraging modified extreme gradient boosting for predictive analytics
Abstract: This study introduces an intelligent system that involves the Modified Extreme Gradient Boosting (MXGBoost) algorithm to help improve clash detection in Building Information Modeling (BIM) processes. It will automate the clash detection among the architectural, civil, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) disciplines while greatly enhancing the coordination of projects with minimal errors to improve performance in construction projects. The implemented model of the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.057, a Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.0034, an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) of 0.972, an R-squared (R²) value of 0.928, and a precision rate of 0.941. The result justifies the model's performance and identifies its potential to streamline predictive analytics in BIM concerning clash detection. This system integrated into the BIM workflow is a severe turn toward cost efficiency and a decrease in most project timelines. This work represents one of the key steps toward automated BIM coordination, heralding the beginning of a new frontier for the industry and providing construction professionals with the much-needed tools to operate such projects effectively.
Author Keywords: Automation; BIM; Clash detection; Construction coordination; MXGBoost; Predictive modeling
Funding Details: Greater Irbid Municipality; European Union or Clima-Med
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses clash detection across architectural, civil, and MEP disciplines (including electrical and fire protection systems) but treats electrical as one of several systems rather than the central focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and validates a Modified Extreme Gradient Boosting (MXGBoost) machine learning model integrated with BIM for clash detection.
relevance_notes: Electrical work is included within MEP scope but is not the primary focus; the contribution is general MEP/BIM clash detection rather than electrical-specific.
research_problem: Traditional clash detection in BIM relies on manual reviews or basic semi-automated tools applied late in design, which is labor-intensive, error-prone, and produces many false positives. These reactive approaches identify conflicts among architectural, structural, and MEP components only after they impact project workflow, increasing cost and extending timelines. There is a need for predictive and proactive methods that can forecast clashes before they occur using advanced machine learning.
research_objective: To develop and implement a predictive framework using a Modified Extreme Gradient Boosting (MXGBoost) algorithm integrated into BIM processes to forecast component clashes before they appear in construction.
specific_methods: Modified Extreme Gradient Boosting (MXGBoost); Stratified k-fold cross-validation; SHAP value analysis; Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE) plots; Feature importance analysis; Recursive partitioning; Adaptive learning rate optimization
data_type: BIM model; CAD drawings; Project records
data_size: 10,000 labeled clash/non-clash samples extracted from 2D drawings and 3D BIM models
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Cost reduction; Schedule compression
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: The MXGBoost model achieved an MAE of 0.057, MSE of 0.0034, AUC of 0.972, R-squared of 0.928, and precision of 0.941, substantially outperforming standard XGBoost (MAE 0.212, AUC 0.665, precision 0.687). Feature importance analysis identified installation sequence as the most influential predictor, followed by clearance requirements, load specifications, spatial position, and element dimensions. The model was validated on the Green Public Park, Ride and Connect facility in Irbid, demonstrating practical capability to identify architectural-structural and MEP clashes early in the design phase.

Author full names: Ya Hui Teo, Jun Hong Yap, Hui An, Simon Ching Man Yu, Limao Zhang, Jie Chang, Kang Hao Cheong
Title: Enhancing the MEP coordination process with BIM technology and management strategies
Abstract: Building Information Modeling (BIM) has been increasingly used in coordinating the different mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) services in the construction industries. As the construction industries are slowly adapting to BIM, the use of 2D software may become obsolete in the future as MEP services are technically more complicated to coordinate, due to respective services' codes of practice to follow and limit ceiling height. The 3D MEP designs are easy to visualize before installing the respective MEP services on the construction site to prevent delay in the construction process. The aid of current advanced technology has brought BIM to the next level to reduce manual work through automation. Combining both innovative technology and suitable management methods not only improves the workflow in design coordination, but also decreases conflict on the construction site and lowers labor costs. Therefore, this paper tries to explore possible advance technology in BIM and management strategies that could help MEP services to increase productivity, accuracy, and efficiency with a lower cost of finalizing the design of the building. This will assist the contractors to complete construction works before the targeted schedule and meet the client's expectations.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modeling (BIM); construction industry; design modeling; machine learning; management strategies; mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) services; productivity
Funding Details: SUTD-ZJU IDEA, (201903, CFP 2019)

Author full names: Hyun Jeong Seo, Gyu Mi Park, Minjie Son, Ah-Jeong Hong
Title: Establishment of virtual-reality-based safety education and training system for safety engagement
Abstract: The current safety education and training system has a number of problems, namely that the actual risks in the field are not reflected and that workers cannot be engaged in safety education. Therefore, we conducted a study to build a VR-based safety education system that reflects the problems actually occurring in the field. The risk points of the electrical construction sites were derived through in-depth interviews with various stakeholders such as field workers, safety managers, and management. A risk scenario was also constructed by analyzing the causes and effects of existing accident cases. A safety education system was constructed to which the established risk scenario was applied. In the virtual construction site, the site's own model, safety equipment, and members were implemented in a 3D model to form a virtual reality environment. This environment is intended to provide an educational environment wherein workers can immerse themselves in safety, specifically because this VR-based environment can induce active participation by providing safety information through various experiences. In addition, in this study, a VR-based safety education system that reflects the field conditions was designed to prepare basic data for the modernization and activation of safety education.

Author full names: Saman Yarmohammadi, Baabak Ashuri
Title: Exploring the approaches in the implementation of BIM-based MEP coordination in the USA
Source title: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Abstract: The design coordination of Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) systems is a manual process conducted during preconstruction phase of building projects. The difficulties associated with the conventional MEP coordination process have resulted in extensive efforts to enhance the process through utilization of BIM technology. The widespread adoption of BIM technology by AEC (architecture/engineering/construction) firms has given rise to new means and methods of conducting MEP coordination. However, there is limited knowledge about various approaches that are currently practiced as BIMbased coordination. Also, there is a need to identify information items and metrics needed to properly capture and measure the effect of BIM on enhancing coordination process. The major objective of this study is to capture the underpinnings of BIM-based coordination process as currently practiced by AEC firms in the U.S. A nationwide survey was conducted to capture the subject matter experts' opinions in the following areas of BIM-based coordination: (1) Current approaches of conducting coordination using BIM; (2) Information items collected to monitor coordination process; (3) Factors affecting coordination production rate; (4) Metrics used by AEC companies to measure production rate; and (5) Identified best practices leveraged by companies to enhance BIM utilization in the coordination process. The findings of this study indicated that "remote coordination" and "regular coordination" are the most widely used BIM-based coordination approaches. Number of clashes resolved in each meeting, BIM competency of team members, and number of trades attending meetings were identified as the most commonly collected information items by AEC firms throughout the MEP coordination process. Team experience level, preliminary design quality, and MEP system complexity were found as the most important factors that impact the MEP coordination process. Several best practices were recommend by participants in this study to improve the efficiency of MEP coordination process. These strategies were described under the four different phases of an MEP project: initiation and planning, 3D modeling, MEP coordination, and construction and installation. The results of this study are expected to enhance our knowledge about differences among various BIM-based coordination approaches, their popularity among practitioners, and their effects on the coordination progress. The findings of this research will help AEC firms by describing information requirements for establishing a procedure to track, measure, and compare MEP coordination progress across different construction projects.

Author full names: Rahul Chaudhari, Paul Goodrum, Nolan Brady, Matt Jones, Matthew Hallowell, Tom Yeh, Siddharth Bhandari
Title: Exploring the impact of augmented reality on work performance in a full-scale MEP assembly task: Study of industry and novice populations
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Building information modeling (BIM) has revolutionized the construction industry; however, field personnel still rely on traditional methods for design interpretation. Augmented reality (AR) head-mounted display devices (HMDDs) offer a promising alternative for delivering three-dimensional design information, yet their effectiveness must be validated across diverse populations with a significant sample size. This study examines the impact of AR HMDDs on mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) assembly tasks, evaluating key performance metrics: task completion time, rework rate, and error rate. Both novice and experienced participants completed assembly tasks in industrial and laboratory settings, marking the first comparative analysis of AR models with different levels of detail (LOD 300 and LOD 400) against traditional isometric paper plans. Findings indicate that the AR LOD 400 model significantly improved all performance metrics across both populations, except for the error rate among industry professionals. The AR LOD 300 model notably reduced rework rates, while traditional paper plans were the least effective. Age did not significantly impact performance, whereas higher spatial cognition enhanced novices' efficiency. Participants acknowledged AR HMDDs, particularly the LOD 400 model, as beneficial for design comprehension; however, some also reported distractions. These insights highlight the need for user-centered AR interface design and tailored training strategies to enhance usability and efficiency. As the first study to investigate the influence of AR model LODs on construction task performance with a robust sample size, the results provide valuable guidance for optimizing AR HMD technology, training protocols, and design information delivery in the construction industry.
Author Keywords: Augmented reality; Construction assembly; Head-mounted display device; Levels of detail; Work performance

The correlation between AR performance and demographic factors like age and spatial cognition in construction assembly remains largely unexplored.

Author full names: Jad Chalhoub, Steven K. Ayer
Title: Exploring the performance of an augmented reality application for construction layout tasks
Abstract: While Building Information Modeling (BIM) adoption has increased in the industry, most office-to-site design communication still happens using 2D paper plans. Augmented Reality (AR) has been shown to be able to deliver design information to end users, but the degree to which it impacts different applications is still being investigated. This paper explores the use of AR for viewing full-scale model content to support point layout tasks. The paper answers questions related to the performance, accuracy, and effort required from practitioners to use AR as compared to traditional paper documentation. Thirty-two current electrical construction practitioners participated in the experiment completing eight layout tasks each. Half of these tasks were completed with AR and the other half were completed using paper. The results suggest AR enables faster layout of points, while requiring less physical and mental demand compared to paper. However, accuracy is significantly less accurate and may be below some installation standards. Through discussions with the partner organization, it was determined that elevation accuracy may not always be a major concern for electrical device layout, since electrical systems are frequently laid out before walls are framed. This means that points are only placed on the ground, negating the need for elevation accuracy during layout. Therefore, the results suggest potential to using a hybrid point layout method, leveraging the faster layout horizontally with AR and the accuracy of elevation measurement using traditional measuring tapes, when necessary. This paper contributes to the AR body of knowledge by empirically demonstrating the performance benefits and drawbacks of current AR technology for point layout tasks as compared to current paper documentation. This will enable practitioners to evaluate whether to use AR for a point layout task, depending on the required accuracy tolerances, and it will enable researchers to study appropriate applications through a better understanding of the capabilities of current generation AR devices.

Author full names: Taewook Kang, Jonghoon Kim
Title: Feature template-based parametric swept geometry generation from point cloud data: Pipeline design in building systems
Source title: Journal of Pipeline Systems Engineering and Practice
Abstract: Reverse engineering has been used in construction, including residential and commercial buildings and heavy civil constructions, to help integrity access complex or combined damages. However, the previous reverse-engineering process was an inefficient timely manner approach, which was simple to measure the dimensions by the equipment, recorded and modeled by personnel who has engineering knowledge. For this reason, this research study focuses on developing reverse-engineering methods to support the previous reverse-engineering process, which is the parametric swept geometry definition and generation with a predefined feature template (FT) from point cloud data (PCD). PCD were recently obtained and used from the scanned images for facility management purposes. However, the geometry modeling for management in PCD required a lot of effort and time consumption. This research study focuses on the pipelines in mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems as a case study. The swept geometry, which includes ducts, cable trays, pipes, and H-beams, was modeled considering standardized dimensions and constraints in MEP systems in building construction. This study also finds how to generate the parametric swept geometry from PCD based on the predefined FT and provide engineers/designers with flexible reverse-engineering capabilities.
Author Keywords: Feature template (FT) similarity; Parametric swept geometry; Point cloud data (PCD); Predefined feature template; Reverse engineering
Funding Details: BIM Technology Development 3D Urban Building Model Process Automation; Korea Institute of Construction Technology, KICT

Existing automatic reverse-engineering methods fit curves or primitive shapes to point cloud data but do not produce geometry with standardized dimensions and constraints needed for MEP fabrication, quantity estimation, and structural analysis. Without predefined templates reflecting standardized design intent, reverse-engineered geometry from scanned point clouds is difficult to reuse or process for downstream engineering tasks.

key_findings: The FTS-based algorithm successfully identified correct feature templates with high similarity scores (e.g., H-beam 93.09%, duct 99.93%, C-section 85.61%) and generated parametric swept geometries. With up to 50% missing data, H-beam and duct templates were still correctly identified with average similarity around 89.92%, while C-sections were misidentified when more than 10% of data was missing. Interview survey showed strong ratings for reducing reverse-engineering time (4.13/5) and creating standardized geometry (4.38/5), but moderate ratings for template reusability (3.63) and ease of use (3.25).

Author full names: Gustavo S. da Rocha, João Paulo C. Rodrigues, Daniel da Silva Gazzana
Title: Fire risk of electrical installations: a fuzzy petri net approach applied to the National Museum of Brazil
Abstract: Over time, electrical fires have been recurring disasters around the world. As a common preventive strategy, the use of a standard checklist has become a traditional technique widely adopted today. However, this procedure might not capture the full spectrum of risk levels or address interconnected issues, thus potentially compromising effective risk management. Conversely, allowing for prioritization and immediate remediation of fire hazards, the incorporation of expert knowledge and data-driven insights to gauge risk factors is noteworthy. In this context, the approach proposed, rooted in fuzzy Petri nets, optimizes resource allocation by focusing on the highest-risk elements, leading to a more substantial risk reduction. An enhanced methodology was implemented, considering the pre-fire conditions at the Brazilian National Museum. Simulation results indicate that simple corrections in the electrical installation could have significantly reduced the fire risk, possibly preventing the tragedy. These findings underscore the method's effectiveness, emphasizing the importance of thorough electrical risk management. It suggests that the catastrophe might have been avoided had the risks been appropriately addressed. The model emerges as an essential instrument for enhancing risk assessment and strategic resource allocation, especially vital in resource-constrained environments characteristic of developing countries like Brazil. However, it is important to clarify that the proposed methodology is based on expert systems, offering an alternative approach to risk quantification when statistical data and deterministic methods are unavailable. This methodology integrates expert judgment and fuzzy logic for qualitative risk assessments, enabling the identification and prioritization of risk factors despite the lack of quantitative data. While sensitivity analysis is not applicable in this context, validation can be achieved through consensus among expert groups who evaluate the model's assumptions and outcomes.
Author Keywords: Electrical failures; Electrical fires; Fuzzy knowledge representation; Fuzzy petri net; National Museum Fire

This limitation is especially problematic in older buildings with budget constraints, where maintenance personnel must judge which issues require urgent intervention. The lack of statistical data and a comprehensive methodology for quantifying electrical fire risk further hampers effective risk management, as exemplified by the 2018 National Museum of Brazil fire.

Hierarchical ranking identified oxidation on connections (0.500), loose connections (0.375), inconsistencies in circuit breaker design (0.281), and dust/moisture on connections (0.250) as the most critical risk factors. Combined correction of just four top non-conformities (p32, p22, p03, p29) reduced the simulated fire risk by approximately 52%, suggesting the tragedy could have been mitigated with targeted, low-cost interventions.

Author full names: Boyu Wang, Chao Yin, Han Luo, Jack C.P. Cheng, Qian Wang
Title: Fully automated generation of parametric BIM for MEP scenes based on terrestrial laser scanning data
Abstract: As-built building information modeling (BIM) has gained much attention in mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) systems for better facility management. To create as-built BIMs, laser scanning technology is widely used to collect raw data due to its high measurement speed and accuracy. Currently, as-built models are mostly drawn by experienced personnel in BIM modeling software with point cloud data as reference, which is labor intensive and time consuming. This study presents a fully automated approach to converting terrestrial laser scanning data to well-connected as-built BIMs for MEP scenes. According to the geometry complexity, MEP components are divided into regular shaped components and irregular shaped components. A 2D to 3D analysis framework is developed to detect objects and extract accurate geometry information for the two categories of MEP components. Firstly, the MEP scene is divided into slices on which rough geometry information of components' cross sections is extracted. Then, the extracted information on different slices is integrated and analyzed in 3D space to verify the existence of MEP components and obtain refined geometry information used for modeling. Following the detection stage, an MEP network construction approach is developed for MEP components connection and position fine-tuning. Finally, the extracted geometry information and connection relationships are imported into Dynamo to automatically generate the parametric BIM model. To validate the feasibility of the proposed technique, experiments were conducted with point clouds acquired from three scenes in Hong Kong. A comprehensive assessment is presented to evaluate the as-built model quality with three indices: retrieval rate, geometry parameter accuracy and deviation from point clouds to as-built model. The experiment results show that the proposed technique could successfully transform laser scanning data of MEP scenes to as-built BIMs with sufficient accuracy for facility management purpose.
Author Keywords: As-built geometric modeling; Building information model (BIM); MEP scene; Terrestrial laser scanning
Funding Details: Innovation and Technology Commission, ITC, (UIM/344); Innovation and Technology Commission, ITC

As-designed BIMs cannot reflect as-built conditions due to design changes, assembly deviations, and addition/alteration works during O&M. Previous automated approaches focus mainly on pipelines, rely heavily on accurate local features (normals, curvatures) that fail under noise/occlusion, and do not model complete MEP systems including both regular and irregular shaped components and their connections.

Pipe radius extraction achieved RMSE of 2.45 mm and average relative error of 1.86% versus as-designed values. Author full names: Zhen-Zhong Hu, Shuang Yuan, Claudio Benghi, Jian-Ping Zhang, Xiao-Yang Zhang, Ding Li, Mohamad Kassem
Title: Geometric optimization of building information models in MEP projects: Algorithms and techniques for improving storage, transmission and display
Abstract: Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) models are generally characterized by information redundancy and a high density of irregularly shaped components. Consequently, they require large storage spaces and are not conducive for interchange purposes. Geometric optimization of MEP models can play a significant role in facilitating model exchange and handover by increasing storage, transmission and display efficiency. To date, the body of knowledge on such geometric optimization, unfortunately, is still very narrow. This paper presents and tests a solution for the optimization of storage, transmission, and display of MEP models. Storage optimization was achieved through a mapping-based model description method and a novel Quadric-Error-Metric (QEM) mesh simplification algorithm, reducing required storage while maintaining the contour of components. For transmission optimization, a normal vector compression algorithm and a fixed-dictionary specific compression algorithm were proposed to achieve efficient compression of data thus, fulfilling the need for cross-platform interchange. Display optimization was obtained through a normal vector regeneration algorithm for clustering triangle meshes to improve 3D display effects. The evaluation of the solution, performed for each individual component separately as well as for an entire solution, proved successful. Volume of storage data was reduced by 40% to 50% through mesh simplification. Data transmission volume was reduced by more than 80% for components with complicated geometry without affecting the topology of the components. Finally, the display process was capable of decreasing the number of triangles and delivering very good quality of displayed models.
Author Keywords: BIM; Display; Geometric model; MEP; Storage; Transmission
Funding Details: National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (51778336); Tsinghua University, THU; National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKRDPC, (2017YFC0704200)

Existing research has a dearth of studies addressing geometric compression and storage optimization for MEP BIM, even though requirements for efficient display and data interaction exceed the capabilities of existing technology. The lack of such methods may compromise the exploitation of valuable design and construction information during facilities management.

The normal vector regeneration algorithm successfully produced smooth display of curved surfaces even after aggressive simplification.

Author full names: Jingjing Guo, Qian Wang, Jae-Hyuk Park
Title: Geometric quality inspection of prefabricated MEP modules with 3D laser scanning
Abstract: Prefabricated mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) module is one of the off-site construction technologies that can significantly improve productivity. To successfully install MEP modules on-site, performing a geometric quality inspection in prefabrication factories is required to ensure all as-produced MEP elements have accurate positions and correct dimensions. However, the traditional manual inspection process is inaccurate and time-consuming. To address the problem, this study presents an automated geometric quality inspection technique to estimate the geometric properties of MEP modules using 3D laser scanning. The proposed technique firstly extracts the steel frame of the MEP module from the scanned data and registers the scanned data with the as-designed building information modeling (BIM) model of the module. Each MEP element in the module is then detected from the scanned data with the proposed shape fitting algorithm customized for different types of elements. Lastly, the checklist items for geometric quality inspection are computed based on the extracted as-built MEP elements. Experiments on two prefabricated MEP modules were conducted, which demonstrated the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed method.
Author Keywords: 3D laser scanning; Building information modeling (BIM); Geometric quality inspection; Prefabricated MEP module
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses prefabricated MEP modules including cable trays (electrical pathways) and pipes/ducts as a unified MEP assembly, with electrical work as one of several systems inspected.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops algorithms using 3D laser scanning and BIM for automated geometric quality inspection, with novel point cloud processing techniques.
relevance_notes: Cable trays are explicitly part of the inspection scope; electrical relevance is via MEP coordination and prefabricated electrical pathways rather than electrician-specific work.
research_problem: Prefabricated MEP modules require geometric quality inspection before site installation to prevent rework, but the traditional manual inspection using tape measures is inaccurate, time-consuming, and labor-intensive. Existing 3D laser scanning methods for MEP focus on cylindrical pipes or pipe spool fabrication and cannot handle prefabricated MEP modules that contain mixed element types (pipes, cable trays, ducts) with occlusions and noise. There is no automated method to extract and inspect the dimensions and positions of these heterogeneous MEP elements relative to a steel frame.

future_research_directions: Reduce manual parameter setting to improve automaticity; Optimize data acquisition parameters such as scanning resolution and scanner positions; Improve robustness to occlusions in tightly-spaced elements; Avoid inaccuracy caused by subjective parameter settings

pain_point_addressed: Quality control; Prefabrication feasibility; Coordination or clash detection
electrical_work_category: Prefabricated electrical assemblies; MEP coordination generally
key_findings: The proposed technique successfully detected all MEP elements in both test modules, with 98.7% of 154 measured discrepancies (152 out of 154) within the 10 mm tolerance and 74.7% within 5 mm. Average absolute discrepancies were 2.8 mm for MEP 1 and 3.78 mm for MEP 2. The proposed method took 30-35 minutes versus 52-53 minutes for manual inspection, requiring only one person instead of two. Scanning at eight locations or four frontal sides outperformed scanning at four corner sides due to better visibility of element end segments.

Author full names: Andong Qiu, Ganquan Shi, Zhouwang Yang
Title: Graph-based decomposition for electrical wiring design in interior finishing
Abstract: Electrical wiring design within interior finishing is subject to various practical criteria, thereby resulting in time-intensive manual implementation. Despite this, most applications offer only basic collision detection tools, limiting design capabilities. In response, a graph-based decomposition approach is introduced, framing electrical wiring design as an optimization problem on a non-uniform grid graph derived from building information. This method breaks down the problem into two distinct subproblems, which are sequentially solved to achieve an optimal overall solution. Through experimentation across synthetic and real-world instances, performance improvements are demonstrated compared to two conventional integer programming solvers, yielding cost reductions averaging 12.3% when compared to a leading commercial application for interior design. These findings demonstrate the potential integration of this approach into building information modeling platforms, offering an efficient toolset for electrical wiring design in interior finishing.
Author Keywords: Electrical wiring design; Interior finishing; Minimum-bend shortest path; Non-uniform grid; Problem decomposition

Most existing BIM and interior design applications offer only basic collision detection tools rather than comprehensive EWD plans. Generic ILP solvers can model the problem but suffer from dramatically increasing solving times as problem scale grows, while metaheuristics cannot guarantee optimality.

On 9 selected real-world instances, the approach reduced total cost by an average of 12.3% compared to COOHOM (commercial interior design software) and 14.6% compared to manual design. Author full names: Hyojoo Son, Changwan Kim, Hyunchul Choi
Title: High-quality as-is 3D thermal modeling in MEP systems using a deep convolutional network
Abstract: With the growing need for automated condition monitoring and analysis in existing buildings, significant effort has been spent on the development of three-dimensional (3D) thermal models. However, little attention has been paid to ensuring the quality of these 3D thermal models, which can directly impact the accuracy of condition monitoring and analysis results. This study aims to propose a method to generate a high-quality 3D thermal model for mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems by bridging the quality discrepancy between high-resolution laser scan data and low-resolution thermal images using a deep convolutional neural network. The proposed method consists of two main parts: (1) improving the resolution of thermal images based on a deep convolutional network and (2) generating a high-quality 3D thermal model by mapping improved thermal images. The performance of the thermal image resolution improvement was validated using a dataset consisting of 312 thermal images. The results demonstrated that the quality of the improved thermal images based on a deep convolutional network was higher than conventional bicubic interpolation in terms of root mean square error (RMSE), peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), and structural similarity (SSIM). Qualitative analysis of a 3D thermal model utilizing the resolution-improved thermal images was also conducted. This was further qualitatively analyzed to have resulted in improved overall quality of the 3D thermal model. The ability to generate a high-quality 3D thermal model can help auditors to perform automated condition monitoring and analysis in buildings based on objective and accurate data.
Author Keywords: Condition monitoring; High-quality 3D thermal model; Laser scan data; MEP system; Thermal image
Funding Details: Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport, MOLIT, (1615009817); Korea Agency for Infrastructure Technology Advancement, KAIA
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper targets MEP (mechanical, electrical, and plumbing) systems for condition monitoring, but the demonstrated application is on a cogeneration plant facility with mechanical components (pipelines, valves, equipment) rather than electrical-specific systems.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies a deep convolutional neural network combined with laser scanning and thermal imaging to generate 3D thermal models.

research_problem: Existing 3D thermal modeling methods for MEP systems suffer from a quality discrepancy between high-resolution laser scan data and low-resolution thermal images, which results in low-quality 3D thermal models when thermal images are mapped onto dense point clouds. Conventional resolution improvement methods (e.g., bicubic interpolation, FLIR's MSX and UltraMax) have limitations such as jagged artifacts, blurred temperature deviation areas, dependence on edge information from digital images, or reliance on human-induced camera motion. Prior research has not adequately addressed how the low resolution of thermal images degrades the overall quality of the resulting 3D thermal model used for condition monitoring.
research_objective: To propose a method that generates a high-quality 3D thermal model for MEP systems by bridging the resolution gap between laser scan data and thermal images using a deep convolutional neural network.

Author full names: Qian Wang, Jiajun Li, Xiaoying Tang, Xinchen Zhang
Title: How data quality affects model quality in scan-to-BIM: A case study of MEP scenes
Abstract: In scan-to-BIM process, the quality of scan data (i.e. data quality) greatly affects the quality of the generated BIM model (i.e. model quality). However, there has been little research that investigates the relationship between data quality and model quality in a quantitative manner. Hence, this study aims to understand the relationship between data quality and model quality using a case study on mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) scenes. Two MEP scenes were scanned with different scanning settings (angular resolutions and scanning locations), and a total of 20 point cloud datasets were obtained. Then, each point cloud dataset was converted into a BIM model by a BIM modeler. Afterwards, the data quality and model quality were measured for each MEP member. The data quality was measured using Degree of Completeness (DOC) and Point Density (PD), and model quality was measured using recognizability, radius error (for pipe), width error (for duct), and length error. Using these measures, the relationship between data quality and model quality was examined regarding significance and correlation. The analysis results show that DOC and PD play different roles in influencing recognizability and various modeling errors. Given a certain model quality requirement, the required data quality can be inferred from the analysis results, which can help achieve a better planned scan-to-BIM process.
Author Keywords: Data quality; MEP scenes; Model quality; Scan-to-BIM
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP scenes broadly (pipes and ducts) but focuses on mechanical/plumbing components; electrical components like conduit or cable trays are not modeled in the experiments.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally engages with scan-to-BIM, laser scanning/point cloud processing, and BIM model reconstruction, which are core digital technologies in construction.

research_problem: In scan-to-BIM workflows for existing buildings, the quality of laser scan data directly affects the quality of the resulting BIM model, but the relationship between data quality and model quality has not been quantitatively investigated. It remains unknown what level of scan completeness and point density is required to achieve a given modeling accuracy, particularly for cluttered MEP scenes where occlusions and varying member sizes complicate manual modeling. This gap hinders effective scan planning and the determination of data quality requirements for scan-to-BIM projects.
research_objective: To understand the relationship between scan data quality and BIM model quality through a case study on MEP scenes using two data quality metrics and four model quality metrics.
specific_methods: Terrestrial laser scanning; Point cloud slicing and voxelization; Manual scan-to-BIM modeling by student modelers; Pearson correlation analysis; p-value significance testing; Monte Carlo simulation of pipe radius fitting
data_size: 20 point cloud datasets from 2 MEP scenes covering 59 MEP members (39 pipes, 20 ducts), modeled by 20 student modelers
future_research_directions: Consider additional data quality parameters such as accuracy and noise level; Investigate effect of different modeling methods on model quality; Extend to other MEP member types such as cable trays; Include MEP connectors like elbows and tees in modeling; Use regression models to quantitatively predict model quality; Develop scan planning linking scanning settings to data quality requirements
digital_technology: IoT or sensors; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Quality improvement; Decision support; Automation of manual task
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Quality control
key_findings: Recognizability and pipe radius error were significantly correlated only with maxDOC (degree of completeness), while duct width error was significantly correlated with both maxDOC and maxPD (point density), and length error was significantly correlated with aveDOC. To achieve a pipe radius error under 2 mm, maxDOC should exceed 0.6; to achieve duct width error around 3 mm, maxDOC should be at least 0.7 and maxPD at least 300,000 points/m2. Smaller pipes were more sensitive to completeness, with simulated radius error increasing fivefold as DOC dropped from 0.75 to 0.25. Data completeness emerged as more critical than data density across most model quality metrics.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Pipe radius error <2 mm achievable with maxDOC ≥0.6; recognizability rate of 94% at maxDOC >0.8 versus 0% at maxDOC <0.2
research_sponsor: Not reported

Modeling methods were uniform among student modelers, so effects of different modeling approaches were not tested. The work covered only cylindrical pipes and rectangular ducts without connectors, and the analysis used correlation rather than regression so it cannot predict achievable model quality from given data quality.

Author full names: Rahul Chaudhari, Paul Goodrum, Siddharth Bhandari, Matthew Hallowell, Matt Jones, Nolan Brady, Tom Yeh
Title: How does augmented reality head-mounted display impact the ability to detect safety hazards while performing piping assembly?
Abstract: Augmented Reality (AR) Head Mounted Displays Devices (HMDDs) have the potential to revolutionize information delivery during the construction phase. However, concerns remain about whether AR HMDDs impact workers' ability to detect changes in their surroundings, which could pose safety risks. In this controlled experiment, one hundred industry craft workers participated in an assembly task on a full-scale Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) model using three information formats: traditional isometric paper drawings and two AR models at levels of detail (LOD) 300 and 400 that vary based on the density of information provided. A safety hazard scenario was introduced, and the response time to detect the change was recorded. Findings revealed a significant difference in response times, with non-AR HMDD users detecting changes more quickly than AR HMDD users. Further investigation examined the correlation between workers' age, spatial cognition, and response time to detect changes. This study is one of the first in the construction domain to introduce hazards (referred to as change) and examine AR HMDDs' impact on individuals' ability to detect them.
Author Keywords: Augmented reality; Change detection; Construction assembly; Head-mounted display devices; Situational awareness
Funding Details: National Science Foundation, NSF, (1928398, ID 1928398); National Science Foundation, NSF
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The study uses a full-scale MEP test bed including electrical conduit assembly tasks alongside mechanical piping, with participants from electrical and other trades, but electrical work is not the sole focus.

research_problem: Prior studies on AR HMDDs in construction have largely ignored perceptual limitations such as change blindness, and the few that examined situational awareness lacked ecological validity due to small samples of students in unrealistic lab tasks. It is unclear whether AR HMDDs compromise workers' ability to detect environmental changes that pose safety hazards while performing primary assembly tasks. Additionally, the relationships between worker age, spatial cognition, and hazard detection in AR-assisted environments have not been examined despite the aging construction workforce.
research_objective: To investigate how AR HMDDs impact craft workers' ability to detect safety hazards (changes) during MEP assembly tasks and how age and spatial cognition correlate with response time.
specific_methods: Controlled experiment with three groups; Shapiro-Wilk normality test; Kruskal-Wallis H test; Bonferroni post-hoc pairwise comparison; Spearman's rank correlation; Wilcoxon rank-sum test; Card Rotation Test; Cube Comparison Test
data_size: 100 industry craft workers recruited; 73 included in final analysis (Paper=33, ARLOD300=17, ARLOD400=23)
future_research_directions: Investigate effects of prior AR training sessions on change detection; Incorporate peripheral alerts such as auditory or haptic feedback; Develop layered information hierarchy to manage cognitive load; Conduct studies on actual construction sites with real tasks; Design AR interfaces tailored to user age and spatial ability

electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); MEP coordination generally
key_findings: Non-AR HMDD users (paper drawings) detected the safety hazard significantly faster than AR HMDD users, with mean response times of 16.4s (paper), 39.9s (ARLOD300), and 44.6s (ARLOD400). Pairwise comparisons showed Paper vs ARLOD300 (p=0.0151) and Paper vs ARLOD400 (p=0.0011) were significantly different, while LOD300 vs LOD400 was not. Age positively correlated with response time (rho=0.29, p=0.012), with the effect most pronounced in the ARLOD400 group, while spatial cognition test scores showed no significant correlation with hazard detection.
contractor_role_impacted: Electrician or field worker; Field supervisor or foreman; Safety manager
quantified_benefit: Non-AR users detected hazards in 16.4s vs 39.9s (LOD300) and 44.6s (LOD400) for AR users; age-response correlation rho=0.29, p=0.012

Author full names: Shiva Pooladvand, Woei-Chyi Chang, Sogand Hasanzadeh
Title: Identifying at-risk workers using fNIRS-based mental load classification: A mixed reality study
Abstract: Construction is one of the most hazardous industries, in part because it involves dynamic and cognitively demanding tasks that tax workers' mental resources. Though some previous studies showed increases in workers' mental load can adversely affect the safety-critical function of workers' situational awareness, limited previous work has utilized machine learning (ML) techniques for mental load classification models with Functional Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (fNIRS) signals to passively identify at-risk workers suffering excessive mental loads. Within an immersive mixed-reality environment simulating an electrical construction task, this study employed ensemble modeling to classify workers' mental load via their fNIRS-captured brain activation. Beyond identifying the prefrontal and motor cortices as the most important brain areas contributing to ML-driven mental load classifications when using fNIRS, this paper also proposes a mental load classification methodology that synthesizes neuroimaging data with individuals' risk-propensity scores to address the limitations of existing personalized and generalized ML models. The results reveal better performance for fNIRS-based mental load classifications given participants' personal risk propensity (risk-seeker and risk-averse). This research, therefore, (1) offers valuable insights into the importance of identifying at-risk workers who are more likely to experience impaired cognitive processing and situational awareness due to increased mental load, and (2) sheds light on reliable pipelines for developing fNIRS-based mental load classification.
Author Keywords: Cognitive demand; Ensemble modeling; fNIRS; Machine learning (ML); Mental load classification; Time pressure
Funding Details: National Science Foundation, NSF, (2049711); National Science Foundation, NSF

Despite the promise of fNIRS for monitoring mental load due to its motion robustness and spatial resolution, there is a paucity of research using fNIRS-based machine learning to classify mental load in construction. Existing personalized and generalized ML models also yield inconsistent results because they neglect individual personality traits such as risk propensity that influence cortical brain activation.
research_objective: To develop and evaluate fNIRS-based ensemble machine learning classification models that identify at-risk construction workers experiencing increased mental load, incorporating risk propensity to address limitations of generalized and personalized models.
specific_methods: Ensemble machine learning (AutoML via Auto-Sklearn); Binary and multi-class classification; General Linear Model (GLM) for fNIRS signal processing; Permutation feature importance analysis; Nested 10-fold cross-validation; Mixed-reality simulation experiment; NASA-TLX style sense-of-presence questionnaire; Sensation-seeking scale grouping

key_findings: Ensemble classifiers achieved 92% accuracy for low vs. moderate mental load, 94% for low vs. high, and 78% for the multi-class model. The prefrontal cortex (PFC, DLPFC) and motor cortices were identified as the most important brain regions for classification, with average and maximum oxygenated hemoglobin values being the most critical features. Risk-propensity-based classification (85% for risk-seekers, 79% for risk-averse) outperformed both the generalized (78%) and personalized (75%) models, demonstrating that integrating personality traits improves fNIRS-based mental load classification.

Author full names: Qinglin Wu, Lijuan Chen, Peixin Shi, Weijun Wang, Sheng Xu
Title: Identifying impact factors of MEP installation productivity: An empirical study
Abstract: BIM-based coordination facilitates the discovery of potential pipeline design and construction conflicts. However, the impact of BIM on-field productivity of MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) installation is unclear because of lacking identification of influencing factors. To fill this research gap, an extensive literature review and quantitative analysis are conducted to identify MEP installation productivity's impact factors. The identified 24 impact factors are categorized into four primary categories: organizational, management, technical, and personnel. A questionnaire is then designed to measure the impact significance, and the feedback is analyzed through the analytic hierarchy process (AHP) method. This paper finds that: (1) organizational management and control at the macro-level impact productivity more than specific technology and operation during the MEP installation; (2) the key factors affecting productivity are project delivery method, contract mode, and quality control (rework). Accordingly, strategies and suggestions are put forward to encourage and implement productive BIM adoption.
Author Keywords: BIM; installation productivity; MEP engineering; quantitative analysis

relevance_notes: Electrical work is bundled within MEP; findings are not electrical-specific but are relevant to electrical contractors engaged in BIM-enabled MEP coordination.
research_problem: The impact of BIM on field productivity of MEP installation is unclear because the specific factors influencing productivity have not been systematically identified or prioritized. Previous studies demonstrated BIM benefits but did not rank the key factors or mechanisms that determine whether BIM adoption actually improves MEP installation productivity. This gap prevents development of effective strategies for productive BIM implementation in MEP projects.

specific_methods: Literature review; Expert consultation (semi-structured interviews); Questionnaire survey; Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP); Cronbach's alpha reliability test; KMO and Bartlett sphericity validity test; Consistency ratio check

key_findings: Organizational factors (weight 0.3931) and management factors (0.3023) impact MEP installation productivity more than technical factors (0.1910) and personnel factors (0.1136), indicating that macro-level organizational control matters more than specific technology or operation. The three most critical individual factors are project delivery method (0.0964), contract mode (0.0919), and quality control/rework (0.0763). Approximately 69.25% of respondents believed BIM use could increase MEP installation productivity by 25-50%, primarily through clash detection, pipeline optimization, and construction simulation.

Author full names: Bita Astaneh Asl, Wazahat Ali Z. Attar, Tanmay Z. Tuscano
Title: Immersive VR versus desktop for spatial understanding of complex MEP systems in 3D coordination training
Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) coordination is a critical process that ensures an effective integration of various building systems, such as architectural, structural, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP). Spatial understanding of MEP systems can be particularly challenging for students with limited industry experience due to their 3D geometry complexity and tight integration with other building systems. Effective training in understanding MEP systems is essential for equipping students with the skills needed to resolve system conflicts and propose solutions in the 3D coordination process. In this study, controlled experiments were conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of immersive virtual reality (VR) environments compared to desktop-based building information modeling (BIM) tools in enhancing students' comprehension of MEP systems. The experiment compared two head-mounted display VR setups: a three-degrees-of-freedom (3DOF) VR utilizing smartphones and viewers and a six-degrees-of-freedom (6DOF) VR using a headset, to a desktop configured with matching degrees of freedom, using a BIM coordination program. The evaluation criteria for spatial understanding were based on system component geometry and the user's self-awareness, as assessed through questionnaire-based subjective feedback. The results showed that 3DOF VR improved students' understanding of object depth, component location and position, and user orientation compared to desktop. However, 6DOF VR provided the most support for navigating and spatial knowledge of MEP systems, leveraging its walkthrough capabilities. Participants found 3DOF VR to be an effective tool for enhancing comprehension of MEP systems, given its affordability and feasibility for simultaneous use by all students in a classroom environment. For educators seeking to integrate VR technologies in the classroom, 3DOF VR can be an affordable and practical solution, while 6DOF VR provides a more realistic experience for deeper spatial understanding that requires headsets and dedicated space for exploration.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP); Three-dimensional (3D) coordination; Virtual reality (VR)
Funding Details: California State University, CSU

Existing VR spatial-perception studies focus on architectural models rather than the linear, spatially constrained components typical of MEP systems.

Author full names: Hisham M. Said, Justin Reginato
Title: Impact of design changes on virtual design and construction performance for electrical contractors
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Specialty trade contractors have increasingly invested in virtual design and construction (VDC) capabilities to virtually model the project, ensure the constructability of the model, engineer its system components, prefabricate its assemblies, and layout the virtual model on-site automatically. This paper investigates the impact of design and scope changes on the performance of VDC tasks by adopting a hybrid research methodology that depends on interviewing and survey samples of electrical contractors. Drivers of VDC cost overruns and best practices to control and recover these costs are presented. This paper provides two contributions to the body of knowledge and industry practice of the construction engineering and management community. First, the paper presents the extent and drivers of the inefficiencies and cost overruns experienced by electrical contractors in managing VDC work scopes. Second, a set of best practices is suggested to manage, plan, and control the performance of VDC tasks of electrical contractors and other specialty contractors.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling; Changes; Electrical contractors; Project planning and design; Virtual design and construction
Funding Details: ELECTRI International

Owners and GCs often misunderstand VDC, requiring contractors to absorb rework costs that they cannot recover. There is limited research quantifying these impacts and providing best practices for managing VDC under design changes specifically for electrical contractors.

Author full names: Sejun Jang, Ghang Lee
Title: Impact of organizational factors on delays in bim-based coordination from a decision-making view: A case study
Source title: Journal of Civil Engineering and Management
Abstract: This study analyzed the impact of organizational factors on delays in building information modeling (BIM)- based coordination for mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems from the decision-making perspective. Recently BIM-based coordination has been regarded as a critical phase in project delivery but suffers from delays during the coordination process. This study investigated three complexity factors that often contribute to coordination delays: the number of participants - the total number of participants involved in a decision-making process for resolving a coordination issue; the level of the decision makers - the highest decision-maker involved in a problem-resolution process; and the heterogeneity of participants - the number of trades related to an issue. Using 95 major coordination issues derived from 11,808 clashes in a case study, the correlations between the coordination time and the complexity factors were analyzed. The coordination time linearly increased as each factor increased. The number of participants had the highest correlation with the coordination time, followed by the level of decision makers and the heterogeneity of participants. The findings stress the significance of integration between BIM and lean approaches, such as Obeya (big room) and Shojinka (flexible manpower line), during BIM-based coordination to expedite decision-making processes and eventually to reduce the coordination time.
Author Keywords: BIM; Coordination time; Delay; Design management; Organizational management
Funding Details: Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy, Republic of Korea, (20174010201320); Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

This gap leaves project teams without an understanding of how organizational structure influences coordination efficiency.

Author full names: Juan Francisco Fernández Rodríguez
Title: Implementation of BIM virtual models in industry for the graphical coordination of engineering and architecture projects
Abstract: Building information modeling (BIM) has traditionally been considered as a tool for the graphical representation of architectural and engineering projects. This technology has become a key instrument for the development of virtual models that simulate the constructive process and facilitate the analysis of the designed solutions to detect incidents linked to traditional bi-dimensional projects. This article focuses on the use of this technology to optimize the design of MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) facilities and architecture by developing virtual BIM models. Therefore, the purpose of this research is to analyze two experiences of complex buildings that have developed a BIM Execution Plan to improve the coordination of all disciplines involved, in order to explore how these real experiences can contribute to the implementation of the use of this technology in the construction industry. The results of the research are divided into two aspects: on the one hand, the improvements that BIM brings to the coordination and optimization of MEP facilities, linked to the typification of design incidents detected to anticipate conflicts between disciplines, facilitate collaborative design between different agents, keep graphic documentation updated and avoid execution problems and additional costs; and on the other hand, the keys to facilitating the extension of the use of this technology in the industry. Therefore, the conclusions of the research point out to the need for improvement in the automation of incident detection and the reduction in design process deadlines, as well as the need to simplify the virtual modeling process to bring it closer to unqualified personnel involved in the workflow and facilitate the implementation of this technology in the construction industry.
Author Keywords: 3D visualization; building information modeling; graphical analysis; MEP facilities design; virtual modeling

Author full names: Iman Bitaraf, Ali Salimpour, Pedram Elmi, Ali Akbar Shirzadi Javid
Title: Improved building information modeling based method for prioritizing clash detection in the building construction design phase
Abstract: The rising complexity of construction projects and the industry's commitment to sustainable practices have driven the extensive adoption of Building Information Modeling (BIM) technology. A core function of BIM is the early identification and resolution of clashes during the design phase, which serves to mitigate costly rework and delays in the construction process. This study presents an advanced method for classifying and prioritizing hard clashes between structural components and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems. Employing the Best-Worst Method (BWM), this research assigned specific weights to structural and MEP elements based on expert evaluations. Six parameters were incorporated into this prioritization framework: the weights determined by the BWM, outputs from Navisworks software (v2021), the ratio of MEP volume to floor volume, the functional purpose of each floor, and the number of adjacent elements. A custom-developed plugin for Autodesk Navisworks integrated these parameters, enabling real-time automated clash prioritization. Clashes were ranked by criticality through a calculation involving the six parameters, which enhanced the efficiency of clash detection by optimizing time and cost considerations during the design phase. Case study results indicate that beams and columns represent the most critical structural elements, while ducts are identified as the most significant MEP elements. The proposed method substantially improves clash detection and prioritization efficiency and accuracy, yielding considerable benefits in project management and resource allocation.

Author full names: Shuju Jing, Gichun Cha, Michael Bekele Maru, Byoungjoon Yu, Seunghee Park
Title: Improved building MEP systems semantic segmentation in point clouds using a novel multi-class dataset and local–global vector transformer network
Abstract: Point cloud semantic segmentation for mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems is crucial for establishing MEP systems digital twins. Deep learning has shown promise in automatically deriving point-wise semantic labels from point clouds. However, the utilization of 3D deep learning methods for directly segmenting complex, occluded, and densely distributed multi-class MEP systems in common building point clouds remains unexplored due to the lack of specialized public datasets. To address this, our study introduces a public, richly annotated, and multi-class point cloud dataset for MEP systems in common buildings, consisting of 92.10 million points across four buildings, 56 areas, and nine component categories. Subsequently, we propose Trans2Net, a novel and customized 3D deep learning method for the automatic and precise segmentation of MEP components, featuring local vector transformer module, global-aid vector transformer module, and learnable feature fusion operator that enhance the model's sensitivity to local features, capture global context with sparse points, and dynamically fuse weighted local and global features. Experimental results demonstrate that Trans2Net significantly outperforms seven other state-of-the-art methods on the MEP dataset, achieving an overall accuracy of 97.39%, a mean accuracy of 95.45%, and a mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 90.76%. Notably, it surpasses the second-ranked method, PointMeta, by a margin of 9.91% in mIoU. This study provides a novel paradigm for automatically understanding MEP systems in point clouds and offers robust support for digital twin management of MEP systems, contributing to the prolonged lifespans of MEP systems.
Author Keywords: Deep learning; Local–global vector transformer; Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems; Point clouds dataset; Semantic segmentation
