Author full names: Boyu Wang, Fangzhou Lin, Mingkai Li, Zhenyu Liang, Zhengyi Chen, Mingzhu Wang, Jack C.P. Cheng
Title: Informative as-built modeling as a foundation for digital twins based on fine-grained object recognition and object-aware scan-vs-BIM for MEP scenes
Abstract: Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems are critical for delivering essential services and ensuring comfortable environments. To improve the management efficiency of these complex systems, digital twins (DTs) that reflect the as-is conditions of facilities are increasingly being adopted. To generate DT models, laser scanners are widely used to capture as-built environments in the form of high-resolution images and dense 3D measurements. However, existing scan-to-BIM methods primarily produce basic geometric models, lacking detailed descriptive attributes of the components. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an informative DT model generation method for MEP systems based on fine-grained object recognition and object-aware scan-vs-BIM. The proposed method adopts a few-shot learning strategy to detect target objects in complex 3D environments and identify their family types based on vision foundation models. Following this, the association between as-designed components and as-built installations is formulated as a bipartite graph matching problem, which is solved using the Hungarian algorithm. This enables the automated updating of as-designed models into as-built DT models. Notably, the proposed association method is robust and applicable to components with significant installation deviations, a common challenge in MEP systems. The feasibility of the proposed approach was validated through experiments conducted on two construction sites in Hong Kong. Results demonstrated that the proposed approach significantly enhanced the accuracy of the scan-vs-BIM of MEP systems, thereby enabling informative DT model generation.
Author Keywords: Digital Twin Model; Laser Scanning; MEP System; Scan-to-BIM; Scan-vs-BIM; Vision Foundation Model

Author full names: Xiaofeng Liao, Yiminxuan Liu, Yongtong Huang, Yu Luo, Zhaoyin Zhou, Heap-Yih Chong
Title: Integrating CAD, BIM, immersive technology, and 3D Gaussian Splatting for construction model coordination with ISO 19650
Abstract: Traditional construction project management approaches suffer from technical isolation and process deficiencies, particularly in whole-life-cycle model coordination. This paper addresses how to develop a comprehensive management framework that optimizes model coordination through technology integration. This multi-technology integration framework combines CAD drawings, Building Information Modeling (BIM), immersive technology, and 3D Gaussian Splatting scanning while implementing ISO 19650 standards for information management. A hospital project case study was used and validated featuring complex structural and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) coordination scenarios. This empirical study demonstrated an approximate 22%-35% reduction in pipeline dismantling rates, 40% shorter review cycles for complex nodes, and significantly improved construction quality verification accuracy. These outcomes highlight how technological synergies can overcome traditional management limitations and establish a robust foundation for future engineering projects. Future research should validate the framework across diverse project types, explore additional integrated technologies, and conduct long-term impact assessments during operation phases.

Author full names: Tushar Jadhav
Title: Integrating knowledge of project management and BIM for MEP education: Review and recommendations
Abstract: The purpose of this paper is to address the importance of project management education and building information modeling (BIM) for coordinating mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) services. Coordination of MEP services for large projects is often complex and challenging. A systematic literature review was carried out to understand the previous research on the coordination of MEP services for various building projects. This paper proposes a unique framework to simplify the process of learning MEP. The framework recommends covering MEP education in three stages: fundamentals of project management, fundamentals of MEP services, and use of BIM for MEP. The paper discusses the importance of learning project management and BIM for reducing the complexity of MEP coordination. The author cites his own teaching experiences and examples to justify the proposed framework. The finding of this study provides significant insights for practicing architects and engineers working in the domain of MEP services and will help them to understand this complex and exhaustive topic in a more simplified way. The study offers an interesting perspective of integrating the fundamentals of project management and BIM for MEP education.
Author Keywords: BIM; MEP; MEP coordination; Project management

Existing studies either focus on the technical knowledge of MEP systems or on BIM software usage, but none integrate project management fundamentals, MEP services knowledge, and BIM into a unified educational approach. Incomplete understanding of MEP among stakeholders leads to delays, cost overruns, disputes, and inefficient operation of MEP systems.

Based on the author's teaching experience from 2015-2020, participants reported more meaningful stakeholder interactions and better understanding of MEP scope after completing the modules. Three key challenges in MEP education are identified: lack of visualization of MEP scope, difficulty in preparing MEP work breakdown structures, and complexity of manual MEP calculations, with the framework proposed to address each.

Author full names: Kayode O. Kazeem, Timothy O. Olawumi, Jibrin J. Adam, Edmond Wai-Ming Lam
Title: Integration of building services in modular construction: A PRISMA approach
Abstract: Modular construction is gaining worldwide attention. Building services are the systems that make buildings comfortable and efficient. Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems provide heating, cooling, lighting, energy distribution, and water services. Modular construction integrates building services into prefabricated modules at the manufacturing site, which are then transported to the construction site. A systematic review (SR) of building services integration within modular construction is thus necessary. Prior to the SR, a quantitative analysis of the retrieved 115 publications from the Scopus database was explored. Using the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guide, 13 relevant publications from the Scopus database and via the Research Rabbit application were profiled. MEP systems integration approaches and research needs were examined. Recent literature emphasizes technology integration and sustainability, while early studies laid the framework for modular approaches. The findings highlight the potential of digital technologies like building information modeling (BIM) and recommend a holistic framework for the entire building's lifetime, from design to operation. Future research directions include performance studies, modular building service adaptation, and industry-wide standards building. Researchers and practitioners seeking to improve modular construction methods and integrate complex building services will gain insights from this study.
Author Keywords: BIM; building services; integration; MEP systems; modular construction; prefabrication

There is a lack of synthesized knowledge on how MEP and building services are integrated within modular construction, including methodologies, technologies, and research gaps.

Keywords related to building services integration appear less frequently (41 occurrences) than general modular construction terms (542 occurrences), indicating an emerging research gap. Author full names: Junjie Li, Shengli Du, Jianfeng Liu, Weibiao Chen, Manfu Tang, Lei Zheng, Lianfa Wang, Chunle Ji, Xiao Yu, Wanli Yu
Title: Language guided 3D object detection in point clouds for MEP scenes
Abstract: In recent years, contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP) has gained popularity for processing 2D data. However, the application of cross-modal transferable learning to 3D data remains a relatively unexplored area. In addition, high-quality, labelled point cloud data for Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) scenarios are in short supply. To address this issue, the authors introduce a novel object detection system that employs 3D point clouds and 2D camera images, as well as text descriptions as input, using image-text matching knowledge to guide dense detection models for 3D point clouds in MEP environments. Specifically, the authors put forth the proposition of a language-guided point cloud modelling (PCM) module, which leverages the shared image weights inherent in the CLIP backbone. This is done with the aim of generating pertinent category information for the target, thereby augmenting the efficacy of 3D point cloud target detection. After sufficient experiments, the proposed point cloud detection system with the PCM module is proven to have a comparable performance with current state-of-the-art networks. The approach has 5.64% and 2.9% improvement in KITTI and SUN-RGBD, respectively. In addition, the same good detection results are obtained in their proposed MEP scene dataset.
Author Keywords: computer vision; object detection
Funding Details: National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKRDPC, (2017YFC0804400, 2017YFC0804401)

Existing 3D detection methods do not leverage cross-modal (image-text) supervision, and applying CLIP-style contrastive language-image pretraining to 3D point cloud detection in MEP environments remains largely unexplored. This makes accurate object detection in complex MEP point clouds challenging.
research_objective: To develop a language-guided 3D object detection system that uses CLIP-style image-text matching to guide point cloud detection in MEP scenes.
specific_methods: Contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP); Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder; Point Cloud Modelling (PCM) module; Weight sharing / transfer from image encoder; Cross-entropy and detection loss optimization; Ablation studies; Benchmark evaluation (mIoU, mAP)

Ablation studies showed the PCM fusion module outperformed simple concatenation/summation fusion, and CLIP pretraining combined with contrastive loss yielded the best 3D mAP (85.00 on KITTI). Author full names: Boan Tao, Frédéric Bosché, Jiajun Li
Title: Mixed Reality-based MEP construction progress monitoring: Evaluation of methods for mesh-to-mesh comparison
Abstract: Visually monitoring progress and geometric quality on site using Mixed Reality (MR) and overlaid Building Information Model (BIM model) is challenging, particularly in complex contexts like complex mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems. This paper proposes and evaluates four individual methods and three combined ones for automated object recognition and deviation evaluation, based on the matching and comparison of the 3D mesh captured on site by MR systems with the mesh geometry of the elements in the (as-designed) BIM model. The four individual methods include: (1) Bounding Box Occupation, (2) Point-to-Surface Distance, (3) Voxel Occupation, (4) Feature Matching. Three combined methods are Method 1∪4, Method 2∪4 and Method 3∪4 (i.e. combining methods 1 and 4, 2 and 4, and 3 and 4, respectively). The methods are evaluated using both synthetic and real data of MEP construction works, with the Method 1∪4 yielding the best performance.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modelling (BIM); Construction progress monitoring; Mesh-to-mesh comparison; Mixed Reality (MR)
Funding Details: McLaughlin & Harvey Construction Ltd
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) systems collectively, with electrical components (cable trays, conduit) included as part of the broader MEP scope but not as the central focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates Mixed Reality (Microsoft HoloLens 2) combined with BIM and computational mesh comparison algorithms for automated construction progress monitoring.
relevance_notes: Electrical work is treated under the MEP umbrella alongside ductwork and piping; cable trays are mentioned but the cylindrical/cuboid examples emphasize ducts and pipes rather than electrical-specific elements.
research_problem: Visual progress and quality monitoring of complex MEP systems on construction sites using Mixed Reality with overlaid BIM currently relies on manual human observation, which is inconvenient and error-prone in cluttered environments with intricate ductwork, piping, and wiring. Existing scan-vs-BIM automation methods using laser scanning or photogrammetry require lengthy offline processing, making them unsuitable for real-time decision-making. Prior MR-based methods (e.g., Kopsida and Brilakis) only detect element presence via bounding boxes and lack robustness against occlusion, noise, and shape deviations.
research_objective: To propose and evaluate multiple mesh-to-mesh comparison methods that automatically process MR-captured 3D mesh data against BIM models for real-time MEP progress monitoring.
specific_methods: Bounding box occupation; Point-to-surface distance; Voxel occupation; Feature matching with circle/rectangle fitting; Principal Component Analysis (PCA); DBSCAN clustering; Coope circle-fitting algorithm; Sensitivity analysis on parameters
data_type: Point cloud; BIM model; Synthetic data
data_size: Two datasets: Living space scene with 160 BIM elements (127 real, 33 synthetic); Usher Institute construction scene with 75 MEP elements (63 built, 12 planned)
future_research_directions: Search and evaluate machine learning-based methods to improve detection robustness; Develop edge or cloud computing infrastructure for near real-time computation; Enable real-time access to mesh data directly on MR devices; Develop 3D deep learning model for semantic segmentation of MEP components; Design specialized helmet-integrated device with upward-facing camera for ceiling capture
digital_technology: BIM; Large language model; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Quality improvement; Automation of manual task; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Quality control; Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: Among four individual methods (Bounding Box Occupation, Point-to-Surface Distance, Voxel Occupation, Feature Matching), Methods 1 and 2 achieved the highest accuracy and recall (0.99) on the controlled dataset, but Method 1 was significantly faster. The combined Method 1∪4 (BBOX Occupation + Feature Matching) achieved the best overall performance with accuracy 0.993, precision 0.992, recall 1.0, and execution time of 5.7×10⁻⁴ seconds per element. On real construction data from the Usher Institute, Method 1∪4 maintained acceptable performance (accuracy 0.853, precision 0.880, recall 0.860) and successfully flagged missing ventilation duct heads and significant deviations.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Project manager
quantified_benefit: Method 1∪4 achieved 0.993 accuracy, 0.992 precision, and 1.0 recall with 5.7×10⁻⁴ seconds processing time per MEP element on synthetic dataset; 0.853 accuracy on real construction data
research_sponsor: McLaughlin & Harvey Construction Ltd
industry_involvement: McLaughlin & Harvey Construction Ltd provided support for the research and supplied the BIM model and access to the Usher Institute construction site at the University of Edinburgh for real-world dataset validation.
limitations_acknowledged: The captured environmental mesh frequently exhibits ghosting, holes, and missing sections, particularly on reflective MEP surfaces, and scanning quality degrades beyond 3 m distance, especially for ceiling elements. The methods cannot handle situations where as-built layouts significantly differ from BIM designs, which is common when builders adjust on-site without updating models. API constraints prevent real-time access to mesh vertex data on the HoloLens 2, requiring offline desktop evaluation rather than live deployment.

Author full names: Hisham Said
Title: Modeling and likelihood prediction of prefabrication feasibility for electrical construction firms
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Electrical contracting firms have consistently pursued industrializing their business through the implementation of prefabrication, lean production, and supply chain integration. Despite the pioneering prefabrication efforts of electrical contractors, there is still no clear understanding of the determinants of electrical prefabrication feasibility in terms of supply chain collaboration and surrounding industry requirements. Accordingly, this paper presents the development of prediction models of prefabrication feasibility for electrical contractors. The research tasks of this study were performed in three main phases. The first phase included various data collection tasks, including conducting semistructured interviews, providing an online questionnaire, and obtaining relevant local economic data of the respondents' metropolitan areas. The second phase involved the utilization of the collected questionnaire responses and economic data to develop logistic regression models that relate the prefabrication feasibility to its statistically significant determinants. The third phase involved validating the developed models by assessing their prediction accuracy and performing extensive sensitivity analysis of their determinants. The findings of this study should prove useful to electrical contractors who need to obtain data-driven assessment of prefabrication feasibility considering their business attributes and surrounding industry parameters. This study presents the two main contributions to the study field of construction prefabrication. First, prefabrication feasibility was found to be significantly dependent on four industry-related determinants: regional economic growth, industry competition, labor cost rate, and worker union resistance. Second, prefabrication feasibility was found to be significantly dependent on two main internal firm-related determinants: building information modeling capability and supply coordination with vendors.
Author Keywords: Construction materials and method
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper is explicitly and centrally about electrical contractors, electrical prefabrication, and the electrical construction industry, drawing on NECA membership and IBEW union context.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper uses statistical modeling (logistic regression) rather than AI, but BIM capability is identified as a key determinant of prefabrication feasibility, engaging with digital technology as a substantive variable.
relevance_notes: Primary method is statistical (logistic regression) rather than AI/ML, but BIM is a central firm-related determinant and the paper is highly relevant to the electrical contractor audience (NECA/ELECTRI).
research_problem: Electrical contractors have increasingly pursued prefabrication as a production strategy, yet there is no clear understanding of what determines prefabrication feasibility in terms of supply chain collaboration and surrounding industry requirements. Prior research on electrical contracting has examined scheduling, change orders, BIM use, and prefabrication best practices, but has not investigated how industry-level parameters (economic conditions, labor unions, competition) and firm-level supply chain integration jointly affect prefabrication feasibility. Contractors therefore lack data-driven means to assess whether prefabrication is feasible given their business attributes and local industry context.
research_objective: To identify industry-related and firm-related determinants of electrical prefabrication feasibility and develop logistic regression models that predict its likelihood.
specific_methods: Semi-structured interviews; Online questionnaire survey; Logistic regression (forward and backward stepwise); Multicollinearity analysis (VIF, Pearson correlation); Prediction accuracy validation; One-way sensitivity analysis
data_type: Survey responses; Interview transcripts; Economic indicators
data_size: 78 usable questionnaire responses from electrical contractors (from 84 received) plus 12 semi-structured interviews; 70 entries used for model estimation and 8 for testing
future_research_directions: Collect a larger sample of electrical contractors to improve model confidence; Develop an online prefabrication feasibility assessment tool with user interface; Continuously validate and improve models using new contractor data; Provide data-driven prefabrication recommendations tailored to individual firms; Perform detailed modeling of variables currently treated as binary (e.g., BIM, vendor partnerships)
value_proposition: Decision support; Cost reduction; Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Prefabrication feasibility; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: Prefabrication feasibility is significantly dependent on four industry-related determinants (regional economic growth/unemployment, industry competition measured by employees per firm, electricians' hourly wage, and union acceptance of outsourcing) and two firm-related determinants (BIM capability and supply coordination with vendors). The backward stepwise logistic regression model (Model B) achieved a 90.0% correct classification rate on the estimation sample, while both Model A and Model B achieved 75% accuracy on the 8-case external validation set. BIM capability was the most strongly significant predictor (p=0.0003), and union resistance to outsourcing (average -1.1) was greater than resistance to prefabrication itself (0.526).
contractor_role_impacted: Business owner or executive; Project manager; BIM coordinator or VDC manager
quantified_benefit: Model B correctly classified 90.0% of observed cases (87.5% of non-prefabrication and 92.1% of prefabrication cases); 75% accuracy on external validation
research_sponsor: ELECTRI International (Foundation for Electrical Construction)
industry_involvement: ELECTRI International sponsored the study and a task force of electrical construction, supply, and manufacturing professionals provided initial input, guidance, and validation of results. Twelve semi-structured interviews were conducted with seven contractors, one vendor, three manufacturers, and one NECA local chapter executive director, and the questionnaire was distributed via the NECA email list of ~2,925 firms.

Author full names: Ang Li, Boyu Wang, Xingbo Gong, Fangli Hou, Xingyu Tao, Jun Ma, Jack C.P. Cheng
Title: Multi-agent integration of LLMs and graph-based method for automated MEP constructability checking
Abstract: Manual constructability checking of complex mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) designs is inefficient and error-prone. However, automating this process is hindered by the complexity of MEP drawings, the variety of design specifications, and the insufficient integration of checking algorithms with fragmented checking processes. Addressing these issues, this paper proposes an automated checking system that integrates large language models (LLMs) and graph technologies within a collaborative multi-agent framework. The system comprises three components: the MEP graph agent that constructs structured scene graphs from drawings; the MEP rule agent that converts textual rules into knowledge graphs; and the MEP checking agent that identifies violation via graph matching and reasoning. Validated on three real-world projects, the system achieved an average accuracy of 92% in violation detection. By offering an intuitive interface for issue identification, this approach significantly enhances coordination efficiency and reduces manual effort. This framework establishes a robust foundation for future AI-driven automated compliance checking in the construction domain.
Author Keywords: Constructability checking; Graph-based method; LLM; MEP design; Multi-agent
Funding Details: Innovation and Technology Fund, ITF, (ITS/394/23FP)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly with electrical elements (cable trays, power/weak current trays, electrical conduits) explicitly included as a substantial part of the constructability checking scope, including region-specific electrical codes.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops an LLM-based multi-agent framework integrated with graph-based methods and computer vision for automated constructability checking.
relevance_notes: Electrical work is treated as one of several MEP systems rather than the primary focus, but the method explicitly handles electrical elements and codes (EMSD, NEC, IEC 61537, etc.).
research_problem: Manual constructability checking of complex MEP designs is inefficient and error-prone, with designers reviewing over 100 cross-sections per project. Existing automated approaches are hindered by the complexity and high density of MEP drawings, the diversity of design specifications across regions and disciplines, and the insufficient integration of checking algorithms with fragmented checking processes. Current methods focus on architectural elements, neglect quantitative spatial relationships needed for constructability, and lack formalized frameworks to translate natural language rules into computable representations.
research_objective: To develop an automated MEP constructability checking system that extracts design information from drawings, formalizes constructability requirements, and identifies violations using an LLM-based multi-agent framework with graph techniques.
specific_methods: LLM-based multi-agent framework; Scene graph construction; Knowledge graph representation; VF2 subgraph isomorphism algorithm; Rule-based graph reasoning (IF-THEN); Hybrid LLM + OpenCV pixel extraction; Case study validation; Confusion matrix and R2/MAPE evaluation

data_size: 60 cross-sections from 3 real-world projects (20 per project)
future_research_directions: Extend analysis from 2D cross-sections to sequences for 3D path-dependent rules; Validate rules like pipe slopes and overall system routing in 3D; Expand knowledge base to include more domain-specific MEP regulations; Integrate checking agent with external engineering simulation tools; Incorporate human-in-the-loop mechanisms to mitigate LLM hallucination; Add hybrid deterministic verification for safety-critical checks

key_findings: The MEP graph agent achieved over 96% node accuracy and over 92% edge accuracy in extracting MEP elements and relationships, substantially outperforming naive LLM (71-74% node) and OpenCV (77-79% node) baselines. The MEP rule agent achieved over 99% accuracy in translating natural language rules into knowledge graph representations across different regions, elements, and severity levels. The MEP checking agent attained near-flawless accuracy (90-100%) on Level 1 structural checks, 85-95% on Level 2 reasoning tasks, and R2 values of 0.84-0.91 with MAPE of 6.1-8.1% on Level 3 quantitative tasks, at a cost of approximately $0.1-$0.2 and 16.4 seconds per cross-section for the most complex tasks.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer; Project manager
quantified_benefit: Average 92% accuracy in violation detection across three real-world projects; MEP graph agent achieved >96% node and >92% edge identification accuracy
research_sponsor: Innovation and Technology Fund, HKSAR (No. ITS/394/23FP)
industry_involvement: Three real-world Hong Kong projects (Tung Chung West Station MTR, O·PARK2 organic waste treatment facility, HJT Commercial Complex) provided cross-section drawings for validation, but no explicit industry advisory panel or task force is reported.
codes_addressed__codes_details: Paper extensively references regional electrical, mechanical, and plumbing codes including Hong Kong EMSD Code of Practice for Electricity (Wiring) Regulations, BSRIA Guide BG55/2018, ArchSD General Specification; Mainland China GB 50016 (fire protection), GB 50738 (ventilation), JGJ/T 16 (electrical design of civil buildings), GB 50168 (cable line installation), GB 50015 (water supply/drainage), GB 51348 (electrical design); European EN 1507, IEC 61537 (cable trays), IEC 60364 (low-voltage installations), EN 50174 (IT cabling), EN 752, BS EN 1610, EN 12056; US SMACNA, NFPA 70 (NEC), NEC 300, NFPA 90A, IPC 2021.

limitations_acknowledged: The framework analyzes 2D cross-sections individually, omitting longitudinal relationships between them, so 3D path-dependent rules such as pipe slopes and overall system routing cannot be validated. The system was validated only on common constructability rules and has not been extended to more domain-specific regulations or integrated with external engineering simulation tools. The probabilistic nature of LLMs introduces hallucination risks that remain a concern for safety-critical engineering checks despite high measured accuracy.

Author full names: Shiva Pooladvand, Sogand Hasanzadeh
Title: Neurophysiological evaluation of workers' decision dynamics under time pressure and increased mental demand
Abstract: Productivity demands, time pressure, and cognitively demanding construction tasks increase workers' arousal and stress; however, it is not clear how these factors may worsen risk compensation effects and lead to unanticipated hazards. This paper examined the extent to which limited time and increased mental load amplify the risk compensatory behavior of workers. Using an immersive mixed-reality environment to simulate an electrical construction task, this paper applied a promising neuroimaging approach (functional near-infrared spectroscopy- fNIRS) to study changes in individuals' cognitive responses and decision dynamics under normal and stressful conditions. The results showed that workers failed to process surrounding information due to limited cognitive resources, and misperceived potential risks under time pressure and increased mental demand. These cognitive failures then led to increased overreliance on safety protections and at-risk decisions, and decreased safety performance. This paper offers valuable insights into the potential neural mechanism driving risk-taking and risk compensatory behaviors and the importance of counteracting risk compensation bias in the construction industry.
Author Keywords: Construction safety; Decision dynamics; fNIRS; Mental demand; Mixed reality; Risk compensation; Risk perception; Risk-taking behaviors; Time pressure; Virtual reality; Working memory
Funding Details: ELECTRI International; National Science Foundation, NSF, (2049842, 2049711)

relevance_notes: Strong fit for both electrical construction focus and digital technology (MR/VR + neurophysiological sensing).
research_problem: While safety interventions are widely deployed in construction, their effectiveness may be undermined by risk compensation bias where workers take more risks when protected. Although time pressure and mental demand are known to affect safety behaviors, it remains unclear how these external stressors amplify risk compensatory behaviors, particularly in high-risk electrical work. The neural mechanisms underlying such stressed decision-making in construction have not been empirically investigated.

data_size: 33 participants (22 male, 11 female) across 3 experimental conditions
future_research_directions: Repeat experiment with electrical workers of varying experience levels; Assess peer effect impacts on decision-making under risk; Investigate varying intensity of time pressure and task complexity; Increase sample size to further examine gender impacts; Study risk compensation at group or organizational level
digital_technology: IoT or sensors; Mixed reality; Virtual reality; Wearables
value_proposition: Safety improvement; Training effectiveness; Workforce wellbeing
pain_point_addressed: Safety or electrocution or arc flash; Skilled labor shortage or training
key_findings: Under time pressure and added cognitive demand, subjects showed significantly increased activation in the prefrontal cortex (particularly right DLPFC) and motor cortex, indicating higher mental workload. Safety performance declined by 13% under time pressure alone and by 42% under combined time pressure and cognitive demand, with more simulated arc flash accidents occurring despite unchanged safety protections. Workers overrelied on protective equipment and misperceived electrical risks when stressed, demonstrating amplified risk compensatory behaviors driven by limited working memory resources and a focus on gains over losses.

limitations_acknowledged: The subjects were students with related experiences rather than professional electrical workers. Although multiple neuropsychophysiological responses were collected, only fNIRS neuroimaging data was analyzed and discussed in this paper.

Author full names: Robert Lopez, Heap-Yih Chong, Conrad Pereira
Title: Obstacles preventing the off-site prefabrication of timber and MEP services: Qualitative analyses from builders and suppliers in Australia
Abstract: Limited empirical and qualitative studies focus on the detailed processes and obstacles for coordinating off-site prefabrication between builders and suppliers. This research aims to identify and address the obstacles that currently prevent the further expansion of off-site prefabrication, with a research scope on timber and mechanical/electrical/plumbing (MEP) services in construction projects. The focal point of this research is to highlight their obstacles. A total of forty interviews were conducted and analyzed from four builders' organizations and four suppliers' organizations to ascertain their obstacles in coordinating the practice of off-site prefabrication. The results found the builder's obstacles were sustainability, quality assurance (QA), mass production, CAD/BIM, technological support, commercial arrangements, system building, buffering in supply, schedule monitoring, productivity, flexibility, engagement, risks, and multiple supply arrangements. The supplier's obstacles were design, financing and subcontracting, coordination, recognized practices, risks, multiple supply arrangements, and constraints. Moreover, the builders and suppliers had identified some ways to harmonize off-site prefabrication of timber. Some examples of timber prefabrication technology include joinery, doors and/or windows, structural floor/wall/roof frames, partitions, trusses, stairs, balustrades, and others. MEP services with in situ construction comprise the use of power sources and working coordination. The most important outcome of this investigation is that these obstacles can be addressed through collaboration and coordination. This is because there is a traditionally a lack of collaboration amongst builders and their suppliers. Furthermore, there is a lack of coordination between them in general. The research contributes to the improved timber and MEP services collaboration and coordination in off-site prefabrication, which can be referred to by other approaches of modular construction.
Author Keywords: Australia; builder; collaboration; construction; coordination; in situ; MEP; off-site; on-site; prefabrication; supplier; timber

Prior studies addressed defects in completed timber modules or algorithms for on-site MEP assembly, but did not address obstacles in the off-site prefabrication processes themselves or the lack of collaboration and coordination between builders and suppliers.

Supplier obstacles included design, financing and subcontracting, coordination, recognized practices, risks, multiple supply arrangements, and constraints. The most important finding is that these obstacles can be addressed through improved collaboration and coordination, since traditionally there is a lack of both between builders and their suppliers. Earlier engagement of suppliers in the digital design phase would enhance project integration and coordination.

Author full names: Shuju Jing, Xiangyang Li, Michael Bekele Maru, Byoungjoon Yu, Gichun Cha, Seunghee Park
Title: Occlusion-aware hybrid learning framework for point cloud understanding in building mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems
Abstract: The semantic segmentation of point clouds in building MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) systems provides a system-level foundation for digital modeling and energy management. However, the densely interwoven and suspended nature of MEP components leads to fixed, irreversible occlusion patterns in point cloud data, introducing inductive biases that impair model generalization and segmentation accuracy. To address this, we propose a hybrid perception synergistic learning framework that integrates heterogeneous data sources. First, occlusion-aware synthetic point clouds are generated based on a multi-view convex hull algorithm, introducing compensation and loss simulation for the occluded regions in real point clouds, providing the model with enhanced implicit geometric priors. Then, a multi-granularity deep learning method, MEP-PNeXt, is proposed to establish a feature extraction mechanism that spans from global uniform sampling to local topology preservation. This approach reduces the reliance on similar geometric patterns caused by occlusion, while enhancing occlusion-aware topological modeling and the understanding of fine-grained features. Finally, the synergistic framework conducts hybrid training on MEP-PNeXt using both real and occlusion-aware data, followed by testing and transfer to real-world scenarios. Experimental results show that the framework yields a cumulative improvement of 12.99 % in mean intersection over union. The enhanced robustness and segmentation performance are particularly notable in small components (conduit and hangers) and heavily occluded tubular elements (round duct and pipe). This technology can be integrated into as-built MEP digital twins and automated inspection systems, facilitating the advancement of intelligent building maintenance and energy efficiency optimization.
Author Keywords: and plumbing systems; Building mechanical; Deep learning; electrical; Point clouds; Semantic segmentation; Synthetic data
Funding Details: National Research Foundation of Korea, NRF; Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport, MOLIT; Korea Agency for Infrastructure Technology Advancement, KAIA; Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea, MSIT, (RS-2024-00336270); Ministry of Education, MOE, (RS-2023-00249837, RS-2020-KA158708, TechnologyRS-2020-KA158708)

Existing MEP segmentation methods lack robust occlusion-awareness and local topological representation capabilities, and no systematic framework integrates heterogeneous data sources to optimize deep learning for MEP systems. Additionally, annotated MEP point cloud datasets are scarce, and existing synthetic data approaches focus on general indoor scenes rather than heavily occluded MEP configurations.

MEP-PNeXt outperformed PointNeXt by 11.61% in mIoU, with IoU gains particularly notable for small components (conduit +4.38%, hangers +3.77%) and heavily occluded tubular elements (pipe +4.16%, round duct +11.66%). Author full names: Boyu Wang, Zhengyi Chen, Mingkai Li, Qian Wang, Chao Yin, Jack C.P. Cheng
Title: Omni-Scan2BIM: A ready-to-use Scan2BIM approach based on vision foundation models for MEP scenes
Abstract: Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems play a crucial role in providing various services and creating comfortable environments for urban residents. In order to enhance the management efficiency of these highly complex MEP systems, as-built building information models (BIMs) are being increasingly adopted worldwide. As-built BIMs accurately represent the actual conditions of facilities, making as-built BIM reconstruction significantly important for construction progress tracking, quality assurance, subsequent facility management, and renewal. To create as-built BIMs for MEP systems, laser scanners are widely utilized to capture high-resolution images and dense 3D measurements of the environment in a fast and highly accurate manner. Despite research efforts to automatically achieve "Scan-to-BIM," there are still gaps in applying current solutions to real-world scenarios. One of the major challenges are the limited generalization of existing methods to unseen scenarios without proper training or fine-tuning on custom-designed datasets. To address this issue, this study introduces Omni-Scan2BIM, a novel approach powered by large-scale pre-trained vision foundation models. Omni-Scan2BIM enables the recognition of MEP-related components with a single shot by integrating an all-purpose feature extraction model and a class-agnostic segmentation model. Firstly, given only a single image with a reference mask, the visual features are extracted for both the target component and the collected on-site images using the vision foundation model DINOv2. Secondly, through comparing pixel features, similarity maps are generated for the on-site images. The prior points for the class-agnostic segmentation model are sampled from the local maxima of the similarity map. Thirdly, Segment Anything Model (SAM) is leveraged to sequentially segment the target component. Finally, the target component is segmented out in 3D space based on the transformation matrix describing the spatial relationship between the 2D images and the 3D point clouds. Shape analysis and label fusion are conducted for as-built BIM modeling purpose. To validate the feasibility of the proposed technique, experiments were conducted using data collected from a real construction site in Hong Kong. The results demonstrate that the proposed Omni-Scan2BIM approach can easily generalize to unseen components with significantly improved accuracy and efficiency.
Author Keywords: As-built modeling; Building information model (BIM); One-shot learning; Point clouds; Scan-to-BIM; Segment anything; Vision foundation model
Funding Details: China State Construction Engineering; China Postdoctoral Science Foundation, (2023M740761); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) for as-built BIM reconstruction, with electrical components being one of several MEP system types but not specifically singled out as the primary focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies vision foundation models (DINOv2, SAM), deep learning, laser scanning, point cloud processing, and BIM reconstruction as its primary contribution.
relevance_notes: Paper focuses on MEP-wide Scan2BIM with validation on slim pipes and ducts (mechanical/plumbing components shown in examples); electrical conduit/cable trays not explicitly demonstrated though method would generalize.
research_problem: As-built BIMs for MEP systems differ substantially from as-designed BIMs due to frequent design changes and installation deviations, requiring labor-intensive manual reconstruction. Existing deep learning Scan2BIM methods cannot generalize to unseen scenes without retraining or fine-tuning on custom-designed datasets, while traditional geometric methods struggle with non-Manhattan layouts, slim pipes, and rectangular ducts. There is no ready-to-use approach that bridges as-built imagery and point clouds to as-built BIMs without scene-specific training.
research_objective: To develop Omni-Scan2BIM, a ready-to-use Scan2BIM approach leveraging vision foundation models that recognizes MEP components from a single annotated sample image without training or fine-tuning.
specific_methods: DINOv2 feature extraction; Segment Anything Model (SAM); Cosine similarity mapping; Sequential prompt-based segmentation; RANSAC cylinder fitting; Laplacian-based contraction skeleton extraction; 2D-to-3D label propagation via transformation matrices; Cross-section verification
data_size: 29 laser scanning stations covering a university student apartment construction site; 6 HDR images (2048x2048) per setup; ~82.5 m of slim pipes and 21.3 m of ducts evaluated
future_research_directions: Develop few-shot version of Omni-Scan2BIM for varied lighting and material conditions; Establish quantitative thresholds for image resolution requirements; Address occlusion via deduction approach using as-designed BIM information; Apply method for as-designed model updating to as-built positions; Extend to construction progress tracking applications; Apply to engineering quality inspection workflows
digital_technology: BIM; Laser scanning or LiDAR; Point cloud processing
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Quality improvement; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Quality control; Commissioning or closeout
key_findings: Omni-Scan2BIM successfully reconstructed 75.2 m of 82.5 m slim pipes (recall 91.2%, precision 98.9%) and 19.4 m of 21.3 m ducts (recall 91.1%, precision 100%) using only a single annotated sample image per component class. The method significantly outperformed commercial software EdgeWise, which only recognized 24.5 m of slim pipes (recall 29.7%). Failure cases were attributed to appearance variation between sample and target images (e.g., insulation material differences, lighting variation in confined MEP rooms).
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 91.2% recall and 98.9% precision for slim pipe reconstruction; 91.1% recall and 100% precision for duct reconstruction, vs 29.7% recall for commercial EdgeWise software
research_sponsor: China State Construction Engineering (Hong Kong) Limited and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2023M740761)
industry_involvement: First author is affiliated with China State Construction Science and Technology Limited; data was collected from a real construction site of a university student apartment in Hong Kong, suggesting industry-academic collaboration.
limitations_acknowledged: The one-shot version relies on a single sample image which cannot capture variability in appearance or illumination, leading to false negatives when target objects differ in material (e.g., bronze vs. white-insulated pipes) or lighting conditions. The method's recognition performance is influenced by image quality and occlusion in densely packed MEP components, and the three proposed application directions (as-designed updating, progress tracking, quality inspection) require further investigation.

Author full names: Edgar Pestana, Andrew Paice, Shaun West
Title: Optimizing MEP design in early AEC projects through generative design
Abstract: The digital transformation of the AEC industry through BIM has improved productivity during detailed design and construction planning phases. Early design choices influence a project's success but have yet to benefit from BIM-based approaches. This paper investigates the feasibility and acceptance of employing Generative Design (GD) to optimize early Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) designs in residential real estate for space efficiency. Interviews indicate the main issue is acceptance due to the belief that a GD approach needs to be more robust. BIM is integrated with GD, utilizing the architectural layout (IFC) as input to generate design variants tailored to minimize technical space while ensuring installation feasibility. Robustness is assessed via Monte Carlo Simulation, revealing an estimated success rate of 99% (81% with 95% confidence). These results quantify the robustness of the approach, paving the way to broader acceptance of GD in the early phases of AEC projects.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling; Early AEC project phase; Generative design; Monte Carlo robustness demonstration; Residential buildings
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) design as a unified category in early-phase residential building projects, with electrical as one of three systems but not the central focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and validates a Generative Design approach using a Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-II) integrated with BIM for MEP space optimization.
relevance_notes: Despite 'Electrical' being in the MEP acronym, the paper's stakeholder interviews identified ventilation, heating, and plumbing as the disciplines with the most significant space impact, so electrical is mentioned but not deeply addressed in the prototype.
research_problem: Early-phase MEP design decisions strongly influence AEC project success, but BIM requires considerably more effort than conventional planning during conceptual design. Defining adequate technical space reservations (rooms, shafts, horizontal distribution) iteratively with architects takes weeks per iteration, making it impractical to evaluate multiple feasible variants. While Generative Design has been applied in other phases, industry skepticism about its robustness has prevented its adoption in early AEC project phases.
research_objective: To investigate the feasibility and acceptance of using Generative Design to optimize early MEP designs in residential real estate for space efficiency, and to demonstrate algorithmic robustness.
specific_methods: Generative Design; Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-II); Monte Carlo Simulation; Principal Component Analysis; Stakeholder interviews; Multi-case analysis; V-Model Systems Engineering
data_type: BIM model; Interview transcripts; Simulation output
data_size: 9000 Monte Carlo random samples across 9 BIM building models; 5 expert interviews
future_research_directions: Apply alternative optimization approaches or machine learning heuristics; Improve robustness estimation using non-Gaussian distributions; Extend algorithm to additional MEP disciplines; Migrate to open-source cloud-based digital service; Extend to other building types such as hotels, offices, and mixed-use
ai_technique: Evolutionary or genetic algorithm; Statistical modeling
value_proposition: Decision support; Automation of manual task; Coordination or clash reduction
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery
key_findings: The BIM-GD prototype produced valid solutions in 99.1% of 9000 Monte Carlo samples, with a 95% confidence lower bound of 80.7% success rate. The Genetic Algorithm was classified as widely applicable across building variants and tolerant to parameter values, with most performance gains achieved by the 130th generation in under 12 hours of computation. Visual comparison with B&H detailed designs confirmed acceptable similarity, and the approach saved at least 45% of the time required versus traditional methods.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer; Project manager
quantified_benefit: 99.1% success rate (80.7% with 95% confidence); at least 45% time savings versus traditional methods
research_sponsor: Joint innovation project between Lucerne University of Applied Sciences and Arts (iHomeLab Research Center) and Basler & Hofmann
industry_involvement: Basler & Hofmann, a Swiss consultancy and engineering firm, was a co-developer of the project; five B&H MEP experts were interviewed to define requirements, B&H provided BIM project models for the multi-case analysis, and B&H experts validated the visual comparison between parametric and detailed designs.
codes_addressed__codes_details: VDI 2050 (German standard for technical equipment room space and service shaft requirements) used to define space variables and calculation procedures; SIA 2024 (Swiss standard for room utilization data for energy and building technology) used for typical power and energy values; SIA 31 referenced for project phase time benchmarks.
limitations_acknowledged: The solutions found represent local optima rather than global optima, as different seed values produce different best solutions with similar fitness. The use of Revit's built-in GD algorithm limited opportunities to optimize the genetic algorithm or try alternative optimization approaches. The results are limited to residential apartment buildings in Switzerland and do not cover all building shape variants (e.g., star, round, atrium).

Author full names: Carrie S. Dossick, Gina Neff
Title: Organizational divisions in bim-enabled commercial construction
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: Proponents claim that the adoption of building information modeling (BIM) will lead to greater efficiencies through increased collaboration. In this paper, we present research that examines the use of BIM technologies for mechanical, electrical, plumbing, and fire life safety systems (often referred to as MEP) coordination and how the introduction of BIM influences collaboration and communication. Using data from over 12 months of ethnographic observations of the MEP coordination process for two commercial construction projects and interviews with 65 industry leaders across the United States, we find that BIM-enabled projects are often tightly coupled technologically, but divided organizationally. This means that while BIM makes visible the connections among project members, it is not fostering closer collaboration across different companies. We outline the competing obligations to scope, project, and company as one cause for this division. Obligations to an individual scope of work or to a particular company can conflict with project goals. Individual leadership, especially that of the MEP coordinator in the teams we studied, often substitutes for stronger project cohesion and organization. Organizational forces and structures must be accounted for in order for BIM to be implemented successfully.
Author Keywords: Communication; Constructability; Construction industry; Electrical systems; Information systems; Information technology; Integrated systems; Leadership; Mechanical systems

relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally examines Building Information Modeling (BIM) adoption and its organizational implications for MEP coordination.

While BIM technologically couples project participants by integrating their models, organizational structures separate design and construction teams, limiting information flow and decision-making. The paper investigates why BIM-enabled MEP coordination teams remain organizationally divided despite tighter technological coupling.

The authors identify three competing obligations—to scope, project, and company—that undermine collaboration goals, particularly when subcontractor detailers are organizationally separated from architects and engineers who hold needed information and decision-making power. General contractors rely heavily on individual leadership (especially the MEP coordinator) as a substitute for organizational structures that would enable collaboration, a strategy that works only when strong leadership is present.

Author full names: E.A. Pärn, D.J. Edwards, Michael C.P. Sing
Title: Origins and probabilities of MEP and structural design clashes within a federated BIM model
Abstract: 'Design clashes' encountered during the development of a large multi-storey educational building, awarded under a Joint Contracts Tribunal (JCT) Design and Build contract, are reported upon. The building was developed in Birmingham, UK and the contract value was circa £36 million (UK Sterling, 2015). Members of the project management team (PMT) produced designs that were subsequently integrated by the main contractor into a federated building information modelling (BIM) model; at this stage 404 error clashes were evident between the positions of the mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) designer's and structural designer's building compartments. The contractor deemed that these particular clashes were 'mission critical' as previous experience suggested that project costs could spiral uncontrollably if left unabated. Participatory action research was employed to acquire a deeper understanding and knowledge of the clash incidents. Clash data accrued (in mm) was subsequently quantitatively modelled using the probability density function (PDF) and the cumulative distribution function (CDF). Two models produced were the Log Logistic Three Parameter (3P) (using all data including outliers) and Generalized Gamma distribution (excluding outliers). Both models satisfied Anderson-Darling and Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness of fit tests at α 0.01 and 0.02 levels of significance. Model parameters could be used to forecast similar clashes occurring on future projects and will prove invaluable to PMT members when accurately estimating the time and resource needed to integrate BIM designs. The predictive modelling revealed that 92.98% of clashes reside within the 30–299 mm range while the most probable occurrence of a clash overlap resides in a discrete category of 100–199 mm. Further qualitative investigation is also conducted to understand why these clashes occurred and propagate ideas about how such may be mitigated. The research concludes on two important points, namely: i) BIM is not a panacea to design related construction project rework and that innovative 21st century digital technologies are hampered by 20th century management practices; and ii) improvements in clash and error mitigation reside in a better understanding of tolerances specified to alleviate the erroneous task of resolving unnecessary clashes. Future research is proposed that seeks to: automate the clash detection management, analysis and resolution process; conduct further investigative analysis of the organizational and human resource management influences impacting upon design clash propagation; and devise and validate new procedural methods to mitigate clash occurrence using a real-life project.
Author Keywords: Building information modelling; Clash detection; Cumulative distribution function; Generalized Gamma distribution; Log Logistic (3P) distribution; Probability density function

Prior research on BIM-based clash detection remains anecdotal or limited in scope, and there is little understanding of why clashes propagate despite BIM adoption. The contractor on this project deemed MEP-vs-structural clashes mission critical because past projects showed that unresolved clashes cause project costs to spiral.

Author full names: Ghang Lee, Jonghoon Walter Kim
Title: Parallel vs. Sequential cascading MEP coordination strategies: A pharmaceutical building case study
Abstract: We analyze the impact of parallel vs. sequential design coordination strategies on coordination productivity and information sharing. Previous studies have shown how building information modeling (BIM) could improve interorganizational design coordination between architecture, structure, and mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) components of buildings (MEP coordination, for short) and thus improve the quality and efficiency of a design and construction project in terms of the reduced numbers of errors and requests for information. This paper presents a unique case where two MEP coordinators were hired for a BIM-assisted project, which was a pharmaceutical company headquarters office building in Silver Spring, Maryland. The first coordinator coordinated MEP designs concurrently with other trades, whereas the second coordinator coordinated MEP designs step-by-step in a sequential process. The results of our analysis showed that the two different coordination processes largely affect the number of clashes in the first run of clash detection, coordination meeting time and efficiency, ease in finding root causes of the clashes, and number of coordination cycles to complete the coordination. As such, the sequential coordination strategy was about three times faster than the parallel strategy in terms of coordination productivity. A further examination of these two processes from an information-sharing perspective showed that the sequential coordination process reduces the concentration of information, thus reducing the overload of a coordinator with decision-making tasks, and facilitates information sharing between heterogeneous project participants. The findings of this study have potential as a basis for future development of BIM-based MEP coordination best practices and strategies as well as providing the metrics for understanding, measuring, and predicting the performance of BIM-based MEP coordination and strategically planning the coordination process.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Design coordination; Information sharing; Parallel process; Productivity; Sequential cascading process
Funding Details: Architecture & Urban Development Research Program; Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport, MOLIT

Prior studies noted that expert coordinators tend to work sequentially, yet concurrent engineering theory favors parallel activities. No quantitative comparison of these two strategies within a single project had been reported, leaving best practices for BIM-based MEP coordination process design underspecified.

The sequential process produced fewer than 10 clashes per first-run detection versus more than 50 in the parallel process, shortened coordination meetings to one-third their duration, and reduced coordination cycles. DEMA network analysis showed that the parallel process concentrated information flow on the GC-MEP coordinator (52% of reusable information received), overloading decision-making, whereas the sequential process distributed information more evenly across project participants.

Author full names: Tushar Jadhav, Rahul Deshpande
Title: Pedagogy of teaching MEP to quantity surveying students
Abstract: The quantity surveying (QS) candidates are majorly concerned with activities such as quantification & documentation and engineering specifications & costing, among others. The QS candidates must have a comprehensive understanding of Mechanical, Electrical and Plumbing (MEP) services, as it accounts for a substantial component of building project. Educating QS candidates on MEP is quite challenging due to involvement of multiple systems and their integration. The present study therefore is an attempt to investigate the various pedagogy used for making the teaching learning process to be more effective. The author proposes an interesting pedagogy of teaching MEP services to QS candidates. The proposed pedagogy is explained in detail by discussing a case study on duct layout. The use of building information modeling (BIM) for quantification and costing of MEP services is also highlighted in this paper. The student feedback on teaching MEP using the proposed pedagogy is specified in this study. This paper provides insights towards making the MEP subject more interesting and easier for QS students.
Author Keywords: BIM; case study; costing; MEP; pedagogy; Quantity Surveying

Existing textbooks on quantity surveying and MEP do not provide specific examples of QS for MEP services, and the literature lacks a specific pedagogy for teaching MEP services to QS candidates.

Qualitative feedback indicated that site visits helped students connect fundamentals with real applications, while the manual-vs-BIM comparison helped them understand how software arrives at quantities and costs. The duct layout case study using Revit produced a total cost of INR 39,982 with parameters such as duct area, gauge, velocity, and pressure drop calculated automatically.

Author full names: Li Wang, Fernanda Leite
Title: Process knowledge capture in BIM-based mechanical, electrical, and plumbing design coordination meetings
Source title: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Abstract: In recent years, building information modeling (BIM) has been increasingly applied in design coordination of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems to enhance coordination and communication efficiency. While BIM has been mostly used to store and represent product information, process information such as discussions and decision made during design coordination is usually not properly captured. The research reported in this paper implements a previously developed knowledge representation schema to capture coordination information generated in the BIM-based design coordination process. A prototype system was developed for model-based knowledge capture and tested on a construction project. The proposed system indicates that process information can be captured and represented with direct links to the model, enabling model-based knowledge capture and further analysis of process information.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Information capture and representation; Model-based design review
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP design coordination broadly with electrical as one of several systems, demonstrated on a project with electrical, mechanical, and plumbing components.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops a BIM-based knowledge capture tool as a plugin for Autodesk Navisworks Manage to capture process information during model-based design coordination.
relevance_notes: Paper is MEP-wide rather than electrical-specific; electrical is included as one of the coordinated trades but not the focus.
research_problem: BIM has been widely used to represent product information in MEP design coordination, but process information such as discussions, decisions, and clash resolution rationale generated during coordination meetings is typically not properly captured. Documentation, when it exists, is in the form of text, marked-up drawings, or static 2D snapshots with no linkage to model components, making knowledge reuse and model-based analysis difficult. There is no standardized process or systematic structure to capture clash-relevant information, making the management of clashes tedious and inefficient.
research_objective: To develop a model-based knowledge capture system (TagPlus) that formalizes experiential and process knowledge generated during BIM-based MEP design coordination by linking process information to model components.
specific_methods: Expert interviews; Protocol analysis; Use case modeling (UML); Prototype plugin development; Retrospective test case; External usability validation

value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Knowledge management; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; RFI or change order management; VDC or BIM cost recovery
key_findings: The TagPlus prototype successfully captured 224 clashes from a real construction project's MEP coordination, linking categorized process information directly to model components rather than static 2D snapshots. The system enables searching, grouping, filtering, and exporting clash data in a computer-interpretable format, supporting better localizability, descriptiveness, and analyzability than current viewpoint-based documentation. External validation with two BIM coordinators confirmed the system's usefulness for standardizing documentation and enabling clash searching, while identifying implementation challenges around model version consistency and data input efficiency.

The current input parameters can only be modified through code changes, and the export format is limited to JSON.

Author full names: Sejun Jang, Ghang Lee
Title: Process, productivity, and economic analyses of BIM–based multi-trade prefabrication—A case study
Abstract: Previous studies yielded contradicting results regarding the benefits of multi-trade prefabrication (MTP) based on building information modeling (BIM). This study investigates the causes of the contradicting results by analyzing the process, productivity, and economic benefits of BIM-based MTP through a case study. The process analysis results indicate that coordinating mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems took longer in MTP than in the conventional method because of the newly added offsite coordination activities. Nevertheless, the overall project duration was reduced because of the parallel execution of MTP and concrete work. The productivity analysis shows that the newly added MTP activities also increased person-hours. However, as the assembly process was repeated, the required person-hours decreased by 40% from that of the initial stage because of the learning effect. The case study revealed that the management of coordination activities and the selection of projects were critical for the successful implementation of BIM-based MTP.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling (BIM); Corridor MEP rack; Multi-trade prefabrication; Prefabrication; Productivity

Existing analyses provided only high-level final reduction values or perceived savings without detailed process, productivity, and economic breakdowns. This left construction practitioners without a clear understanding of why MTP outcomes differ and what factors drive its success or failure.

key_findings: MTP increased design and coordination time by about four weeks due to added structural framing design, re-coordination, and spool drawing steps, but shortened on-site MEP work from 36 days to 3 days because fabrication ran in parallel with concrete work. Total labor input for MTP was 113.5% of the conventional baseline, with factory trade work alone reducing labor (HVAC duct −63.8%, HVAC pipe −27.4%, sanitary pipe −41.8%, electrical/IBS tray −8.1%), while structural framing and onsite assembly added 40.4% of total MTP labor. Productivity improved with repetition through a learning effect (labor input dropped from 137% on floor 5 to 95.6% on floor 8), with a learning curve y=1.389175·x^−0.2703 predicting breakeven after roughly 12 floors. Economic viability depends on project size, complexity, lower factory wages, bulk purchasing, and schedule-related incentives.

Author full names: Fangxin Li, Hengjia Yan, Xin Xu, Qiongfang Li
Title: PU-Mamba: A point cloud upsampling network to enhance semantic segmentation of MEP scenes using synthetic data
Abstract: 3D reconstruction of Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) scenes, which requires extracting both geometric and semantic information, is essential for applications such as construction monitoring, quality assessment, and facility management. While 3D laser scanning provides precise point cloud data, the dense configurations and frequent occlusions in MEP environments pose substantial challenges for semantic segmentation. To address these challenges, this study introduces a point cloud upsampling network designed to enrich sparse regions and capture fine structural details, thereby enhancing semantic segmentation performance in MEP scenes. First, a synthetic dataset generation approach is developed to mitigate the scarcity of labeled training data. Subsequently, the PU-Mamba network is proposed as an upsampling solution, leveraging the capabilities of the Mamba architecture to improve point cloud segmentation in complex MEP scenarios. Compared with the highest overall accuracy of 83.50 % of the state-of-the-art methods, experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves an overall accuracy of 88.65 % in semantic segmentation, highlighting the effectiveness of combining synthetic dataset creation with an upsampling network.
Author Keywords: MEP scenes; Point cloud upsampling; Semantic segmentation; Synthetic dataset creation

Author full names: Fukuharu Tanaka, Teruhiro Mizumoto, Hirozumi Yamaguchi
Title: Recognition of house structures from complicated electrical plan images
Abstract: Advances in image analysis and deep learning technologies have expanded the use of floor plans, traditionally used for sales and rentals, to include 3D reconstruction and automated design. However, a typical floor plan does not provide detailed information, such as the type and number of outlets and locations affecting the placement of furniture and appliances. Electrical plans, providing details on electrical installations, are intricate due to overlapping symbols and lines and remain unutilized as house manufacturers independently manage them. This paper proposes an analysis method that extracts the house structure, room semantics, connectivities, and specifics of wall and ceiling sockets from electrical plans, achieving robustness to noise and overlaps by leveraging the unique features of symbols and lines. The experiments using 544 electrical plans show that our method achieved better accuracy (+3.6 pt) for recognizing room structures than the state-of-the-art method, 87.2% in identifying room semantics and 97.7% in detecting sockets.
Author Keywords: electrical plan; floor plan analysis; machine learning
Funding Details: Japan Science and Technology Agency, JST; Core Research for Evolutional Science and Technology, CREST, (JPMJCR21M5)

When only image copies of electrical plans are available (due to lost CAD data or copyright constraints), structural, semantic, and electrical equipment information cannot be readily recovered. Existing methods cannot extract socket types/locations and struggle with obscured doors and unreadable text in such cluttered plans.

Room name identification reached 87.2% average F-measure across 14 room categories, and socket detection across 11 classes achieved a 97.7% macro-average F-measure. Author full names: Zaolin Pan, Yantao Yu, Fu Xiao, Jing Zhang
Title: Recovering building information model from 2D drawings for mechanical, electrical and plumbing systems of ageing buildings
Abstract: Ageing buildings have become a significant concern for many cities, exacerbated by inadequate management and maintenance of Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) systems. Building Information Modelling (BIM) enables efficient MEP Operation and Maintenance (O&M) through the digital representation of system information; however, many ageing buildings were constructed without BIM, and manual reconstruction is costly and inefficient due to the sheer number of such structures. Although some studies have proposed methods for automatically recovering BIM from 2D drawings, few are suitable for MEP systems due to the multiscale and irregular shapes of MEP components. To fill this gap, an automatic approach is proposed for recovering MEP model from 2D drawings with three modules: 1) semantic extraction by combining image cropping with Cascade Mask R-CNN to detect and segment multiscale, irregular MEP components; 2) geometric extraction by semantic-assisted image processing to extract contours and skeletons of irregular parts; and 3) Industry Foundation Class (IFC)-based BIM reconstruction via the open-source pythonOCC and IfcOpenShell. The performance was tested on two MEP systems with 335 and 282 multiscale and irregular elements, and the results show that the method recovered BIMs for the two MEP systems in 2.85 s and 0.79 s, with semantic extraction accuracy exceeding 0.9 and geometric error below 5%. This paper contributes to the existing body of knowledge by providing a semantic and geometric-based approach for recovering multiscale and irregular components from 2D drawings. Future studies could further improve the approach by integrating elevation drawings, reconstructing abstract symbols, and aligning text-geometry.
Author Keywords: BIM reconstruction; Building information model (BIM); Industry foundation classes (IFC); Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems; Operation and maintenance (O&M)
Funding Details: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, HKUST, (R9813); Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, HKUST; Innovation and Technology Commission - Hong Kong, (ITP/002/22LP); Innovation and Technology Commission - Hong Kong; Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation of Guangdong Province, (2022B1515130006); Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation of Guangdong Province

Existing 2D drawing-based BIM reconstruction methods focus on regular architectural/structural components and cannot simultaneously extract accurate semantics and geometry of multiscale, irregular MEP components. Prior MEP studies model components as simple rectangular bodies, ignoring actual contours, and separate geometric from semantic extraction, limiting reconstruction accuracy and applicability.

key_findings: The Cascade Mask R-CNN model achieved mAP above 0.9 for both object detection and instance segmentation across multiscale, irregular MEP components. The proposed semantic-assisted image processing kept perimeter and area errors below 5% for most components. The framework reconstructed IFC-based BIM models for two MEP systems containing 335 and 282 components in 2.85 and 0.79 seconds respectively, demonstrating accurate and efficient reconstruction.

Author full names: Yongqi Wang, Penghui Lin, Limao Zhang, Hongbo Yu, Tiong Lee Kong Robert
Title: Resilience-oriented design for complex MEP systems in BIM
Abstract: Normal operation of a Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) system under random and intentional attacks is important to a building. A systematic research framework is proposed to analyze the resilience of an MEP system and optimize its design. The resilience magnitude in this research measures the ability of the MEP system to keep standard operation when component failures appear. First, the MEP model in Building Information Modelling (BIM) environment is extracted to a graph database by using Revit API, which represents the complex network of an MEP system. Second, the importance of the components and the resilience of the MEP system is analyzed based on the network theory and topological metrics. Third, the failure simulation is carried out by attacking the node of the system randomly and intentionally. Finally, the genetic algorithm is used to optimize the design of the MEP system by adding new edges. The results show that: (i) the graph database is a good representation of the MEP system, and it can convert the 3D model to a format that can be analyzed by data analysis measures, (ii) the same component in the MEP system could have different importance from different perspectives, (iii) the proposed network is more resilient with bridge ratio index and average path length improved by 6.16% and 40.58%, respectively, and (iv) the proposed intentional attack strategy is more conform to reality, and it can cause more severe results to a system. The research can contribute to the implementation of the resilience design theory in the MEP discipline, and create a bond between the 3D model and data analysis.
Author Keywords: Genetic algorithm; Graph database; Intentional attack strategy; Network theory; Resilience
Funding Details: National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (72271101); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC; Huazhong University of Science and Technology, HUST, (3004242122); Huazhong University of Science and Technology, HUST; Hebei Province Outstanding Youth Fund, (2022CFA062); Hebei Province Outstanding Youth Fund

Existing maintenance strategies are reactive or preventive and cannot forecast damages from unanticipated conditions like natural disasters or attacks. There is no established method to convert a BIM-based MEP model into a network representation for resilience analysis and design optimization.
