specific_methods: Graph database extraction via Revit API; Network theory analysis; Degree centrality; Betweenness centrality; Average path length (APL); Genetic algorithm optimization; Random and intentional attack simulation; Bridge ratio index

future_research_directions: Add more constraints based on engineers' expertise when adding edges; Consider component location (level and space) in optimization; Include cost (material and manpower) in objective function; Use multiple objectives to obtain optimal solution; Remove unavailable node connections from variable domain

key_findings: The graph database successfully represents the MEP system, converting the 3D model into an analyzable format. The same component can have different importance from different perspectives (most critical node IDs differ by metric: 1152 by degree, 65 by betweenness, 500 by SAPL impact). The proposed optimized network with 6 added edges improved the bridge ratio index by 6.16% and average path length by 40.58%. Intentional attack strategies based on betweenness centrality cause more severe damage than random attacks.

Author full names: Ahmed Gouda Mohamed, Joseph Ehab Ghaly, Mohamed Marzouk
Title: Revolutionizing semantic integration of maintenance cost prediction for building systems using artificial neural networks
Abstract: The maintenance cost management for building services has significantly advanced with the advent of computational techniques and semantic web technologies. Building services, including HVAC, plumbing, firefighting, and electrical systems, ensure building safety and efficiency. However, predicting maintenance costs is challenging due to the complexity and variability in usage patterns and environmental conditions. To address this challenge, this study introduces a Semantically Integrated Knowledge-based Artificial Neural Network (SIKB-ANN) framework. The SIKB-ANN framework integrates Semantic Web Technology (SWT) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) to improve the accuracy of maintenance cost predictions. SWT enhances data interoperability and standardization, while ANN manages complex non-linear data relationships. The model was trained with historical maintenance data from a firm (2017–2022) and validated using metrics such as Coefficient of Determination (R2), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE). The model achieved high predictive accuracy, with R2 values of 0.986 for the test set and 0.944 for the training set, indicating its robustness and reliability. An essential contribution of this research is the innovative integration of semantic ontologies with ANN, significantly enhancing predictive capabilities and providing a structured data management framework. This approach improves prediction accuracy and supports better decision-making and resource allocation in building management. The study highlights the potential of combining semantic technologies with machine learning to address complex predictive challenges in the built environment. Future research should consider integrating real-time data streams, advanced machine learning techniques, and broader applications in facility management.
Author Keywords: Artificial neural network; Building services; Maintenace costs prediction; Semantic ontology; Semantic web technology
Funding Details: ANCOVA; Artificial Neural Network; EFA-ANN

Author full names: Lang-Tao Wu, Jia-Rui Lin, Shuo Leng, Jiu-Lin Li, Zhen-Zhong Hu
Title: Rule-based information extraction for mechanical-electrical-plumbing-specific semantic web
Abstract: Information extraction (IE), which aims to retrieve meaningful information from plain text, has been widely studied in general and professional domains to support downstream applications. However, due to the lack of labeled data and the complexity of professional mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) information, it is challenging to apply current common deep learning IE methods to the MEP domain. To solve this problem, this paper proposes a rule-based approach for MEP IE task, including a "snowball" strategy to collect large-scale MEP corpora, a suffix-based matching algorithm on text segments for named entity recognition (NER), and a dependency-path-based matching algorithm on dependency tree for relationship extraction (RE). 2 ideas called "meta linking" and "path filtering" for RE are proposed as well, to discover the out-of-pattern entities/relationships as many as possible. To verify the feasibility of the proposed approach, 65 MB MEP corpora have been collected as input of the proposed approach and an MEP semantic web which consists of 15,978 entities and 65,110 relationship triples established, with an accuracy of 81% to entities and 75% to relationship triples, respectively. A comparison experiment between classical deep learning models and the proposed rule-based approach was carried out, illustrating that the performance of our method is 37% and 49% better than the selected deep learning NER and RE models, respectively, in the aspect of extraction precision.
Author Keywords: Information extraction; MEP; Named entity recognition; Natural language understanding; Relation extraction; Rule match; Semantic web
Funding Details: HIT-SCIR; Social computing and information retrieval research center of Harbin Institute of Technology; Stanford NLP Group; Tsinghua University – Glodon Joint Research Center for Building Information Modeling; National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (51778336, 72091512); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC; Tsinghua University, THU

research_problem: There is no existing large-scale semantic web for MEP engineering because current information extraction methods face three problems: IE for MEP is tedious with huge workloads on data collection and processing, deep learning IE methods require large labeled datasets that do not exist for MEP, and deep learning approaches do not handle the complex professional information structure and language representation of the MEP domain well. This hinders the utilization of accumulated MEP knowledge stored in industry specifications, technical manuals, engineering reports, and research literature.
research_objective: To propose a rule-based information extraction approach for the MEP domain that efficiently builds an MEP semantic web with high accuracy from large-scale corpora.

The extracted semantic web showed strong connectivity with a main subgraph of 14,342 nodes (~90% of entities), indicating high coupling in the MEP domain.

Author full names: Ricardo Eiris, Anujeet Jain, Masoud Gheisari, Andrew Wehle
Title: Safety immersive storytelling using narrated 360-degree panoramas: A fall hazard training within the electrical trade context
Abstract: Safety training in the United States construction industry commonly employs classroom-based lecture and storytelling techniques to transfer knowledge to workers and professionals. However, low levels of engagement and low-fidelity representations of the construction jobsites have posed limitations for learners to easily visualize and understand hazard conditions. One emerging technology that has the potential to increase engagement and provide high-fidelity visualizations of construction jobsites is 360-degree panorama virtual environments. This study concentrates on using immersive storytelling within digital 360-degree panoramas to improve hazard recognition and risk perception. A proof-of-concept platform was developed to assess the produced virtual training environment in terms of hazard identification, risk perception, and sense of presence. The platform was conceptualized within the visual and narrative context of electrical trade fall hazards, as this trade often perform complex work at elevated surfaces making them especially susceptible to fall related injuries and fatalities. A between-subject pilot study was conducted with forty construction management student subjects, comparatively evaluating safety immersive storytelling and Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) trained participants (e.g., OSHA 10-hr, OSHA 30-hr). It was found that no statistical differences are present in the average Hazard Identification Index (HII) scores for both approaches, suggesting that the outcomes of the training techniques are equivalent for the narrow scope of fall hazards evaluated in this study (scissor/aerial lifts, scaffolds, roofs/unprotected edges). Nevertheless, time savings in hazard identification training were found; safety immersive storytelling required 15 min of training in contrast 10 or 30 h of OSHA training. Furthermore, it was detected that subjects assigned more or equal risk to ladders, scaffolds, and scissor/aerial lifts hazards for safety immersive storytelling compared to the OSHA condition. Although the subject risk perception scores demonstrate these trends, a statistical analysis performed showed no significant differences between the two experimental groups. All participants perceived that the immersive experience provided a high sense of presence. Based on the experimental results, it was concluded that safety immersive storytelling provides an analogous outcome to OSHA interventions for the studied fall hazards while reducing the required training time.
Author Keywords: 360-degree panorama; Immersive storytelling; Safety training; Virtual reality

Model-based VR addresses visualization but is computationally expensive and project-specific. Existing 360-degree panorama safety training experiences have not yet incorporated storytelling elements, leaving a gap specifically for fall hazard training in the electrical trade, which has disproportionately high fall fatality rates.

specific_methods: Between-subject pilot study; Content analysis of OSHA IMIS accident reports; Hazard Identification Index (HII); Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM); Presence Questionnaire (Witmer and Singer); Two-tailed t-tests for independent unequal variances; 360-degree panorama capture; Augmented reality hotspot interaction
data_type: Accident reports; 2D images; Survey responses; Other

future_research_directions: Evaluate safety immersive storytelling with electrical trade workers at varying expertise levels; Conduct longitudinal studies on knowledge retention; Study other construction trades and other languages such as Spanish; Assess effects of improved 360-degree image and HMD resolution; Explore immersive storytelling as a complement to OSHA training; Investigate 360-degree panoramic videos and collaborative or online learning integration

electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Not specified
key_findings: No statistically significant differences in Hazard Identification Index (HII) scores were found between safety immersive storytelling trained subjects (77.3%) and OSHA trained subjects (75.8%), indicating equivalent outcomes for the studied fall hazards (ladders, scissor/aerial lifts, scaffolds, roofs/unprotected edges). However, immersive storytelling required only 15 minutes of training versus 10 to 30 hours for OSHA training. Subjects assigned more or equal risk severity to ladder, scaffold, and lift hazards under immersive storytelling, and both groups reported high sense of presence (over 80% across all categories). Author full names: Abdullah Alsuhaibani, Fernanda Leite
Title: Scan-versus-BIM without registration for as-built verification: Verifying MEP sleeves ahead of concrete pours
Abstract: The prompt identification of deviations during construction is crucial to minimize the propagation of issues and the impact of rectifications. Scan-versus-building information modeling (scan-versus-BIM) has been the subject of many recent studies focusing on several aspects, including deviation detection and progress monitoring. However, most existing scan-versus-BIM solutions rely on registration between the three-dimensional (3D) point cloud and the as-designed 3D model, making the process time-consuming, complex, and prone to error. This limitation can hinder the practical adoption of scan-versus-BIM in construction projects. Therefore, in this study, a new and simplified scan-versus-BIM without a registration approach is proposed. The framework compares features extracted from BIM with those extracted from a point cloud to identify deviations and monitor progress. This was validated in two case studies involving the automatic verification of the locations of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) sleeves in an RC slab before pouring. Results of the case studies indicate that the proposed approach can be reliably used in checking MEP sleeves, as it correctly detected as-built sleeves and deviations when the minimum deviation threshold was set to approximately 10 mm. However, to generalize the results to more complex scenarios or other applications, further case studies and testing are required. This work contributes to the body of knowledge by offering a new perspective on comparing 3D point clouds with as-designed BIM models and presents construction practitioners with a tangible use case that is readily implementable.
Author Keywords: As-built verification; Deviation detection; Laser scanning; Progress monitoring; Scan-versus-building information modeling
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP sleeve verification broadly, which includes electrical conduit sleeves through concrete slabs, but does not specifically focus on electrical work as distinct from mechanical or plumbing.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies laser scanning, BIM, point cloud processing, and algorithms (RANSAC, DBSCAN) to automate as-built verification.
relevance_notes: Electrical relevance is indirect; MEP sleeves include electrical conduit penetrations but the paper does not distinguish electrical from mechanical/plumbing sleeves.
research_problem: Most existing scan-versus-BIM solutions for as-built verification rely on registration between the 3D point cloud and the as-designed BIM model, which is time-consuming, complex, error-prone, and hindered by noise, occlusion, and temporary objects on construction sites. This registration step creates a barrier to practical adoption. Additionally, MEP sleeve verification before concrete pours is still performed mostly manually despite being critical to preventing costly rework.
research_objective: To propose and demonstrate a simplified scan-versus-BIM approach without registration for verifying MEP sleeve size and location before concrete pours.
specific_methods: RANSAC circle and line fitting; DBSCAN clustering; Visual programming language (Dynamo); Python scripting; Laser scanning; Case study validation; Sensitivity analysis
data_size: Two case studies: a 21.5 m2 slab with 11 MEP sleeves and a 32 m2 slab with 4 sleeves
future_research_directions: Test approach on columns or vertical openings for spacing and dimensions; Conduct cost-benefit analysis versus traditional methods; Apply to other applications benefiting from early deviation detection; Validate in larger areas with smaller sleeve spacing; Test with different laser scanner models and scanned materials
value_proposition: Quality improvement; Automation of manual task; Cost reduction
pain_point_addressed: Quality control; Coordination or clash detection
key_findings: The proposed registration-free scan-versus-BIM approach reliably verified MEP sleeve locations and sizes when the deviation threshold was set to approximately 20 mm, with sensitivity analysis showing reliable performance above a 10 mm threshold. In Case Study 1, nine of eleven sleeves were correctly identified as built within tolerance, two deliberately deviated sleeves were flagged, and two intentionally unbuilt sleeves were correctly identified as missing. Total process time was approximately 3 hours for Case Study 1 and 2 hours for Case Study 2. Thresholds below 10 mm approach laser scanner accuracy limits and produce false deviation reports.
contractor_role_impacted: Field supervisor or foreman; BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: Reliable deviation detection at 20 mm threshold (conservative) and down to 10 mm; approximately 3 hours total processing time for a 21.5 m2 slab with 11 sleeves
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: Two real-world commercial construction projects in the southern United States provided site access for laser scanning, though specific industry partners are not named.
limitations_acknowledged: The approach is sensitive to laser scanner accuracy and residual noise, which may affect RANSAC fitting and change detection. It assumes circular sleeve cross sections and rectangular formwork layouts, and that sleeves and formwork edges extend higher than reinforcement; changes in these assumptions require adjustments. The approach was demonstrated only on relatively small areas (21.5 and 32 m2) with minimum 30 cm sleeve spacing, so larger areas or smaller spacings may affect results.
data_availability__availability_details: Data supporting the findings are available from the corresponding author upon request; the Dynamo script is available as supplemental material in the ASCE Library

Author full names: Christoph Emunds, Jérôme Frisch, Christoph van Treeck
Title: Spatial link prediction: Learning topological relationships in MEP systems
Abstract: In Building Information Modeling (BIM), interactions between building components are defined by parametric relationships. These relationships are critical for analyses and simulations throughout a building's life-cycle. However, many of these relationships are implicit in BIM models, posing challenges when exchanging models between different BIM authoring tools. Existing query systems and languages for BIM can partially infer or deduce missing information, incorporating semantic, geometric, and topological data to make it more accessible. Nonetheless, they often lack a holistic understanding of the building model, focusing on individual vertices, edges, and faces of components' 3D representations. In this paper, we present a novel approach for predicting connectivity relationships in mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) systems using a candidate generation procedure followed by an auto-encoder model to identify relevant component connections. Our approach leverages both the topological structure of MEP systems and the geometric attributes of system components, allowing the model to learn effectively and accurately predict potential connections. We conduct extensive experiments across various settings to evaluate the practical applicability of our approach and demonstrate its effectiveness on graphs of real-world MEP systems. In addition, we design a decoder that explicitly considers the direction and distance between components in order to enhance spatial realism in the predicted connections. To train our models for the task of link prediction in MEP systems, we assembled a dataset of 27 BIM models from residential, industrial, and public buildings. Our results indicate that our approach can significantly enhance the prediction of connectivity relationships, offering valuable insights for improving BIM workflows and interoperability. The source code of our models and experiments is available at https://github.com/RWTH-E3D/SpatialLinkPrediction.
Author Keywords: BIM; Deep learning; Link prediction; MEP; Relationship modeling; Topology
Funding Details: Bundesministerium für Wirtschaft und Energie, BMWi, (03EN1026A); Bundesministerium für Wirtschaft und Energie, BMWi
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP systems broadly (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) and explicitly includes electrical systems as one of the system types in the dataset, though it notes electrical systems are often modeled without explicit cable connections.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies graph neural networks and deep learning auto-encoder models to predict topological relationships in BIM models, with BIM and AI as core contributions.
relevance_notes: Paper covers MEP-wide link prediction; electrical systems are included but the authors note limitations for electrical due to lack of geometric cable connections in BIM models.
research_problem: Many topological relationships between building components in BIM models are implicit and can be lost when exchanging models between different BIM authoring tools and the IFC standard. Existing query systems and rule-based methods can partially infer missing relationships but lack a holistic understanding of the building model, working only on individual vertices, edges, and faces. There is a need for a method that can predict connectivity relationships between MEP components without relying on handcrafted rules for every component combination.
research_objective: To develop an auto-encoder model with a candidate generation procedure that learns topological connectivity relationships between MEP components in BIM models.
specific_methods: Graph Neural Networks; Auto-encoder model; GraphSAGE; EdgeConv; MLP encoder/decoder; DistMult decoder; Bounding box collision detection (AABB and Separating Axis Theorem); 5-fold cross-validation
data_size: 27 BIM models (IFC format) with 442k nodes and 747k edges across residential, industrial, and public buildings
future_research_directions: Extend approach to incorporate other relationship types beyond connectivity; Design improved candidate generation using open ports of components for model healing; Incorporate main pipe axis to reduce cross-cutting predictions; Combine with BIM query languages for sophisticated candidate edge generation; Apply approach to other structural BIM components beyond MEP; Clean up component class labels by reclassifying generic IfcBuildingElementProxy components
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Quality improvement; Coordination or clash reduction
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Quality control
key_findings: The proposed spatial link decoder, which explicitly considers direction and distance between components, significantly outperformed baseline decoders (DistMult, inner product, MLP) in negative sampling experiments, achieving accuracy of approximately 98%, precision of 98.88%, recall of 97.08%, F1 of 97.96%, and AUROC of 99.78%. In the more realistic candidate generation setting using bounding box collisions, all decoders performed comparably with the spatial link decoder achieving roughly 82% accuracy and 89% AUROC. Bounding box scaling of approximately 2.5% captured most ground-truth edges while limiting candidate edges to about 1% of all possible edges. Neighborhood aggregation via GraphSAGE did not improve performance, likely due to noisy IFC class labels and sparse network connectivity.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 97.96% F1-score for link prediction with spatial link decoder under negative sampling; 2.5% bounding box scale factor captures ~100% ground-truth edges while testing only ~1% of all possible edges
research_sponsor: German Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy (BMWi) under grant agreement no. 03EN1026A, project EnergyTWIN
industry_involvement: None reported
codes_addressed__codes_details: Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) standard is central to the work, with specific reference to IfcBuildingElementProxy, IfcDistributionElement, IfcDistributionPort, HasPorts, and IsNestedBy relationships used to define MEP component connections.
limitations_acknowledged: The model has difficulty predicting connections that span very long distances and tends to produce false positives in areas with multiple parallel pipes that have only subtle directional differences. The candidate generation approach cannot capture connections in electrical systems where cable connections are not modeled geometrically, and cannot fix completely misplaced or missing components. The IFC class information was found to be inconsistent and noisy because many MEP components are mapped to the generic IfcBuildingElementProxy class.
data_availability__availability_details: Source code available at https://github.com/RWTH-E3D/SpatialLinkPrediction

Author full names: Awad Hanna, Fawaz Boodai, Mounir El Asmar
Title: State of practice of building information modeling in mechanical and electrical construction industries
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: The North American construction industry has seen a decline in productivity for decades due to various reasons, including a lack of collaboration and the increase in the complexity of systems. These problems are most visible in labor-intensive trades, such as mechanical and electrical construction. Within the last decade, building information modeling (BIM) has emerged as a potential solution to these problems. This paper attempts to highlight the state of practice of BIM in the mechanical and electrical industries. By analyzing responses from an extensive survey and interview process, this paper reaches three key conclusions, namely, (1) 59% of mechanical and electrical contractors that use BIM have 3 years or less of BIM experience; (2) contractors should use one to three BIM staff members and add 1-2% of total project-cost estimates to account for BIM implementation; and (3) more than 70% of mechanical and electrical contractors that have used BIM agree that BIM reduces field conflicts and improves coordination. These findings can help mechanical and electrical construction firms understand the evolving use of BIM and allocate resources appropriately.
Author Keywords: Building information models; Construction management; Construction methods; Electrical construction; Mechanical construction; Project planning and design
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper surveys both mechanical and electrical contractors equally (75 mechanical, 70 electrical) and reports findings specific to electrical contractors throughout.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: BIM is the central digital technology studied, with investigation of BIM tools, adoption, and impact on MEP construction.
relevance_notes: Strong practitioner-focused BIM adoption study with explicit electrical contractor breakdown, though no AI/ML component.
research_problem: The North American construction industry has experienced declining productivity due to lack of collaboration and increasing system complexity, problems most visible in labor-intensive MEP trades. While BIM has emerged as a potential solution, most research targets general contractors and there is a lack of emphasis on how specialty trades such as mechanical and electrical contractors implement BIM. The state of BIM practice in MEP industries, including resource allocation, costs, risks, and realized value, has not been adequately quantified.
research_objective: To investigate the current state of BIM practice in the mechanical and electrical construction industries in North America by gaining insight on current and future BIM implementation and identifying the effect of BIM use on project performance.
specific_methods: Survey questionnaire; Follow-up interviews; Five-point Likert scale; t-test for mean comparison; Descriptive statistics
data_size: 145 completed survey responses (75 mechanical, 70 electrical) out of 1,896 distributed (8% response rate)
future_research_directions: Develop cost-benefit analysis for implementing BIM on mechanical and electrical construction; Create BIM standards for mechanical and electrical construction industries; Increase sample size to reach more generalizable conclusions; Map changes in BIM adoption trends over time; Investigate variations in BIM value based on project complexity and delivery method
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Quality improvement; Cost reduction
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: 59% of mechanical and electrical contractors using BIM have three years or less of experience, and 60% of respondents currently use BIM with electrical contractors (70%) adopting more than mechanical (51%). Contractors should typically use one to three BIM staff members and allocate 1–2% of total project cost for BIM implementation. More than 70% of contractors that used BIM agreed it reduces field conflicts and improves coordination, with clash detection (mean 4.22) and visualization (3.70) as top benefits. Autodesk tools dominate approximately three-fourths of the MEP software market, and the primary risk identified is the lack of BIM protocols during construction.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Estimator; Business owner or executive
quantified_benefit: 61% of respondents implemented BIM at 2% or less of total project cost; better coordination mean score 4.10/5 and reduction in field conflicts 4.02/5
research_sponsor: Not reported
industry_involvement: A professional panel including contractors and survey specialists from the University of Wisconsin Survey Center assisted in survey development; follow-up interviews were conducted with mechanical and electrical contractors and Autodesk software specialists.
industry_references__references_details: Cites American Institute of Architects (AIA) BIM contract documents, Construction Industry Institute, Construction Management Association of America, National Institute of Standards and Technology, and McGraw-Hill SmartMarket Report.
limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge that some findings, such as the value generated by BIM use, may vary based on project factors including complexity, owner initiatives to standardize BIM use, project size, and use of BIM protocols. They note that more research is needed to understand these variations and that the sample should be increased to reach more generalizable conclusions.

Author full names: Awad S. Hanna, Michael Yeutter, Diane G. Aoun
Title: State of practice of building information modeling in the electrical construction industry
Source title: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Abstract: For decades, the North American construction industry has seen a decline in productivity due to many underlying reasons. Moreover, the construction industry in general and electrical construction companies in particular struggled with coordination and productivity. Within the last decade, building information modeling (BIM) has become the means for a real solution to these problems. This paper attempts to highlight the practices and state of BIM in the electrical construction industry. Through an extensive survey and interview process, this research has provided three main conclusions. First, electrical contractors have not yet advanced their implementation of BIM, with 59% of respondents having three years or less experience with BIM. Second, in order to maintain a positive outcome for the use of BIM on a project, contractors should maintain one to three staff members as BIM specialists, adding a cost of 2% or less from the total project cost to implement BIM. Third, BIM does indeed reduce field conflicts and improve coordination. Other than quantifying the current state of practice and impact of BIM on electrical contractors and approximating its future use in this industry, this study contributes to the body of knowledge about BIM by offering the electrical contractors recommendations that will assist them in allocating the proper resources and to understand the evolving use of BIM.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling; Communication; Computer aided design; Construction management; Contractors; Electrical equipment; Information technologies; Planning; Surveys
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses specifically on BIM adoption and practices within the electrical contracting industry, surveying electrical contractors directly.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: BIM is the central digital technology examined throughout the paper, including software tools, total stations, and GPS positioning.
research_problem: Electrical contractors struggle with declining productivity, coordination issues, and adoption of BIM, yet there is limited quantitative research specifically on how electrical contractors use BIM. Prior studies (Azhar/Cochran 2009, Simonian 2009) did not adequately cover performance indicators, associated risks, or planned future use of BIM in electrical construction. The industry lacks standards for information modeling and quantitative evidence of BIM's effects on projects.
research_objective: To describe the current state of practice of BIM in electrical construction, quantify its impact, approximate future use, and offer recommendations to improve BIM understanding and use.
specific_methods: Online survey; Semi-structured interviews; Correlation analysis; 5-point Likert scale analysis; Descriptive statistics
data_size: 70 survey responses (67 complete, 3 incomplete) from ~800 distributed; 8 interviews (1 architect, 1 engineer, 2 GCs, 4 electrical contractors)
future_research_directions: Determine cost of creating BIM models for bidding purposes; Expand scope to other trades (mechanical, plumbing, fire protection, low-voltage); Explore individual BIM topics in greater depth; Investigate how owners can use the final BIM model post-construction; Understand owner information requirements for handover models
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Cost reduction; Quality improvement
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; VDC or BIM cost recovery; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: 70% of medium-to-large electrical contractors surveyed are using BIM, a 2.5-fold increase from 2009 (when only 20% used BIM). Most contractors use 1-3 BIM staff members per project and spend less than 2% of total electrical project cost on BIM implementation. BIM generates the highest value in clash detection (mean 4.31/5), better coordination (4.14/5), and reduction in field conflicts (4.08/5). The largest associated risk is the lack of BIM protocol during the construction cycle (71% of respondents).
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Project manager; Estimator; Business owner or executive
quantified_benefit: BIM cost is less than 2% of total electrical project cost for 68% of users; mean improvement scores of 4.31/5 for clash detection and 4.14/5 for coordination

Interviews conducted with industry leaders from one architecture firm, one engineering firm, two general contractors, and four electrical contractors to validate and interpret survey results.
industry_references__references_details: NECA, ELECTRI International, and Federated Electrical Contractors (FEC) used as sources for survey distribution. ELECTRI International cited as supporting prior research (Azhar/Cochran 2009; Simonian 2009). American Institute of Architects mentioned regarding CAD/BIM standards.
limitations_acknowledged: The sample size was limited to 70 respondents, and the study was unable to fully represent the use of BIM throughout the entire electrical contracting industry. The data primarily represent medium-to-large electrical contractors, with some geographic regions (Southwest, Southeast, Northeast) underrepresented. The scope was also broad, leaving individual topics requiring deeper exploration.

Author full names: Filip Gorski, Damian Grajewski, Pawel Bun, Przemyslaw Zawadzki
Title: Study of interaction methods in virtual electrician training
Abstract: Virtual, Augmented and Mixed Reality technologies can be applied to improve competences of serviceman, or generally employees dealing with technical systems. The paper describes results of a first stage of development of a VR training system dedicated to electricians, working for one of Poland's major electricity providers. Three methods of interaction with the virtual environment and three navigation methods have been implemented in the presented application. To choose the best method of interaction and navigation in the training application, 30 electricians tested different combinations of methods in two training scenarios. Each participant went through the scenario twice, using two different navigation modes for the same interaction mode and scenario. The time of each pass was measured and the participants filled in a questionnaire to give feedback on their subjective impressions of using a given mode. Based on the gathered data analysis, the best interaction and navigation methods have been selected. These initial results will improve interaction and navigation at the next stage of the application development and implementation in the enterprise.
Author Keywords: interaction; training application; Virtual reality
Funding Details: European Commission, EC; Ministerstwo Edukacji i Nauki, MNiSW, (0613/SBAD/4679); Narodowe Centrum Badań i Rozwoju, NCBR, (POIR.01.01.01-00-0463/18); European Regional Development Fund, ERDF, (20202024); National Center for Research and Development, NCRD
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses specifically on developing a VR training system for electricians employed by a major electricity provider, addressing electrical substation switching operations and live-line work procedures.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates a Virtual Reality training application using HMDs, controllers, and the Unity 3D engine, evaluating different interaction and navigation methods.
relevance_notes: Note that the electrical context is operations/maintenance training for electricity utility workers rather than new construction installation, but the work is clearly relevant to electrician workforce training and skill development.

The authors needed to identify which combinations of interaction modes (basic, basic+, medium) and navigation modes (free movement, teleportation, 7-mile steps) were most effective for electricians with limited prior VR experience to inform development of a flexible enterprise training system.

specific_methods: Controlled user experiment; Pre-test and post-test questionnaires; Simulator sickness questionnaire; Paired Comparison Analysis (AHP); Task completion time measurement; 5-point Likert scale survey; Two-round repeated-measures design

future_research_directions: Conduct research on a larger group of users (more than 100) across various professions; Generalize results beyond electrician training; Investigate how interaction and navigation methods affect training effectiveness; Refine the pointer concept in basic+ interaction mode; Adapt teleportation marker size and sensitivity to working field; Implement and deploy full prototype with multiple substation and station scenarios

pain_point_addressed: Skilled labor shortage or training; Safety or electrocution or arc flash

key_findings: The free movement navigation mode was not recommended for electrical substation scenarios due to causing simulator sickness and longer task completion times (581s vs 385s for teleportation in basic mode). Teleportation and 7-mile steps modes performed similarly and were preferred by participants. The complex medium interaction mode should be discarded in favor of basic and basic+ modes (each preferred by 39.3% of respondents vs 21.4% for medium). Nearly 90% of electricians positively assessed VR's suitability for their training, with 54.5% stating it could largely replace traditional training.

Author full names: Guohua Wei, Ding Zhou, Xiaojun Yuan
Title: Target detection technology of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing components based on CV
Abstract: Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems are vital in construction engineering as their installation quality significantly impacts project success. Traditional inspection methods often fail to ensure compliance with building information models (BIMs), leading to safety hazards due to deviations during construction. Spurred by these concerns, this paper introduces a novel BIM-based pipeline construction comparison system that relies on computer vision technology. The developed system uses deep learning algorithms for real-time data collection to enhance monitoring efficiency and accuracy, as well as advanced object detection algorithms to compare real-time construction images with BIMs. The proposed architecture addresses the limitations of existing techniques in handling MEP complexities, and through an automatic comparison and verification process, it detects deviations promptly, ensuring adherence to design specifications. This study innovatively integrates real-time data collection, deep learning algorithms, and an automated BIM comparison mechanism to enhance the accuracy, efficiency, and automation of pipeline installation monitoring, addressing the limitations of existing inspection methods.
Author Keywords: deep learning; edge detection; matching; pipeline construction
Funding Details: 2022 Stable Support Plan Program for Shenzhen-based Universities, (20220815150554000)

Existing object detection approaches (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) struggle with the complexity of MEP components and lack real-time monitoring capabilities and automated comparison mechanisms with BIM. Deviations during construction caused by environmental changes, human error, or design modifications are not promptly detected, leading to operational difficulties and safety incidents.

key_findings: The proposed system achieved 95.4% accuracy in detecting construction deviations using a Hu moments difference threshold of 0.01. The DiffusionEdge-based contour extraction outperformed Canny, SE, OEF, and PiDiNet methods, achieving an F-score of 0.795 (precision 0.92, recall 0.616). BIM projections maintained consistency with on-site photographs in angle and scale, validating the integrated SAM segmentation and contour comparison pipeline for automated construction monitoring.

Author full names: Xinxin Tang, Jili Zhang, Ruobing Liang
Title: The design of heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems based on building information modeling: A review from the perspective of automatic and intelligent methods
Abstract: Heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems constitute a significant portion of building energy usage and carbon emissions. The design quality directly affects the energy-saving and carbon-reduction effects of HVAC systems. A high-quality HVAC system design necessitates the use of automatic and intelligent methods, as traditional HVAC system design is experience-based, repetitive, time-consuming, and difficult to achieve optimality. Building information modeling (BIM) offers a standardized data basis for the implementation of automatic and intelligent methods in HVAC system design practice. This study aims to assess the current status, advantages, and disadvantages of automatic data interaction between BIM and HVAC system design simulation software, intelligent optimization methods for HVAC system design, and automatic clash filtering and resolution methods for mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) systems. To this end, we conducted a comprehensive search of relevant scientific databases and reviewed 105 articles. The primary results indicate that automatic data interaction methods predominantly consist of IFC-based and gbXML-based approaches. HVAC system design optimization problems commonly employ metaheuristic algorithms, analytic hierarchy process (AHP), and Monte Carlo simulation. Additionally, machine learning and hybrid approaches that integrate graph-theory-based methods and optimization methods are extensively utilized for clash filtering and resolution in MEP systems. The findings of this review offer engineers automatic and intelligent approaches for addressing design challenges in practice. They also offer insights into the development of automatic and intelligent BIM-based HVAC system design, which can be beneficial for researchers and practitioners.
Author Keywords: Building information modeling; Clash filtering; Clash resolution; Data interaction; Design optimization; HVAC system design
Funding Details: Dalian Key Field Innovation Team Project, (2020RT04); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC, (51978120); National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC

Prior reviews have not comprehensively addressed automatic data interaction for HVAC-specific information, the classification and solution processes for HVAC design optimization problems, or BIM-based clash filtering and resolution in MEP.

Author full names: Filip Gorski, Jakub Gapsa, Damian Grajewski, Przemyslaw Zawadzki, Mikolaj Maik, Pawel Sobocinski, Przemyslaw Starzynski, Rafal Wojciechowski, Krzysztof Walczak
Title: The impact of virtual reality on technical training: A case study in live-line maintenance
Abstract: This paper presents an evaluation of the effectiveness of virtual reality (VR) as a training tool for electricians working in high-risk environments within the energy infrastructure sector. A study was conducted involving 124 participants, divided into four groups, to compare the outcomes of VR-based training, traditional training, and a combination of both methods. The Virtual Electrician Training system, a comprehensive VR platform featuring realistic 3D models and interactive scenarios, was used to simulate real-world tasks. Results indicate that participants who received VR training followed by traditional training demonstrated the highest knowledge retention and skill acquisition, suggesting that VR is an effective introductory training tool. The study also identified challenges such as user discomfort and varying ease of use, highlighting the need for further refinement of VR systems. The findings support the integration of VR into technical training programs, with potential benefits including improved safety, enhanced learning outcomes, and cost savings. This research contributes to the growing body of knowledge on the application of VR in industrial training and offers insights into optimizing training sequences for maximum effectiveness.
Author Keywords: Electrician training; live-line working; user experience; virtual reality
Funding Details: European Regional Development Fund, ЕФРР; European Commission, EC; Ministerstwo Edukacji i Nauki, MNiSW; Narodowe Centrum Badań i Rozwoju, NCBiR, (POIR.01.01.01-00-0463/18, LIDER/55/0287/L-12/20/NCBR/2021)

Author full names: Jingxiao Zhang, Zhiyue Zhang, Simon P. Philbin, Henk Huijser, Qian Wang, Ruoyu Jin
Title: Toward next-generation engineering education: A case study of an engineering capstone project based on BIM technology in MEP systems
Abstract: To respond to the digital building environment and Industry 4.0 in building information modeling (BIM)-based mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) systems this study developed a new approach to engineering capstone projects. This article reports on a case study of the development of the innovative capstone project for engineering majors, which is based on team-based learning (TBL) combined with the 360-degree evaluation feedback method to cultivate students' BIM competency. The data collection and analysis involves a combination of qualitative and quantitative methods, which aims to evaluate students' learning outcomes and their BIM competency. The results indicate that TBL, combined with the 360-degree evaluation feedback in the capstone project, can be highly effective in improving graduates' BIM competency. This study discusses the interoperability of BIM software tools, problems in the process of data exchange, and provides suggestions for improving the course and BIM team collaboration. The research has identified how professional capabilities for students can be enhanced, enabled through a capstone project, and educators are able to deploy the BIM course to develop engineers that closely meet industry needs. The study provides the case for using the capstone project to improve and cultivate engineering students' BIM competency in MEP systems. This study has provided a new paradigm for applying TBL and 360-degree evaluation feedback to engineering education.

Author full names: Frédéric Bosché, Adrien Guillemet, Yelda Turkan, Carl T. Haas, Ralph Haas
Title: Tracking the built status of MEP works: Assessing the value of a Scan-vs-BIM system
Source title: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Abstract: Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) works constitute a large portion of construction costs and thus need to be appropriately tracked. Assessment of the built status of MEP works in construction projects is however typically limited to subcontractor claims augmented and contrasted with periodic manual inspection. A more detailed manual inspection is costly and not considered worthwhile on most projects. Within a Scan-vs-BIM object recognition framework, three-dimensional laser scanning and project 3D/4D BIM models jointly offer the opportunity for a frequent, detailed, and semantically rich assessment of as-built status of construction projects at a cost that continues to decline. This potential has already been demonstrated for tracking structural works, but remains to be assessed in regard to other work sections, in particular MEP works. This paper explores that opportunity. A Scan-vs-BIM processing system is described with some enhancements over previous works. It is then tested with a representative and challenging case study of the construction of a utility corridor in a university engineering building. The results indicate that the proposed system is significantly challenged when tracking MEP systems constructed using traditional on-site fabrication, due to changes or adjustments made on-site that lead to actual component layouts varying in comparison to designed layouts. This performance could be revisited in cases where off-site prefabrication and preassembly is implemented. The results nonetheless lead the authors to propose a novel data processing system (conceptually described in this paper) integrating Scan-vs-BIM and Scan-to-BIM approaches. This system should provide superior performance over existing systems, enabling automated and robust quality control (including the estimation of the emerging performance metric percent built as designed) and delivery of true as-built BIM models to facility owners and managers.
Author Keywords: As-built status; BIM; Laser scanning; Mechanical electrical and plumbing (MEP) works; Percent built as designed; Scan-to-BIM; Scan-vs-BIM; Three dimensional (3D)

Manual MEP inspection is costly and limited in scope. Existing Scan-vs.-BIM systems that have been demonstrated for structural elements have not been assessed for MEP works, which present unique challenges from occlusions, packed configurations, and greater installation variability than structural components.

key_findings: The Scan-vs.-BIM system achieved 96% precision but only 81% recall on MEP component recognition, far below the near 100% precision and recall previously reported for structural works. The high false negative rate is attributed to MEP elements being installed in positions that deviate significantly from designed positions because of traditional cut-and-fit on-site installation. The proposed %confidence metric correlates well with as-built versus as-designed positional deviations and could support automated estimation of the percent built as designed metric.

Author full names: Jad Chalhoub, Steven K. Ayer
Title: Using mixed reality for electrical construction design communication
Abstract: Building Information Modeling (BIM) techniques have enabled the construction industry to realize various benefits. However, most projects still rely on 2D drawings to communicate the 3D BIM content to construction personnel. While Mixed Reality (MR) could theoretically be the primary means of communicating BIM content to onsite personnel in 3D, there is not currently a thorough understanding of how this might impact the construction performance of industry practitioners. This paper explores this topic by examining the field of electrical construction. It addresses research questions related to: MR's influence on the productivity and quality of electrical conduit construction; and the effects of an industry practitioner's background on his or her performance using MR. To address these topics, a quasi-experiment was conducted that compares the performance of eighteen electrical construction personnel who were tasked with building similar conduit assemblies using traditional paper and MR. Participants completed pre- and post-activity questionnaires to provide their perceptions of the experience. The results suggest that MR enabled: a significantly higher productivity rate; reduced the time required to understand the design; led to fewer errors during the assembly process; and increased the number of accurately constructed conduits as compared to the conduits constructed using traditional paper. Additionally, nearly all participants agreed that MR is easy to use, but most still felt that they would prefer to use paper plans for design communication. The findings of this work were noteworthy because many of the participants had substantial prior experience constructing conduit using paper plans, yet they still performed the task better and faster using MR. While the small sample size limits the extent to which these findings can be generalized, the contribution of this work is in demonstrating, as a proof-of-concept, that MR can be a viable option for communicating existing BIM content to current industry practitioners and that it can offer advantages that are not currently observed through the use of a paper-based communication methods.
Author Keywords: BIM; Mixed Reality; Prefabrication; Productivity
Funding Details: National Science Foundation, NSF, (IIS-1566274); National Science Foundation, NSF

relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally evaluates Mixed Reality (HoloLens) combined with BIM as a design communication technology.
research_problem: While BIM adoption has been increasing in construction, most projects still rely on 2D paper drawings to communicate 3D BIM content to field personnel, which can introduce communication noise and errors. Mixed Reality could theoretically deliver BIM content directly to onsite workers in 3D, but there is no thorough understanding of how MR would impact the construction performance, productivity, and quality of industry practitioners, particularly for electrical conduit assembly. The paper addresses this empirical gap by comparing MR-based and paper-based design communication for prefabricated electrical conduit construction.
research_objective: To examine how Mixed Reality influences the productivity and quality of electrical conduit construction compared to traditional paper plans, and how practitioner background affects MR performance.
specific_methods: Quasi-experiment with double-counterbalanced design; Pre- and post-activity questionnaires; Video coding and behavioral analysis; Paired samples t-test; Independent samples t-test; Convenience sampling
data_size: 18 electrical construction practitioners, each completing 2 conduit assembly tasks (36 trials total)
future_research_directions: Identify construction task attributes that maximize MR benefits; Explore MR for enhancing training techniques; Test MR on active job sites with real conditions; Conduct studies with larger and more diverse participant samples; Develop automated workflows for importing BIM content into MR
value_proposition: Labor productivity; Quality improvement; Training effectiveness
pain_point_addressed: Labor productivity; Prefabrication feasibility; Quality control
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Prefabricated electrical assemblies
key_findings: MR enabled significantly faster conduit assembly (average 277 seconds vs. 504 seconds with paper plans, p=0.000081), reduced time spent looking at design information (64s vs. 191s), and reduced placement time (5s vs. 85s). MR reduced total assembly mistakes by 75% (4 vs. 16) and reduced rework by 72%, with 16 correct final assemblies using MR vs. 11 with paper. Notably, the least-experienced participants using MR were faster than the most-experienced participants using paper. While most participants found MR easier to use, two-thirds still preferred to retain paper plans as part of the design communication package.
contractor_role_impacted: Electrician or field worker; Field supervisor or foreman; BIM coordinator or VDC manager
quantified_benefit: MR reduced average conduit assembly time from 504s to 277s (45% reduction), reduced mistakes by 75%, and reduced rework by 72%
research_sponsor: National Science Foundation Grant No. IIS-1566274; Corbins Electric provided models, materials, and participants
industry_involvement: Corbins Electric, an electrical contractor in the Southwest US, partnered as the industry collaborator by providing the BIM models, prefabricated conduit pieces, paper plans generated through their normal workflow, and 18 employees as study participants.
limitations_acknowledged: The participants were all from a single technologically progressive company and the sample size was small, limiting generalizability. The experiment was conducted in a controlled environment rather than an active jobsite, omitting effects of site conditions, noise, and safety constraints. Technical limitations included the MR HMD not being compatible with standard PPE, time required to prepare MR models was not accounted for, and five participants accidentally closed the model via inadvertent hand gestures.

Author full names: Patricia Andre Tillmann
Title: Using the last planner system to tackle the social aspects of BIM-enabled MEP coordination
Abstract: The aim of this research was to expand the current understanding of social aspects related to the successful adoption of Building Information Modeling (BIM) to support mechanical–electrical–plumbing (MEP) coordination and identify contributions of the Last Planner System (LPS) to tackle these aspects. The paper presents an action-research project carried out by a mechanical contractor. This study revealed that successful BIM-enabled MEP coordination depends not only on efforts to coordinate the model itself, but also on how effectively it is used during the installation phase. Social elements that influenced the effectiveness of BIM-enabled MEP coordination were classified in three categories: clarity of roles and responsibilities, individual vs. collective project leadership, and the existence of agreed-upon processes to follow. In such context, the LPS contributed to strengthening teamwork, providing a structure for increased communication and accountability, and helping the project team to focus on problem-solving. Consequently, the model was used more effectively, reducing rework and improving field productivity.
Author Keywords: Building Information Modeling (BIM); Last Planner System; Mechanical-electrical-plumbing (MEP) coordination
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP coordination with electrical as one of the primary systems causing conflicts (32.66% of constraints were electrical), but the action-research was conducted by a mechanical contractor.

relevance_notes: Electrical work is discussed as a key source of coordination conflicts with mechanical systems, making this relevant to electrical contractors despite the mechanical contractor viewpoint.
research_problem: Despite widespread adoption of BIM for MEP coordination and documented technical benefits, projects continue to experience excessive rework and field productivity losses during MEP installation. The literature has identified social and organizational challenges as major roadblocks but provides limited guidance on managerial techniques to overcome them beyond advocating integrated project delivery. Empirical studies documenting how the Last Planner System can reveal and tackle the social aspects of successful BIM-enabled MEP coordination are scarce.

data_type: Project records; BIM model; Interview transcripts; Other
data_size: One pediatric/obstetric hospital project (521,000 sq ft); 12-month intervention; constraint log growing from 70 to ~1,800 items; 3,000+ owner-issued design changes analyzed
future_research_directions: Identify benefits of adopting LPS to tackle social aspects in earlier stages of BIM-enabled MEP coordination; Investigate LPS contributions to the modeling process itself; Study LPS-BIM integration from project inception rather than installation phase
digital_technology: Augmented reality; Mobile application; Virtual reality
value_proposition: Coordination or clash reduction; Labor productivity; Cost reduction
pain_point_addressed: Coordination or clash detection; Labor productivity; Organizational adoption of technology
key_findings: The study revealed that successful BIM-enabled MEP coordination depends not only on model coordination itself but on how effectively the model is used during installation. Social elements influencing effectiveness fell into three categories: clarity of roles and responsibilities, individual vs. collective project leadership, and existence of agreed-upon processes. LPS components strengthened teamwork, improved communication and accountability, and focused the team on problem-solving, leading to reduced rework and improved field productivity. The constraint log showed 69% of issues were trade conflicts (81% related to electrical and architectural systems), with lead time to resolve issues decreasing significantly over the intervention.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Field supervisor or foreman; Project manager
quantified_benefit: 77% of weekly plan failures attributable to coordination-related causes; 98% of identified constraints potentially avoidable with better model constructability and coordination; lead time to resolve field issues reduced over 12-month intervention

This limits understanding of how LPS might address social aspects in earlier phases including the modeling process itself.

Author full names: Jalberth F. Araujo, Rodrigo T. Guimaraes, Celio A. Silva, Henrique D. Silva
Title: Virtual environment for assisting learning of residential electrical installations
Source title: Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
Abstract: In this paper, the importance of electrical installations in technical and undergraduate courses in the field of electricity and the limitations faced in practical activities in conventional laboratories, such as high demand and time restrictions, is addressed. To solve this problem, virtual laboratories play a crucial role as tools in the teaching-learning process, offering simulated experiments. In this work, a virtual environment was developed to assist in the learning of electrical installations. The environment allows students to enhance their knowledge and practical skills in the area. Additionally, both teachers and students benefit from improving teaching and learning practices provided by the virtual environment. One of the main advantages of this environment is the flexibility it offers to students, allowing them to perform practical tasks outside the academic environment. This flexibility expands learning opportunities since students are not restricted to the physical laboratory and can practice and develop their skills in electrical installations anywhere and at any time.

Author full names: Boyu Wang, Qian Wang, Jack C.P. Cheng, Changhao Song, Chao Yin
Title: Vision-assisted BIM reconstruction from 3D LiDAR point clouds for MEP scenes
Abstract: Mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) system provides various services and creates comfortable environments to residents in cities. To enhance the management efficiency of the highly complex MEP system, as-built building information modeling (BIM) is increasingly adopted in the world. Currently, as-built BIMs are mostly drawn manually by modelers in BIM modeling software referring to point clouds or on-site photos, which is time consuming and labor intensive. This study presents a novel fused BIM reconstruction approach for MEP scenes. The proposed approach makes the best of the rich semantic information provided by images and accurate geometry information provided by 3D LiDAR point clouds. Firstly, a state-of-the-art deep learning model focusing on semantic segmentation is fine-tuned for the MEP dataset, and then RGB images collected with depth camera are segmented with the well-trained model. Secondly, taking the segmented images and the corresponding depth images as input, a semantic-rich 3D map is generated. Thirdly, an instance-aware component extraction algorithm in LiDAR point clouds given approximate object distribution in 3D space is developed. In the component extraction algorithm, a label transfer technique is proposed to firstly determine the rough locations of targeting objects in LiDAR point clouds. Then, accurate component locations are determined for three types of components including irregular shaped components, regular shaped components, and secondary components attached to walls. Finally, the BIM model is reconstructed based on component extraction results. To validate the proposed technique, experiments were conducted in four MEP rooms in a water treatment plant in Hong Kong. It is demonstrated that the proposed technique is more accurate and more efficient with wider range of applications compared to previous BIM reconstruction methods.
Author Keywords: As-built modeling; Building information model (BIM); Deep learning; Fused method; LiDAR point clouds; MEP scene
Funding Details: Innovation and Technology Commission, ITC, (UIM/344); Innovation and Technology Commission, ITC
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP scenes broadly but explicitly includes several electrical components (electrical pipes/conduit, electrical boxes, sockets, lighting) as core targets of the reconstruction method.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally applies deep learning (DeepLabv3+), BIM reconstruction, LiDAR point cloud processing, and depth camera-based SLAM as its primary contribution.
relevance_notes: Electrical components are a substantial part of the nine component types modeled, including secondary components (electrical pipes, electrical boxes, sockets) that previous methods neglected.
research_problem: As-built BIM models for MEP systems are typically created manually in modeling software referring to point clouds or photos, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive. Existing automated methods rely mostly on LiDAR point clouds alone and struggle with complex MEP scenes, particularly for components with complex shapes and small secondary components like electrical boxes and sockets. Previous studies have not combined the rich semantic information of 2D images with the geometric accuracy of LiDAR point clouds, and secondary components attached to walls are often neglected.
research_objective: To develop a novel fused BIM reconstruction approach for MEP scenes that combines semantic information from depth camera images with geometric accuracy of 3D LiDAR point clouds.
specific_methods: DeepLabv3+ semantic segmentation; Visual-inertial SLAM (VINS); TSDF volumetric mapping; DBSCAN clustering; Niching migratory multi-swarm optimizer (NNMSO); RANSAC and MSAC plane fitting; Iterative Closest Point (ICP) registration; Label transfer technique
data_type: Point cloud; 2D images; BIM model; Sensor readings
data_size: 2000 labeled RGB images for training DeepLabv3+ (1500 train, 300 validation, 200 test); validation on four MEP rooms in a water treatment plant (sizes ranging from 6x6.5x3 m to 33x8x3.5 m)
future_research_directions: Improve trajectory planning of data collecting agents; Ensure better coverage of depth camera data during scanning; Automate the currently manual rough registration step
ai_technique: Computer vision; Deep learning; Evolutionary or genetic algorithm
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Quality improvement; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Commissioning or closeout; Coordination or clash detection; Quality control
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Lighting and controls; MEP coordination generally
key_findings: The proposed fused method successfully modeled 304 MEP components across four test rooms with recall of 91.0% and precision of 95.6%. For primary components (valves, pumps, pipes, ducts, I-beams), recall and precision were 97.5% and 98.7%, representing 7.8% and 13.7% improvements over the direct slicing method. For secondary components (electrical pipes, boxes, sockets), recall and precision reached 85.2% and 92.6%. Average deviation from LiDAR points to reconstructed BIM was under 0.03 m (about 0.15% of scene size), and total processing time was reduced by approximately 35%.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer; Owner or client
quantified_benefit: Primary components: 97.5% recall and 98.7% precision (7.8% and 13.7% improvement over previous method); 35% reduction in total processing time; average deviation under 0.03 m
research_sponsor: Innovation and Technology Commission of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (Project No. UIM/344) and ATAL Engineering Limited
industry_involvement: ATAL Engineering Limited co-sponsored the research, and data were collected in an operational water treatment plant in Hong Kong, with a treatment plant manager providing reference information on typical manual modeling times.
limitations_acknowledged: The developed fused BIM reconstruction technique relies on good coverage of collected depth camera data. If the camera moves too fast or the operator misses parts of the scene, the generated semantic-rich 3D map will be incomplete, which adversely affects post-processing of LiDAR point clouds. The scanning trajectory currently requires careful manual planning by the operator.

Author full names: Katrin Kraus, Martin Berger, Thomas Keller, Elke Brucker-Kley
Title: VR-based workplace training and spaces of learning: A social space study of VR training for apprentice electricians
Source title: International Journal for Research in Vocational Education and Training
Abstract: Context: This study uses a social space approach to investigate Virtual Reality (VR)-based workplace learning in the context of apprenticeship training in the electronics industry in Switzerland. It was one part of a project which developed a prototype VR environment that enables apprentices to practice testing an electrical installation in a virtual garage using VR headsets. The study uses a spatial theory perspective to understand how the apprentices use the VR environment to develop their vocational competence and how spaces of learning are created through this process. Approach: The study applies a socio-spatial perspective to a triangulation of the results of a qualitative content analysis of structured interviews with 16 apprentices and their 11 trainers and a quantitative analysis of a close-ended apprentice survey questionnaire (N = 16). Results: When a VR environment is used for workplace learning, spaces of learning are created from the interplay of four spatial dimensions: The regulation and practices of use of the VR environment, the locality of use, the educational potential of the VR environment, and a mental representation of the real workplace represented in the VR environment. The VR headset acts as a boundary object in the training relationship. Other important findings of the study are: Automated access to learning opportunities reduces pressure on the teaching-learning relationship; the haptic dimension of work is important for competence development; the VR environment enables personalized learning with unlimited opportunities for practice; the lack of variety in the operational scenario enables apprentices to focus on practicing and perfecting procedures but does not reflect the complexity of the real world. Conclusion: Our socio-spatial analysis shows that when VR environments are used in workplace training the interplay of physical, mental, and virtual spatial dimensions leads to the emergence of learning spaces. The access to the VR environment, the location where it is used, and the active use of its learning features interact with the mental representations of the depicted environment. Spaces of learning created using a VR environment can complement traditional workplace training by facilitating the development of specific aspects of vocational competence.
Author Keywords: Communities of Practice; Educational Technology; Learning Activities; Learning Process; Social Space; VET; Vocational Education and Training; Workplace Learning
Funding Details: Zurich Higher Education Institutions; DIZH

Existing research on VR in education has not addressed the social-spatial dynamics of competence development in apprenticeship training.

future_research_directions: Investigate the haptic dimension in vocational competence development; Clarify relationship between virtual and real workplace learning locations; Examine autonomy in VR versus social control in workplace learning; Study multi-user VR systems enabling social learning; Explore VR headsets as boundary objects and power dynamics in training; Include female apprentices and other occupations in follow-up studies

pain_point_addressed: Skilled labor shortage or training; Commissioning or closeout
electrical_work_category: Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance); Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices)
key_findings: Spaces of learning in VR-based workplace training emerge from the interplay of four spatial dimensions: regulation and practices of use, locality, educational potential, and experience-based mental representation of the real workplace. Author full names: Michael Holly, Carina Weichselbraun, Florian Wohlmuth, Florian Glawogger, Maria Seiser, Philipp Einwallner, Johanna Pirker
Title: VRChances: An immersive virtual reality experience to support teenagers in their career decisions
Abstract: In this paper, we present a tool that offers young people virtual career guidance through an immersive virtual reality (VR) experience. While virtual environments provide an effective way to explore different experiences, VR offers users immersive interactions with simulated 3D environments. This allows the realistic exploration of different job fields in a virtual environment without being physically present. The study investigates the extent to which performing occupational tasks in a virtual environment influences the career perceptions of young adults and whether it enhances their understanding of professions. In particular, the study focuses on users' expectations of an electrician's profession. In total, 23 teenagers and eight application experts were involved to assess the teenager's expectations and the potential of the career guidance tool.
Author Keywords: career guidance; job simulation; unemployment; virtual reality
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper uses the electrician profession as a scenario for VR-based career guidance for teenagers, but it does not address electrical construction practice, contractors, or jobsite work in any substantive way.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates an immersive virtual reality application as its primary contribution.
relevance_notes: Although the electrician scenario involves installing sockets, switches, and multiway switches, the study's purpose is youth career guidance and vocational orientation, not electrical construction productivity, training of working electricians, or contractor practice. Out of scope for an electrical construction review.
research_problem: Teenagers and young adults often struggle to choose a career path because they lack a detailed understanding of various professional fields and find it difficult to imagine the typical tasks of different jobs. There is a gap between young people's established career expectations and real job requirements, and traditional career guidance materials do not adequately bridge this gap. The authors propose VR as a way to let young people explore professions through immersive simulated tasks without physical presence or risk.
research_objective: To develop and evaluate an immersive VR career guidance tool (VRChances) that lets teenagers perform occupational tasks virtually and to assess its effect on their job understanding and career decisions.
specific_methods: Pre/post questionnaires; Expert review; User study; Wilcoxon signed-rank test; Sentiment analysis (polarity and subjectivity); Word cloud analysis; Likert-scale self-assessment
data_size: 23 teenagers (ages 15-21) and 8 experts (5 job coaches, 3 professional electricians)
future_research_directions: Conduct long-term studies on sustained impact and knowledge retention; Expand to a wider array of professions (healthcare, engineering, creative); Integrate personalized feedback and adaptive learning paths; Investigate gender-specific impacts of VR career guidance; Increase sample size and diversity in evaluation; Allow longer play time and richer task content
value_proposition: Training effectiveness; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Skilled labor shortage or training
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Lighting and controls
key_findings: After using VRChances, teenagers' descriptions of the electrician profession became sharper and more aligned with experts' descriptions, with increased mentions of personal qualities and cognitive abilities. Self-assessed knowledge increased from 1.91 to 2.48 on a 5-point scale, but the change was not statistically significant (Wilcoxon p = 0.097). Sentiment toward job outlook shifted from positive to more neutral and nuanced, and interest in becoming an electrician was largely unchanged (3 of 4 initially interested remained so). Experts (coaches and electricians) rated the tool positively as a supplement to regular career guidance.
contractor_role_impacted: Not specified
quantified_benefit: Self-assessed job knowledge increased from 1.91 to 2.48 on a 5-point Likert scale (not statistically significant, Wilcoxon p = 0.097)

industry_involvement: A local social service provider (Jugend am Werk Steiermark) collaborated on development and recruited teenagers and job coaches; three professional electricians from a local facility management department participated in expert evaluation.

limitations_acknowledged: The authors acknowledge a small sample size (8 experts, 23 young job seekers) and reliance on self-assessed knowledge. Only one job scenario (electrician) was evaluated, the 30-minute time limit constrained task completion, and the simulation only scratches the surface of the occupation. They also note that VR cannot fully substitute real-life experience factors such as colleague interaction, physical exertion, temperature, smell, or taste.
