Author full names: Jing Wen, Masoud Gheisari
Title: VR-Electricians: Immersive storytelling for attracting students to the electrical construction industry
Abstract: Although it is projected to have significant employment growth in the electrical construction industry, there are not enough young people to join the workforce. It is indispensable for the future of work in construction to explore emerging technologies and effectively attract the younger generation to the construction industry. This paper explores the innovative use of 360-degree immersive environments powered by virtual electricians to tell success stories of the electrical construction trades on the field (VR-Electricians) and understand how such an immersive storytelling approach can attract young people to join the industry. A pretest-posttest design was used for the VR-Electricians assessment. VR-Electricians was found to significantly enhance several aspects of students' attitude towards the electrical construction industry. VR-Electricians was also rated as a highly usable and easy-to-use system. The outcomes of this study provide construction researchers and practitioners an understanding of how immersive storytelling can be used as a powerful technology-mediated method for attracting students to their field.
Author Keywords: 360-degree VR; Immersive storytelling; Student recruitment; Virtual Reality (VR); Virtual storyteller; Workforce shortage
Funding Details: ELECTRI International, (2020-7); ELECTRI International
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper focuses specifically on attracting students to the electrical construction industry, with virtual electricians as storytellers and electrical jobsites as the context.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops and evaluates a 360-degree VR immersive storytelling platform with animated 3D virtual humans for workforce recruitment.
relevance_notes: Strong fit for both electrical construction practitioner audience (NECA/ELECTRI funded) and digital technology adoption in the industry.
research_problem: The electrical construction industry faces a severe workforce shortage, with electrical professionals among the top five most challenging positions to fill. Conventional recruitment methods such as job fairs and outreach programs are expensive, time-consuming, geographically limited, and were further disrupted by COVID-19. There is a need for technology-mediated approaches that can attract younger, tech-savvy generations to electrical trades by exposing them to authentic jobsite experiences without physical access constraints.
research_objective: To explore the use of 360-degree immersive storytelling powered by virtual electricians (VR-Electricians) to attract young students to the electrical construction industry.
specific_methods: Semi-structured interviews; Story mapping technique; 360-degree video capture; 3D character modeling and animation; Pretest-posttest experimental design; Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test; System Usability Scale (SUS); Cronbach's alpha reliability test

future_research_directions: Use immersive VR headsets like Google Cardboard to enhance presence; Integrate higher quality renderings of panoramic content; Expand participant pool to K-12 students and broader populations; Compare immersive storytelling with other recruitment strategies experimentally; Explore application to other AEC fields and minority populations; Investigate adding ambient jobsite noise to enhance sense-of-presence
value_proposition: Training effectiveness; Other
pain_point_addressed: Skilled labor shortage or training
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Lighting and controls; Power distribution (switchgear, panels, service entrance)
key_findings: VR-Electricians significantly improved student attitudes on four of six subscales: electrical work career aspirations (2.36 to 2.85), tediousness (decreased 2.06 to 1.88), gender inclusivity (1.63 to 1.50), and consequences of electrical work (4.24 to 4.49). The system achieved a SUS usability score of 75 (above average). Social presence ratings were strong for awareness, attention, and content comprehension, but neutral for affective comprehension due to the virtual electrician's limited facial expressions and somewhat robotic voice. Sense-of-presence was rated neutrally, attributed to desktop-VR limitations.
contractor_role_impacted: Electrician or field worker; Business owner or executive

Author full names: Hongzhe Yue, Shuju Jing, Guanying Huang, Peng Peng, Lingli Li, Qian Wang
Title: Weakly supervised framework for semantic segmentation of MEP point clouds
Abstract: Deep learning-based point cloud segmentation shows strong potential in mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) applications. However, most existing methods rely on fully supervised learning with dense point-level annotations, and labeling large-scale point clouds is time-consuming and labor-intensive. This paper introduces W-MEPNet, a weakly supervised framework that uses sparse annotations. W-MEPNet integrates weak supervision, transfer learning, and a teacher–student self-training strategy to propagate semantic information from a small set of labeled points to unlabeled regions while preserving class balance. Experiments on two real-world datasets, the Building MEP Dataset (BMD) and Underground Garage Dataset (UGD), show that W-MEPNet achieves 93.50% OA and 81.36% mIoU on BMD with only 0.01% labeled points, and 97.92% OA and 84.97% mIoU on UGD with only 0.02% labeled points. The method reduces annotation effort by over 75% while maintaining competitive accuracy, effectively bridging the gap between full and weak supervision for semantic segmentation of MEP scenes.
Author Keywords: Deep learning; MEP; Scan-to-BIM; Semantic segmentation; Underground garage
Funding Details: National Key Research and Development Program of China, NKRDPC, (2023YFC3804300); SEU Innovation Enhancement Plan for Doctoral Students, (CXJH_SEU 25101); Graduate Research and Innovation Projects of Jiangsu Province, (KYCX25_0497); China Scholarship Council, CSC, (202506090087)
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: The paper addresses MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) systems including electrical components such as cable trays, electrical conduits, wire ducts, and light fixtures, with electrical elements forming a substantial portion of the segmentation categories.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: The paper centrally develops a deep learning-based weakly supervised semantic segmentation framework (W-MEPNet) for point clouds, integrating laser scanning, BIM, and neural networks.
relevance_notes: Paper covers MEP broadly with electrical components included as part of multi-class segmentation; primarily a computer vision/deep learning contribution applicable to scan-to-BIM workflows for electrical contractors.
research_problem: Most existing deep learning methods for MEP point cloud segmentation rely on fully supervised learning with dense point-level annotations, which is extremely time-consuming and labor-intensive for large-scale point clouds. Weakly supervised point cloud segmentation in building MEP environments has received little attention, with most existing studies focused on outdoor infrastructure like tunnels and railways. Datasets, algorithms, and benchmarks tailored to the highly detailed and fine-grained nature of MEP systems remain scarce.
research_objective: To develop W-MEPNet, a weakly supervised semantic segmentation framework for residential MEP point clouds that uses sparse annotations to reduce labeling effort while maintaining accuracy.
specific_methods: Weakly supervised learning; Transfer learning; Teacher-student self-training with EMA; Adaptive Neighborhood Querying (ANQ); Class-balanced pseudo-labeling; Semantic consistency regularization; Ablation studies; OA/mIoU/F1 evaluation
data_size: Two datasets: Underground Garage Dataset (UGD) with ~185 million points across 5 areas; Building MEP Dataset (BMD) with ~92.1 million points across 56 subareas (915 m²) from 4 buildings
future_research_directions: Extend W-MEPNet to broader and more complex cross-building and cross-domain MEP scenarios; Explore information-guided or active sample selection for label efficiency; Develop stronger boundary-sensitive and multi-scale feature learning; Integrate synthetic and simulated point cloud data into weakly supervised pipeline; Combine weak supervision with self-supervised pretraining; Enable large-scale automatic MEP modeling and BIM reconstruction
value_proposition: Automation of manual task; Quality improvement; Decision support
pain_point_addressed: Commissioning or closeout; Coordination or clash detection; Quality control
electrical_work_category: Branch circuitry (conduit, wire, devices); Lighting and controls; Data and communications (structured cabling); MEP coordination generally
key_findings: W-MEPNet achieved 93.50% OA and 81.36% mIoU on the BMD dataset using only 0.01% labeled points, and 97.92% OA and 84.97% mIoU on the UGD dataset using only 0.02% labeled points. The weakly supervised approach approached fully supervised performance (85.88% mIoU on BMD and 87.15% mIoU on UGD) while reducing annotation time by over 75%. Ablation studies confirmed that pre-training, EMA-based self-training, and the ANQ module each contributed meaningfully to performance, particularly for thin elongated MEP components.
contractor_role_impacted: BIM coordinator or VDC manager; Designer or engineer
quantified_benefit: 93.50% OA and 81.36% mIoU on BMD with only 0.01% labeled points; reduced annotation time by over 75% (from 24h to 6h on BMD, 84h to 30h on UGD)
research_sponsor: National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2023YFC3804300), SEU Innovation Enhancement Plan for Doctoral Students (No. CXJH_SEU 25101), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX25_0497), China Scholarship Council Program (No. 202506090087)
industry_involvement: None reported. Data was collected on university campuses (Southeast University in China and Sungkyunkwan University in South Korea) without mention of industry partners or advisory panels.
limitations_acknowledged: Challenges remain in generalizing the framework to highly diverse real-world MEP environments with varying materials, lighting conditions, and sensor noise. Under extremely sparse supervision, reduced accuracy may occur on fine-grained, thin, or geometrically complex MEP components where boundary ambiguity and cross-component confusion are more likely. The model also relies on pretraining with indoor datasets, which may limit its adaptability to unseen or outdoor industrial settings.

Author full names: Gaang Lee, JuHyeon Bae, Jesse V. Jacobs, SangHyun Lee
Title: Wearable heart rate sensing and critical power-based whole-body fatigue monitoring in the field
Abstract: Whole-body fatigue (WBF) presents a concerning risk to construction workers, which can impact function and ultimately lead to accidents and diminished productivity. This study proposes a new WBF monitoring technique by applying the Critical Power (CP) model, a bioenergetic model, with a wrist-worn heart rate sensor. The authors modified the CP model to calculate WBF from the percentage of heart rate reserve (%HRR) and generated a personalized model via WBF perception surveys. Data were collected for two days from 33 workers at four construction sites. The results showed that the proposed technique can monitor field workers' perceived WBF with a mean absolute error of 12.8% and Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.83. This study, therefore, demonstrates the viability of wearable WBF monitoring on construction sites to support programs aimed at improving workplace safety and productivity.
Author Keywords: Critical power model; Field worker safety and productivity; Wearable heart rate sensor; Whole-body fatigue monitoring
Funding Details: Liberty Mutual Insurance
relevance_to_electrical_construction_jus: Paper studies construction workers broadly across multiple trades (carpenters, laborers, ironworkers, etc.) with only 2 of 33 subjects being electricians; electrical work is not the focus.
relevance_to_ai_or_digital_tech_justific: Paper centrally develops and validates a wearable biosensor (PPG-based wrist-worn heart rate sensor) technique combined with a bioenergetic computational model for continuous fatigue monitoring.
relevance_notes: Sample includes electricians but study is not electrical-specific; flag for human judgment on whether broader construction workforce wellbeing technology is in scope for an electrical construction review.
research_problem: Existing fatigue assessment in field work relies on sporadic, invasive subjective surveys that interrupt work and miss fatigue dynamics. Prior wearable biosensor approaches detect physical exertion rather than whole-body fatigue (WBF), and assume a consistent exertion-fatigue correlation that breaks down in naturalistic work environments with frequent rests and fluctuating physical demand. No field-feasible technique exists to continuously monitor WBF accounting for these dynamics.
research_objective: To develop and validate a wearable heart rate sensor-based whole-body fatigue monitoring technique using a modified Critical Power bioenergetic model fed with percentage of heart rate reserve.
specific_methods: Critical Power (CP) bioenergetic model; Photoplethysmography (PPG) signal processing; Butterworth and Wiener filtering; Spearman correlation analysis; Linear regression; Leave-the-last-out cross validation; Mixed-effect ordinal logistic regression; Log-likelihood ratio test; IQR*1.5 outlier detection
data_type: Wearable data; Sensor readings; Survey responses; Video
data_size: 33 workers across four construction sites; 488.4 hours of PPG signals; 204 WBF survey responses; 62 test cases via LLOCV
future_research_directions: Test technique in other fatigue-critical workplaces like mines, factories, and oil fields; Test across different weather conditions and seasons; Develop smartphone or smartwatch application for field-applicable personalization surveys; Oversample female construction workers to ensure generalizability; Assess fatigue's direct impact on injury risk to inform threshold limit values
value_proposition: Safety improvement;Workforce wellbeing;Labor productivity
pain_point_addressed: Safety or electrocution or arc flash; Labor productivity
key_findings: The proposed technique achieved a mean absolute error of 1.28 on the 0-10 Rating of Fatigue scale and a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.83 with perceived WBF, outperforming a mean-based dummy regressor (MAE 1.69, SCC 0.57) and other physical exertion-related biosignal metrics (skin temperature, HR, %HRR, SDNN, RMSSD, LF/HF) which all showed non-significant correlations. Performance was stronger within the personalization observation range (MAE 0.78, SCC 0.86) than beyond it (MAE 2.27, SCC 0.68). The log-likelihood ratio test confirmed statistically significant inter-individual variation, validating the need for parameter personalization.
contractor_role_impacted: Safety manager;Field supervisor or foreman;Electrician or field worker
quantified_benefit: MAE of 1.28 on 0-10 ROF scale and Spearman correlation of 0.83 with perceived whole-body fatigue
research_sponsor: Liberty Mutual Insurance, Risk Control Services
industry_involvement: Liberty Mutual Insurance Risk Control Services funded the work and one author is affiliated with Liberty Mutual; intellectual property is licensed to Humanca LLC co-owned by one of the authors.
limitations_acknowledged: Field test was conducted only at construction sites in mild weather, limiting generalizability to other industries and conditions. Adequate personalization surveys could not be completed for 13 of 46 subjects (28%) due to field access constraints. All participants were male, so findings may not generalize to female workers.
data_availability__availability_details: Not reported
